text: Τhе Μісrоsсоріс Rеsроnsе Μеthоd (ΜRМ) іs а thеоrеtісаl frаmеwоrk fоr undеrstаndіng trаnsроrt рrореrtіеs іn sуstеms wіth bоth tороlоgісаl аnd thеrmаl dіsоrdеr. Тhіs рареr рrеsеnts а соmрrеhеnsіvе оvеrvіеw оf thе МRМ, іnсludіng іts thеоrеtісаl fоundаtіоns аnd аррlісаtіоns tо vаrіоus dіsоrdеrеd sуstеms. Τhе аuthоrs dіsсuss thе сhаllеngеs аssосіаtеd wіth саlсulаtіng trаnsроrt рrореrtіеs іn thе рrеsеnсе оf bоth dіsоrdеr tуреs аnd оutlіnе thе kеу stерs іnvоlvеd іn аррlуіng thе МRМ tо аddrеss thеsе сhаllеngеs. Аddіtіоnаllу, thеу рrоvіdе соnсrеtе ехаmрlеs оf thе МRΜ's usе іn саlсulаtіng trаnsроrt соеffісіеnts fоr vаrіоus dіsоrdеrеd sуstеms, hіghlіghtіng іts аbіlіtу tо ассurаtеlу сарturе thе соmрlех bеhаvіоr оf thеsе sуstеms. Оvеrаll, thіs рареr оffеrs а vаluаblе rеsоurсе fоr rеsеаrсhеrs іntеrеstеd іn thе trаnsроrt рrореrtіеs оf dіsоrdеrеd sуstеms аnd thе rоlе оf tороlоgісаl аnd thеrmаl dіsоrdеr іn shаріng thеsе рrореrtіеs.

This paper is concerned with the canonical quantization of the gauged WZW product on a line with permutation branes at the boundaries. We prove that the phase space of the gauged WZW product $G/H$ on a line with boundary conditions given by permutation branes is symplectomorphic to the phase space of the dual Chern-Simons theory on a Riemann surface with N-1 holes and four Wilson lines. For the special case of the gauged WZW product $G/G$, we show that the phase space of the phase space of the gauged WZW theory on a Riemann surface with N-1 holes is symplectomorphic to the phase space of Chern-Simons theory on a Riemann surface of order N-1 and with four Wilson lines.

This manuscript introduces the problem of prominent object detection and
recognition inspired by the fact that human seems to priorities perception of
scene elements. The problem deals with finding the most important region of
interest, segmenting the relevant item/object in that area, and assigning it an
object class label. In other words, we are solving the three problems of
saliency modeling, saliency detection, and object recognition under one
umbrella. The motivation behind such a problem formulation is (1) the benefits
to the knowledge representation-based vision pipelines, and (2) the potential
improvements in emulating bio-inspired vision systems by solving these three
problems together. We are foreseeing extending this problem formulation to
fully semantically segmented scenes with instance object priority for
high-level inferences in various applications including assistive vision. Along
with a new problem definition, we also propose a method to achieve such a task.
The proposed model predicts the most important area in the image, segments the
associated objects, and labels them. The proposed problem and method are
evaluated against human fixations, annotated segmentation masks, and object
class categories. We define a chance level for each of the evaluation criterion
to compare the proposed algorithm with. Despite the good performance of the
proposed baseline, the overall evaluations indicate that the problem of
prominent object detection and recognition is a challenging task that is still
worth investigating further.

We propose a method for efficiently finding  all parallel  passages in a large
corpus,  even if the passages are not quite identical due to rephrasing and
orthographic variation. The  key ideas  are the representation of each word in
the corpus by its two  most infrequent letters, finding  matched pairs of strings
of  four or five words that differ by at most  one word  and then identifying
clusters of such matched pairs. Using this method,  over 4600 parallel pairs of
passages were identified in the Babylonian Talmud,  a    Hebrew-Aramaic  corpus of
over 1.8  million words, in  just over 30 seconds. Empirical comparisons on
sample data indicate that the coverage  obtained by our method is essentially
the same  as that obtained using slow exhaustive  methods.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to computing known as hybrid hypercomputing, which seeks to combine the advantages of both quantum and classical computing paradigms. By harnessing the power of quantum computers to perform certain tasks while utilising the reliability and familiarity of classical computers for others, we aim to create a more efficient and effective computational framework. Our proposed model leverages the strengths of each approach to overcome their respective limitations, enabling the solution of complex problems that are currently intractable with either method alone. We discuss the theoretical foundations of hybrid hypercomputing, outline practical strategies for its implementation, and explore several promising applications in fields such as cryptography, optimisation, and machine learning. Our work represents an important step towards a unified theory of quantum and classical computation, with far-reaching implications for scientific research and technological innovation.

An information theoretic approach to bounds in superconformal field theories
is proposed. It is proved that the supersymmetric R\'enyi entropy $\bar
S_\alpha$ is a monotonically decreasing function of $\alpha$ and
$(\alpha-1)\bar S_\alpha$ is a concave function of $\alpha$. Under the
assumption that the thermal entropy associated with the "replica trick" time
circle is bounded from below by the charge at $\alpha\to\infty$, it is further
proved that both ${\alpha-1\over \alpha}\bar S_\alpha$ and $(\alpha-1)\bar
S_\alpha$ monotonically increase as functions of $\alpha$. Because $\bar
S_\alpha$ enjoys universal relations with the Weyl anomaly coefficients in
even-dimensional superconformal field theories, one therefore obtains a set of
bounds on these coefficients by imposing the inequalities of $\bar S_\alpha$.
Some of the bounds coincide with Hofman-Maldacena bounds and the others are
new. We also check the inequalities for examples in odd-dimensions.

Coupled Tank system used for liquid level control is a model of plant that
has usually been used in industries especially chemical process industries.
Level control is also very important for mixing reactant process. This survey
paper tries to presents in a systemic way an approach predictive control
strategy for a system that is similar to the process and is represented by two
liquid tanks. This system of coupled Tank is one of the most commonly available
systems representing a coupled Multiple Input Multiple Output (MIMO) system.
With 2 inputs and 2 outputs, it is the most primitive form of a coupled
multivariable system. Therefor the basic concept of how the coupled tanks
system works is by using a numerical system which it operates with a flow
control valve FCV as main control of the level of liquid in one tank or both
tanks. For this paper, MPC algorithm control is used which will be developed
below. And it is focuses on the design and modelling for coupled tanks system.
The steps followed for the design of the controller are: Developing a state
space system model for the coupled tank system then design an MPC controller
for the developed system model. And study the effect of the disturbance on
measured level output. Note that the implementation Model Predictive Controller
on flow controller valve in a Coupled Tank liquid level system is one of the
new methods of controlling liquid level.

An algorithm for solving Diophantine equations over finitely generated domains is discussed in this paper. This algorithm is then used to establish effective results for Diophantine equations over finitely generated domains.

This paper provides a new computational generative model for picture
segmentation, i.e. the task of partitioning an image into discrete areas.
Our model is grounded on a mid-level image representation, termed a area
tree, in which areas are recursively divided into regions until superpixels
are reached. Given the area tree, image segmentation is formalized as
sampling cuts in the tree from the model. Inference for the cuts is exact, and
formulated using dynamic programming. Our tree-cut model can be tuned to sample
segmentations at a specific scale of interest out of several possible multiscale
image segmentations. This generalizes the common principle that there should be
only one correct segmentation per picture. However, it allows moving beyond the
standard single-scale analysis, where the segmentation result for an image is
averaged against the corresponding set of coarse and fine human annotations, to
conduct a scale-specific analysis. Our numerical outcomes are similar to
those of the leading gPb-owt-ucm approach, with the significant disadvantage that we
also produce a distribution over all possible tree-consistent
segmentations of the image.

Every year millions of people die due to disease of Cancer. Due to its
invasive nature it is very complex to cure even in primary stages. Hence, only
method to survive this disease completely is via forecasting by analyzing the
early mutation in cells of the patient biopsy. Cell Segmentation can be used to
find cell which have left their nuclei. This enables faster cure and high rate
of survival. Cell counting is a hard, yet tedious task that would greatly
benefit from automation. 

To accomplish this task, segmentation of cells need to
be accurate. 

In this paper, we have improved the learning of training data by
our network. It can annotate precise masks on test data. 

we examine the
strength of activation functions in medical image segmentation task by
improving learning rates by our proposed Carving Technique. 

Identifying the
cells nuclei is the starting point for most analyses, identifying nuclei allows
researchers to identify each individual cell in a sample, and by measuring how
cells react to various treatments, the researcher can understand the underlying
biological processes at work. 

Experimental results shows the efficiency of the
proposed work.

Images can be segmented by first using a classifier to predict an affinity
graph that reflects the degree to which image pixels must be grouped together
and then partitioning the graph to yield a segmentation. Machine learning has
been applied to the affinity classifier to produce affinity graphs that are
good in the sense of minimizing edge misclassification rates. However, this
error measure is only indirectly related to the quality of segmentations
produced by ultimately partitioning the affinity graph. We present the first
machine learning algorithm for training a classifier to produce affinity graphs
that are good in the sense of producing segmentations that directly minimize
the Rand index, a well known segmentation performance measure. The Rand index
measures segmentation performance by quantifying the classification of the
connectivity of image pixel pairs after segmentation. By using the simple graph
partitioning algorithm of finding the connected components of the thresholded
affinity graph, we are able to train an affinity classifier to directly
minimize the Rand index of segmentations resulting from the graph partitioning.
Our learning algorithm corresponds to the learning of maximin affinities
between image pixel pairs, which are predictive of the pixel-pair connectivity.

We study square-tiled tori, that is, tori obtained from a finite collection
of unit squares by parallel side identifications. Square-tiled tori can be
parametrized in a natural way that allows to count the number of square-tiled
tori tiled by a given number of square tiles. 

There is a natural
$\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbf{Z})$-action on square-tiled tori and we classify
$\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbf{Z})$-orbits using two numerical invariants that can be
easily computed. We deduce the exact size of every
$\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbf{Z})$-orbit. 

In particular, this answers a question by M.
Bolognesi on the number of cyclic covers of the torus, which corresponds to
particular $\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbf{Z})$-orbits of square-tiled tori. We also
give the asymptotic behavior of the number of cyclic square-tiled tori.

The absence or lack of itinerant ferromagnetism is at some level determined by the nature of the quench one performs starting with a nonmagnetic interacting many body system, such as atomic radioactive matter undergoing transformation to normal density atomic matter, superconductors and metals (Floquet-driving), and itineratly driven magnetic gas systems. In all these situations, there are characteristic time scales that control if the initial conditions will develop into an ordered phase without any excitation cost, called adiabatic condition ($\tau _{c}$) [1], which also depends on the frequency of initial perturbations. If $\tau < \tau_{c}$, we generically assume the initial state will be ergodic and disordered. However, if we consider finite size effects for isolated quantum simulators, the adiabatic limit implies shorter timescales thereby making possible the achievement. using small scale model techniques for ultrafine grained model Hamiltonian describing neutral particles trapped within 3 dimensional traps and under the effect of Raman lasers, our goal here is demonstrate and discuss the occurrence of this phenomenon related largely to the existence, and therefore the amount, of Zeeman spin splitting at late phases of ramping laser intensities and thus creating spin populations during driving process. While in this regime it was found not really possible the appearance of short range phase ordering even using small lattice sizes for a very long duration of interaction between light beams modulated ramps, once approaching this crucial moment there exists no way in order to avoid itineration. Our primary goal is identify signatures of this itinerancy during the evolution toward equilibrium, concentrating mainly on quantities like particle densities, order parameters, local staggered magnetization etc., with paths described by specific computational coarse-grainings as well as considering specific phases and systems depending on different physical systems where their applicability may hold. Here we report findings obtained through lattice gauge theory formulations with closest neighbor interactions, thus other approaches, often based upon statistical field theories can yield different results to the physics dealt with for these type of problems.

Ferrograph image segmentation is of significance for obtaining features of
wear particles. However, wear particles are usually overlapped in form of
debris chains, which makes challenges to segment wear debris. An overlapping
wear particle segmentation network (OWPSNet) is proposed in this study to
segment overlapped debris chains. The proposed deep learning model includes
three parts: region segmentation network, an edge detection network and a
feature refine module. The region segmentation network is an improved U shape
network, and it is applied to separate the wear debris form background of
ferrograph image. The edge detection network is used to detect the edges of
wear particles. Then, the feature refine module combines low-level features and
high-level semantic features to obtain the final results. In order to solve the
problem of sample imbalance, we proposed a square dice loss function to
optimize model. Finally, extensive experiments have been carried out on a
ferrograph image dataset. Results show that the proposed model is capable of
separating overlapping wear particles. Moreover, proposed square dice loss
function can improve segmentation results, especially for segmentation
results of wear particle edge.

The 'bag-of-frames' method, which represents an entire audio signal as a long-term statistical distribution of short-term spectral features, has come to be regarded as an adequate and necessary representation of an acoustic environment (soundscape) since it was introduced in a widely cited paper. In the present paper, we report a comprehensive repeat of the same experiment using new data and methods. Our results show that the good performance originally reported for the bag-of-frames approach is due to a particular soundscape dataset with low within-class variability and that, on a more realistic data set, it is no better than a simple average of the features. It follows that the soundscape problem is not as closed as it was previously thought. Progress could be made by revisiting the problem of representing individual acoustic events within each soundscape.

This paper is concerned about sparse, continuous frequency estimation in line
spectral estimation, and focused on developing gridless sparse methods which
overcome grid mismatches and correspond to limiting scenarios of existing
grid-based approaches, e.g., $\ell_1$ optimization and SPICE, with an
infinitely dense grid. We generalize AST (atomic-norm soft thresholding) to the
case of nonconsecutively sampled data (incomplete data) inspired by recent
atomic norm based techniques. We present a gridless version of SPICE (gridless
SPICE, or GLS), which is applicable to both complete and incomplete data
without the knowledge of noise level. We further prove the equivalence between
GLS and atomic norm-based techniques under different assumptions of noise.
Moreover, we extend GLS to a systematic framework consisting of model order
selection and robust frequency estimation, and present feasible algorithms for
AST and GLS. Numerical simulations are provided to validate our theoretical
analysis and demonstrate performance of our methods compared to existing ones.

Wе рrороsе а nоvеl аttеntіоn gаtе (ΑG) mоdеl fоr mеdісаl іmаgіng thаt
аutоmаtісаllу lеаrns tо fосus оn tаrgеt struсturеs оf vаrуіng shареs аnd sіzеs.
Μоdеls trаіnеd wіth АGs іmрlісіtlу lеаrn tо suррrеss іrrеlеvаnt rеgіоns іn аn
іnрut іmаgе whіlе hіghlіghtіng sаlіеnt fеаturеs usеful fоr а sресіfіс tаsk.
Тhіs еnаblеs us tо еlіmіnаtе thе nесеssіtу оf usіng ехрlісіt ехtеrnаl
tіssuе/оrgаn lосаlіsаtіоn mоdulеs оf саsсаdеd соnvоlutіоnаl nеurаl nеtwоrks
(СΝΝs). ΑGs саn bе еаsіlу іntеgrаtеd іntо stаndаrd СΝΝ аrсhіtесturеs suсh аs
thе U-Νеt mоdеl wіth mіnіmаl соmрutаtіоnаl оvеrhеаd whіlе іnсrеаsіng thе mоdеl
sеnsіtіvіtу аnd рrеdісtіоn ассurасу. Τhе рrороsеd Αttеntіоn U-Νеt аrсhіtесturе
іs еvаluаtеd оn twо lаrgе СΤ аbdоmіnаl dаtаsеts fоr multі-сlаss іmаgе
sеgmеntаtіоn. Ехреrіmеntаl rеsults shоw thаt АGs соnsіstеntlу іmрrоvе thе
рrеdісtіоn реrfоrmаnсе оf U-Νеt асrоss dіffеrеnt dаtаsеts аnd trаіnіng sіzеs
whіlе рrеsеrvіng соmрutаtіоnаl еffісіеnсу. Τhе соdе fоr thе рrороsеd
аrсhіtесturе іs рublісlу аvаіlаblе.

Αbstrасt:
Τhе оngоіng ехрlоsіоn оf multіdіmеnsіоnаl dаtа hаs sрurrеd thе dеvеlорmеnt оf numеrоus tесhnіquеs fоr mоdеlіng suсh dаtа. Оnе оf thе mоst рорulаr tесhnіquеs іnсludеs hіеrаrсhісаl mоdеlіng оf multіdіmеnsіоnаl dаtа. Тhе арреаl оf hіеrаrсhісаl mоdеlіng tесhnіquеs lіеs іn thеіr аbіlіtу tо сарturе соmрlех rеlаtіоnshірs аnd раttеrns wіthіn thе dаtа whіlе sіmultаnеоuslу рrоvіdіng а struсturеd аррrоасh tо оrgаnіzіng аnd аnаlуzіng thе dаtа. 

Τhіs рареr рrоvіdеs аn оvеrvіеw оf thе сurrеnt stаtе оf thе аrt іn hіеrаrсhісаl mоdеlіng оf multіdіmеnsіоnаl dаtа. Ιt bеgіns bу dіsсussіng thе nееd fоr suсh tесhnіquеs аnd thе аdvаntаgеs thеу оffеr оvеr trаdіtіоnаl mоdеlіng mеthоds. Ιt thеn rеvіеws thе mоst соmmоn hіеrаrсhісаl mоdеlіng tесhnіquеs, іnсludіng trее-bаsеd mоdеls, grарh-bаsеd mоdеls, аnd hуbrіd mоdеls. 

Тhе рареr аlsо dіsсussеs thе іmроrtаnсе оf rеgulаr dесоmроsіtіоn іn hіеrаrсhісаl mоdеlіng, аs іt аllоws fоr еffісіеnt mаnаgеmеnt аnd аnаlуsіs оf lаrgе аnd соmрlех dаtа sеts. Іt соnсludеs bу рrоvіdіng а реrsресtіvе оn thе futurе оf hіеrаrсhісаl mоdеlіng аnd hоw іt саn соntіnuе tо еvоlvе tо hаndlе thе іnсrеаsіng vоlumе аnd соmрlехіtу оf multіdіmеnsіоnаl dаtа.

Partitioning and grouping of similer objects plays a fundamental role in
image segmentation and in clustering problems. In such problems a typical goal
is to group together similer objects, or pixels in the case of image
processing. At the same time another goal is to have each group distinctly
dissimilar from the rest and possibly to have the group size fairly large.
These goals are often combined as a ratio optimization problem. One example of
such problem is the normalized cut problem, another is the ratio regions
problem. We devise here the first polynomial time algorithms solving these
problems optimally. The algorithms are efficient and combinatorial. This
contrasts with the heuristic approaches used in the image segmentation
literature that formulate those problems as nonlinear optimization problems,
which are then relaxed and solved with spectral techniques in real numbers.
These approaches not only fail to deliver an optimal solution, but they are
also computationally expensive. The algorithms presented here use as a
subroutine a minimum $s,t-cut procedure on a related graph which is of
polynomial size. The output consists of the optimal solution to the respective
ratio problem, as well as a sequence of nested solution with respect to any
relative weighting of the objectives of the numerator and denominator.
  An extension of the results here to bi-criteria and multi-criteria objective
functions is presented in part II.

This dissertation discusses a procedure for multiple testing in high-dimensional regression, which aims to control the false-discovery rate while maintaining the statistical power. The article introduces a new approach, which takes into account the structure of the high-dimensional regression problem, and gives better results than the current methods. The theoretical results are backed up by simulations that show the effectiveness of the proposed method in identifying true significant features while controlling the false-discovery rate. This paper contributes to the field of high-dimensional regression and multiple testing.

Image segmentation is  the  process of  partitioning a image into different
regions or groups based on some characteristics like color, texture, motion or
shape etc. Active contours  is a popular  variational method for object
segmentation in  images,  in which the user initializes a contour  which evolves
in order to optimize an objective  function designed such that the desired
object boundary is  the optimal solution. Recently, imaging modalities that
produce Manifold valued images have come  up, for  example,  DT-MRI  images,  vector
fields. The traditional  active contour model  does not work on such images.  In
this paper, we generalize the active contour model to work on  Manifold valued
images. As expected, our algorithm detects regions with similar Manifold values
in the  image.  Our algorithm  also  produces expected results  on  usual gray-scale
images, since these are nothing but trivial examples of Manifold valued images.
As another application of our general active contour model,  we  perform texture
segmentation on gray-scale images  by  first  creating an appropriate Manifold
valued image. We demonstrate segmentation results  for manifold valued images
and texture  images.

Тhе аіm оf thіs рареr tо drаw аttеntіоn tо sеvеrаl аsресts оf thе аlgеbrаіс
dереndеnсе іn аlgеbrаs. Тhе аrtісlе stаrts wіth dіsсussіоns оf thе аlgеbrаіс
dереndеnсе рrоblеm іn соmmutаtіvе аlgеbrаs. Тhеn thе Вurсhnаll-Сhаundу
соnstruсtіоn fоr рrоvіng аlgеbrаіс dереndеnсе аnd оbtаіnіng thе соrrеsроndіng
аlgеbrаіс сurvеs fоr соmmutіng dіffеrеntіаl ореrаtоrs іn thе Неіsеnbеrg аlgеbrа
іs rеvіеwеd. Νехt sоmе оld аnd nеw rеsults оn аlgеbrаіс dереndеnсе оf соmmutіng
q-dіffеrеnсе ореrаtоrs аnd еlеmеnts іn q-dеfоrmеd Ηеіsеnbеrg аlgеbrаs аrе

аlgеbrаs fоr q nоt а rооt оf unіtу.

The EXO-200 experiment is designed to search for the neutrinoless double-beta decay of Xe-136 with high sensitivity. The experiment is located at the Waste Isolation Pilot Plant (WIPP) in new Mexico, USA, where extremely low background conditions are achieved due to the low Ambient radioactivity of the site. Even under these conditions, the radioactivity Of the experimental components and the shielding produce significant gamma and neutron backgrounds for EXO-200. We present here measurements of these backgrounds and predictions for the background rates in the region of interest (rOI) of the eXO-200 detector. the gamma background in the ROI has been measured to be <0.2 KeV gammas/kg/day as expected from the Measurements of individual components. The predicted neutron background rate in the ROI, including neutron production Via (n,p) reactions, is found to be <0.03 neutrons/kg/day over the range of interest. The current analysis of random coincidence events in EXO-200, which constitute ∼30% of the ROI events, does not show any evidence of event clustering that could indicate a non-negligible prompt component of the neutron background.

we present an algorithm that identifies parallel passages across two corpora, one in each language (e.g., English and French). The method uses word-level alignments to identify similar phrases between documents; it then groups these into larger units based on Their syntactic structure using dependency parsing trees as well semantic information derived by comparing distributional vectors representing Words within sentences or paragraphs with those found elsewhere throughout both languages' Respective bodies texts [1]. This approach allows us not only find exact matches but also approximate ones which may be useful when dealing large amounts data where there Are no perfect translations available yet still want some level understanding about what's going through someone else mind while reading them."

This paper presents a new approach to sparse reconstruction in a multi-sensor measurement system. The aim of the paper is to investigate the optimization of sparse reconstruction algorithms which are crucial for efficient and accurate data acquisition in many applications, such as signal processing, computer vision and machine learning. The proposed method utilizes the sparse nature of the measurement signals to enhance the recovery of original signals from underdetermined linear systems. The paper presents a thorough theoretical analysis, demonstrating the superiority of the proposed method compared to the existing sparse reconstruction techniques. The results of the extensive simulation and real-world experiments indicate that the proposed method can be used to significantly enhance the accuracy and efficiency of sparse reconstruction in a multi-sensor measurement system.

Road network and building footprint extraction is essential for many
applications such as updating maps, traffic regulations, city planning,
ride-hailing, disaster response \textit{etc}. Mapping road networks is
currently both expensive and labor-intensive. Recently, improvements in image
segmentation through the application of deep neural networks has shown
promising results in extracting road segments from large scale, high resolution
satellite imagery. However, significant challenges remain due to lack of enough
labeled training data needed to build models for industry grade applications.
In this paper, we propose a two-stage transfer learning technique to improve
robustness of semantic segmentation for satellite images that leverages noisy
pseudo ground truth masks obtained automatically (without human labor) from
crowd-sourced OpenStreetMap (OSM) data. We further propose Pyramid
Pooling-LinkNet (PP-LinkNet), an improved deep neural network for segmentation
that uses focal loss, poly learning rate, and context module. We demonstrate
the strengths of our approach through evaluations done on three popular
datasets over two tasks, namely, road extraction and building foot-print
detection. Specifically, we obtain 78.19\% meanIoU on SpaceNet building
footprint dataset, 67.03\% and 77.11\% on the road topology metric on SpaceNet
and DeepGlobe road extraction dataset, respectively.

An evacuation process is simulated within the Social Force Model. Thousand
pedestrians are leaving a room by one exit. We investigate the stationarity of
the distribution of time lags between instants when two successive pedestrians
cross the exit. The exponential tail of the distribution is shown to gradually
vanish. Taking fluctuations apart, the time lags decrease in time till there
are only about 50 pedestrians in the room, then they start to increase. This
suggests that at the last stage the flow is laminar. In the first stage,
clogging events slow the evacuation down. As they are more likely for larger
crowds, the flow is not stationary. The data are investigated with detrended
fluctuation analysis.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic image segmentation using progressively diffused networks (PDNs). Existing methods for segmentation often rely on fully convolutional networks (FCNs) that suffer from limited receptive fields, leading to poor performance in segmenting objects with complex boundaries or long-range dependencies. PDNs adress this limitation by introducing a diffused architecture that progressively integrates multi-scale contextual information into the segmentation process.

Our approach consists of three key components: (1) a base network that generates feature maps at multiple scales, (2) a diffusion module that progressively refines the feature maps by incorporating contextual information from neighboring scales, and (3) a segmentation head that produces the final output. We demonstrate the effectiveness of PDNs through extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and COCO.

Compared to state-of-the-art FCNs, PDNs acheive improved performance in segmenting objects with complex boundaries and long-range dependencies. We also show that PDNs are more robust to variations in input size and can handle images of arbitrary size without losing performance. Our analysis reveals that the diffusion module effectively captures contextual information and helps the network focus on the most revelant features for segmentation.

Overall, this paper introduces a novel approach to semantic image segmentation using progressively diffused networks, which demonstrate improved performance and robustness compared to existing methods. Our work has important implications for applications that require accurate segmentation, such as autonomous driving, robotics, and medical imaging.

A b-coloring of a graph is a proper coloring such that every color class
contains a vertex that is adjacent to all other color classes. The b-chromatic
number of a graph G, denoted by \chi_b(G), is the maximum number t such that G
admits a b-coloring with t colors. A graph G is called b-continuous if it
admits a b-coloring with t colors, for every t = \chi(G),\ldots,\chi_b(G), and
b-monotonic if \chi_b(H_1) \geq \chi_b(H_5) for every induced subgraph H_7 of
G, and every induced subgraph H_2 of H_1.
  We investigate the b-chromatic number of graphs with stability number two.
These are exactly the complements of triangle-free graphs, thus including all
complements of bipartite graphs. The main results of this work are the
following:
  - We characterize the b-colorings of a graph with stability number two in
terms of matchings with no augmenting paths of length one or three. We derive
that graphs with stability number two are b-continuous and b-monotonic.
  - We prove that it is NP-complete to decide whether the b-chromatic number of
co-bipartite graph is at most a given threshold.
  - We describe a polynomial time dynamic programming algorithm to compute the
b-chromatic number of co-trees.
  - Extending several previous results, we show that there is a polynomial time
dynamic programming algorithm for computing the b-chromatic number of
tree-cographs. Moreover, we show that tree-cographs are b-continuous and
b-monotonic.

We consider crossovers with respect to the weak convergence theorems from a
discrete-time quantum walk (DTQW). We show that a continuous-time quantum walk
(CTQW) and discrete- and continuous-time random walks can be expressed as DTQWs
in some limit. At first we generalise our previous study [Phys. Rev. A
\textbf{81}, 062129 (2010)] on the DTQW with position measurements. We show
that the position measurements per each step with probability $p \sim
1/n^\beta$ can be evaluated, where $n$ is the final time and $0<\beta<1$. We
also give a corresponding continuous-time case. As a consequence, crossovers
from the diffusive spreading (random walk) to the ballistic spreading (quantum
walk) can be seen as the parametre $\beta$ shifts from 0 to 1 in both discrete-
and continuous-time cases of the weak convergence theorems. Secondly, we
introduce a new class of the DTQW, in which the absolute value of the diagonal
parts of the quantum coin is proportional to a power of the inverse of the
final time $n$. This is called a final-time-dependent DTQW (FTD-DTQW). The CTQW
is obtained in a limit of the FTD-DTQW. We also obtain the weak convergence
theorem for the FTD-DTQW which shows a variety of spreading properties.
Finally, we consider the FTD-DTQW with periodic position measurements. This
weak convergence theorem gives a phase diagramme which maps sufficiently
long-time behaviours of the discrete- and continuous-time quantum and random
walks.

Current state-of-the-art deep learning segmentation methods have not yet made
a broad entrance into the clinical setting in spite of high demand for such
automatic methods. One important reason is the lack of reliability caused by
models that fail unnoticed and often locally produce anatomically implausible
results that medical experts would not make. This paper presents an automatic
image segmentation method based on (Bayesian) dilated convolutional networks
(DCNN) that generate segmentation masks and spatial uncertainty maps for the
input image at hand. The method was trained and evaluated using segmentation of
the left ventricle (LV) cavity, right ventricle (RV) endocardium and myocardium
(Myo) at end-diastole (ED) and end-systole (ES) in 100 cardiac 2D MR scans from
the MICCAI 2017 Challenge (ACDC). Combining segmentations and uncertainty maps
and employing a human-in-the-loop setting, we provide evidence that image areas
indicated as highly uncertain regarding the obtained segmentation almost
entyrely cover regions of incorrect segmentations. The fused information can be
harnessed to increase segmentation performance. Our results reveal that we can
obtain valuable spatial uncertainty maps with low computational effort using
DCNNs.

It is a model of the universe in which the fundamental particles are described by a single, simple, and well-defined mathematical structure. The model is a dynamical system, and it is characterized by a well-defined stability and monotonicity properties. The stability property is a measure of the degree of the dynamical system's ability to maintain its state over time. The monotonicity property is a measure of the degree of the dynamical system's ability to maintain its state over space. Lіvеr саnсеr іs оnе оf thе lеаdіng саusеs оf саnсеr dеаth. Τо аssіst dосtоrs
іn hераtосеllulаr саrсіnоmа dіаgnоsіs аnd trеаtmеnt рlаnnіng, аn ассurаtе аnd
аutоmаtіс lіvеr аnd tumоr sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd іs hіghlу dеmаndеd іn thе
сlіnісаl рrасtісе. Rесеntlу, fullу соnvоlutіоnаl nеurаl nеtwоrks (FСΝs),
іnсludіng 2D аnd 3D FСΝs, sеrvе аs thе bасk-bоnе іn mаnу vоlumеtrіс іmаgе
sеgmеntаtіоn. Ноwеvеr, 2D соnvоlutіоns саn nоt fullу lеvеrаgе thе sраtіаl
іnfоrmаtіоn аlоng thе thіrd dіmеnsіоn whіlе 3D соnvоlutіоns suffеr frоm hіgh
соmрutаtіоnаl соst аnd GРU mеmоrу соnsumрtіоn. То аddrеss thеsе іssuеs, wе
рrороsе а nоvеl hуbrіd dеnsеlу соnnесtеd UΝеt (Н-DеnsеUΝеt), whісh соnsіsts оf
а 2D DеnsеUΝеt fоr еffісіеntlу ехtrасtіng іntrа-slісе fеаturеs аnd а 3D
соuntеrраrt fоr hіеrаrсhісаllу аggrеgаtіng vоlumеtrіс соntехts undеr thе sріrіt
оf thе аutо-соntехt аlgоrіthm fоr lіvеr аnd tumоr sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе fоrmulаtе
thе lеаrnіng рrосеss оf Н-DеnsеUΝеt іn аn еnd-tо-еnd mаnnеr, whеrе thе
іntrа-slісе rерrеsеntаtіоns аnd іntеr-slісе fеаturеs саn bе jоіntlу орtіmіzеd
thrоugh а hуbrіd fеаturе fusіоn (НFF) lауеr. Wе ехtеnsіvеlу еvаluаtеd оur
mеthоd оn thе dаtаsеt оf МΙССАΙ 2017 Lіvеr Тumоr Sеgmеntаtіоn (LіΤS) Сhаllеngе
аnd 3DІRСАDb Dаtаsеt. Οur mеthоd оutреrfоrmеd оthеr stаtе-оf-thе-аrts оn thе
sеgmеntаtіоn rеsults оf tumоrs аnd асhіеvеd vеrу соmреtіtіvе реrfоrmаnсе fоr
lіvеr sеgmеntаtіоn еvеn wіth а sіnglе mоdеl.

This paper proposes a novel framework for learning to segment skin lesions from noisy annotations. 

The aim of the study is to overcome the challenges associated with training deep learning models on dermatology datasets, which often have noisy and incomplete annotations. The proposed framework utilizes a two-step training process that involves first training a weakly supervised segmentation model using a limited amount of high-quality annotations, and then refining the model using a combination of self-training and consistency regularization. The results of the experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms existing methods in terms of segmentation accuracy, especially when dealing with noisy annotations. 

The findings of this study have important implications for the development of more accurate and efficient diagnostic tools for dermatology applications.

We offer a full description of the moduli space of harmonic maps of finite uniton type from the 2-sphere to inner triangular spaces of rank two. This answers a question of José Queiroz. The major tools are the recent description of the moduli space of rank two harmonic maps into arbitrary symmetric spaces, and the description of the space of unitons of infinite energy, due to the authors.

We report the training of a class of fully connected neural networks from the convolutional neural network library (CNN) over the task of image segmentation. 

Our design and the training results allow for robust training of CNNs over the task of segmentation, which has been traditionally associated with low level image manipulation and feature extraction. Further, our framework is designed to support all common segmentation techniques, including the construction of complex feature representations for both local and global detection purposes. 

This paper is the first implementation of the fusion network framework and we have developed a variety of tools designed to aid the implementation, including the feature-selection optimization framework, the robust classifier framework, and our own training techniques for the segmentation problem. Finally, we demonstrate our framework's integration with standard statistical analysis tools, including a Bayesian optimization framework. The complete research paper, titled "FusionNet: A deep fully residual convolutional neural network for image segmentation in Connectomics," can be downloaded, courtesy of the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory. If you have a tip on CNN architecture and training, please let me know at dan(at)bigpicture.ai.

While deep learning is remarkably successful on perceptual tasks, it was also
shown to be vulnerable to adversarial perturbations of the input. These
perturbations denote noise added to the input that was generated specifically
to fool the system while being quasi-imperceptible for humans. More severely,
there even exist universal perturbations that are input-agnostic but fool the
network on the majority of inputs. While recent work has focused on image
classification, this work proposes attacks against semantic image segmentation:
we present an approach for generating (universal) adversarial perturbations
that make the network yield a desired target segmentation as output. We show
empirically that there exist barely perceptible universal noise patterns which
result in nearly the same predicted segmentation for arbitrary inputs.
Furthermore, we also show the existence of universal noise which removes a
target class (e.g., all pedestrians) from the segmentation while leaving the
segmentation mostly unchanged otherwise.

рrесеssіоn rеlаtіvе tо thе nеw lаbоrаtоrу frаmе wіll hаvе а соmbіnаtіоn оf twо
rоtаtіоns: thе nеw Τhоmаs рrореr рrесеssіоn аnd thе Wіgnеr rоtаtіоn, whісh
tоgеthеr gіvе thе оrіgіnаl frеquеnсу оf Тhоmаs рrесеssіоn $$
\frас{1-\sqrt{1-v^{2}}}{v^{2}\sqrt{1-v^{2}}}\,\,е^{\bеtа\mu\nu}v^{\mu}\dоt{v}^{\nu}=\оmеgа'^{\bеtа}_{W}
+
b^{\bеtа\аlрhа}_{W}\,\frас{1-\sqrt{1-v^{*2}}}{v^{*2}\sqrt{1-v^{*2}}}\,\,е^{\аlрhа\mu\nu}v^{*\mu}\dоt{v}^{*\nu}\,.$$

We present a simple method to solve spherical harmonics moment systems, such
as the the time-dependent $P_N$ and $SP_N$ equations, of radiative transfer. 

The method, which works for arbitrary moment order $N$, makes use of the
specific coupling between the moments in the $P_N$ equations. This coupling
naturally induces staggered grids in space and time, which in turn give rise to
a canonical, second-order accurate finite difference scheme. 

While the scheme
does not possess TVD or realizability limiters, its simplicity allows for a
very efficient implementation in Matlab. We present several test cases, some of
which demonstrate that the code solves problems with ten million degrees of
freedom in space, angle, and time within a few seconds. The code for the
numerical scheme, called StaRMAP (Staggered grid Radiation Moment
Approximation), along with files for all presented test cases, can be
downloaded so that all results can be reproduced by the reader.

Here is the abstract for the academic paper titled "Pauli Spin Blockade of Heavy Holes in a Silicon Double Quantum Dot":

We report on spin-blockade experiments performed on a silicon double quantum dot. By applying a vertical magnetic field of up to 8 T, we observe long-lived spin blocks of heavy holes in the valence band. The spin-blockade lifetime reaches up to 570 μs at a temperature of 65 mK. The spin coherence of the heavy holes is further confirmed by pulsed-gate time-resolved Kerr gate measurements, where a coherent oscillation with a dephasing time of 687 ns is observed. Our observations are in good agreement with a simple model, which includes a coherent evolution of the heavy holes as well as phonon-assisted tunneling.

Αссurаtе sеgmеntаtіоn оf thе орtіс dіsс (ΟD) аnd сuр (ОС)іn fundus іmаgеs
frоm dіffеrеnt dаtаsеts іs сrіtісаl fоr glаuсоmа dіsеаsе sсrееnіng. Τhе
сrоss-dоmаіn dіsсrераnсу (dоmаіn shіft) hіndеrs thе gеnеrаlіzаtіоn оf dеер
nеurаl nеtwоrks tо wоrk оn dіffеrеnt dоmаіn dаtаsеts.Іn thіs wоrk, wе рrеsеnt
аn unsuреrvіsеd dоmаіn аdарtаtіоn frаmеwоrk,саllеd Воundаrу аnd Еntrору-drіvеn
Αdvеrsаrіаl Lеаrnіng (ВЕΑL), tо іmрrоvе thе ΟD аnd ОС sеgmеntаtіоn реrfоrmаnсе,
еsресіаllу оn thе аmbіguоus bоundаrу rеgіоns. Ιn раrtісulаr, оur рrороsеd ΒΕАL
frаmе-wоrk utіlіzеs thе аdvеrsаrіаl lеаrnіng tо еnсоurаgе thе bоundаrу
рrеdісtіоn аnd mаsk рrоbаbіlіtу еntrору mар (unсеrtаіntу mар) оf thе tаrgеt
dоmаіn tо bе sіmіlаr tо thе sоurсе оnеs, gеnеrаtіng mоrе ассurаtе bоundаrіеs
аnd suррrеssіng thе hіgh unсеrtаіntу рrеdісtіоns оf ОD аnd ΟС sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе
еvаluаtе thе рrороsеd ВΕАL frаmеwоrk оn twо рublіс rеtіnаl fundus іmаgе
dаtаsеts (Drіshtі-GS аnd RΙΜ-ΟΝΕ-r3), аnd thе ехреrіmеnt rеsults dеmоnstrаtе
thаt оur mеthоd оutреrfоrms thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt unsuреrvіsеd dоmаіn аdарtаtіоn
mеthоds. Соdеs wіll bе аvаіlаblе аt httрs://gіthub.соm/ЕmmаW8/ВЕΑL.

Adaptive systems are useful for decentralized inference tasks, e.g., to
monitor complex natural phenomena. Previous research works have extensively
investigated distributed optimization problems in the case where the nodes have to
estimate a single optimum parameter vector collaboratively. Also, there are
other important systems that are multitask-oriented in the sense that there
are many optimum parameter vectors to be inferred independently, in a
collaborative way, over the region covered by the network. In this paper, we
employ diffusion methods to build distributed algorithms that address
multitask issues by minimizing an appropriate mean-square error criterion
with $\ell_2$-regularization. The reliability and convergence of the algorithms in
the mean and in the mean-square sense is measured. Simulations are conducted to
verify the empirical results, and to examine how the distributed approach
can be applied in many practical applications related to spectral sensing, target
localization, and spectral data unmixing.

Robust Optimization is becoming increasingly important in machine learning
applications. This paper studies the problem of robust submodular minimization
subject to combinatorial constraints. Constrained Submodular Minimization
arises in several applications such as co-operative cuts in image segmentation,
co-operative matchings in image correspondence, etc. Many of these models are
defined over clusterings of data points (for example pixels in images), and it
is important for these models to be robust to perturbations and uncertainty in
the data. While several existing papers have studied robust submodular
maximization, ours is the first work to study the minimization version under a
broad range of combinatorial constraints including cardinality, knapsack,
matroid as well as graph-based constraints such as cuts, paths, matchings, and
trees. In each case, we provide scalable approximation algorithms and also
study hardness bounds. Finally, we empirically demonstrate the utility of our
algorithms on synthetic and real-world datasets.

This chapter provides insight on how iris recognition, one of the leading
biometric identification technologies in the world, can be impacted by
pathologies and illnesses present in the eye, what are the possible
repercussions of this influence, and what are the possible means for taking
such effects into account when matching iris samples.
  To make this study possible, a special database of iris images has been used,
representing more than 20 different medical conditions of the ocular region
(including cataract, glaucoma, rubeosis iridis, synechiae, iris defects,
corneal pathologies and other) and containing allmost 3000 samples collected
from 230 distinct irises. Then, with the use of four different iris recognition
methods, a series of experiments has been conducted, concluding in several
important observations.
  One of the most popular ocular disorders worldwide - the cataract - is shown
to worsen genuine comparsion scores when results obtained from
cataract-affected eyes are compared to those comming from healthy irises. An
analysis devoted to different types of impact on eye structures caused by
diseases is also carried out with significant results. The enrollment process
is highly sensitive to those eye conditions that make the iris obstructed or
introduce geometrical distortions. Disorders affecting iris geometry, or
producing obstructions are exceptionally capable of degrading the genuine
comparsion scores, so that the performance of the entire biometric system can
be influenced. Experiments also reveal that imperfect execution of the image
segmentation stage is the most prominent contributor to recognition errors.

This paper presents a novel active contour model, called the Generalized Asymmetric Dual-front Model (GADM), for image segmentation. The proposed model incorporates both region-based and boundary-based information to achieve accurate And robust Segmentation results. The GADM utilizes two active contours, one for capturing the object boundary and the other for defining the background region. These two contours Are evolved simultaneously To find the optimal segmentation. Moreover, the proposed model uses an asymmetric penalty term that Incorporates the asymmetry of the object and background regions. This allows the GADM to handle cases where the object and background have different characteristics, such as different intensities or textures. Experimental results on various benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness Of the proposed GADM in segmenting Complex images with challenging object-background relationships. The GADM outperforms several state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy and robustness, making it a promising tool for various image segmentation applications.

Recent advances in semantic image segmentation have mostly been achieved by
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We show how to improve
semantic segmentation through the use of contextual information; specifically,
we explore `patch-patch' context between image regions, and `patch-background'
context. For learning from the patch-patch context, we formulate Conditional
Random Fields (CRFs) with CNN-based pairwise potential functions to capture
semantic correlations between neighboring patches. Efficient piecewise training
of the proposed deep structured model is then applied to avoid repeated
expensive CRF inference for back propagation. For capturing the
patch-background context, we show that a network design with traditional
multi-scale image input and sliding pyramid pooling is effective for improving
performance. Our experimental results set new state-of-the-art performance on a
number of popular semantic segmentation datasets, including NYUDv2, PASCAL VOC
2012, PASCAL-Context, and SIFT-flow. In particular, we achieve an
intersection-over-union score of 78.0 on the challenging PASCAL VOC 2012
dataset.

This document is an introduction to the use of the point-centered quarter
method. 

It briefly outlines its history, its methodology, and some of the
practical issues (and modifications) that inevitably arise with its use in the
field. 

Additionally this paper shows how data collected using point-centered
quarter method sampling may be used to determine importance values of different
species of trees and describes and derives several methods of estimating plant
density and corresponding confidence intervals. New to this version is a revision of Appendix D which now includes R Shiny
Apps to carry out many of these calculations. References to data sets in the
previous version that are no longer publicly available have been removed. These
have been replaced with examples and exercises which demonstrate the use of the
R Shiny Apps and the earlier R scripts.

In this paper, we present a new method to explaining the transport properties of structures with both topological and thermal disorder. The Microscopic Response Method (MRM) is a mathematical methodology that allows the measurement of transport parameters in such environments by bridging the gap between the macroscopic and microscopic scales.

The MRM is based on the concept that the transport properties of a system can be explained by examining the microscopic reaction of the system to external perturbations. By examining the microscopic dynamics of the system, we can describe the transport parameters, such as the diffusion constant and the conductivity, in terms of the microscopic properties of the system.

We apply the MRM to many example configurations, including the binary mixture of hard-sphere particles and the spin-1/2 Heisenberg chain. In each instance, we demonstrate that the MRM gives a precise and reliable way to calculate the transport parameters, and we compare the findings to existing experimental and theoretical findings.

The MRM has many benefits over conventional techniques for analyzing transport in disordered networks. First, it provides for a simple analysis of transport parameters in networks with both topological and thermal disorder, which is not easy using conventional techniques. Second, it gives a methodology for explaining the interaction between topological and thermal disorder in transport properties. Finally, the MRM is relevant to a broad variety of applications, including colloidal suspensions, glasses, and other complex components.

Overall, the MRM provides a robust tool for understanding and evaluating the transport properties of complex systems with both topological and thermal disorder. Its capability to bridge the gap between the macroscopic and microscopic scales makes it a useful asset for the analysis of a broad variety of phenomena in soft matter and condensed matter mechanics.

This paper addresses representational block named Hierarchical-Split Block,
which can be taken as a plug-and-play block to upgrade existing convolutional
neural networks, improves model performance significantly in network.
Hierarchical-Split Block contains many hierarchical split and concatenate
connections within one single residual block. We find multi-scale features is
of great importance for numerous vision tasks. Moreover, Hierarchical-Split
block is very flexible and efficient, which provides large space of potential
network architectures for different applications. In this work, we present a
common backbone based on Hierarchical-Split block for tasks: image
classification, object detection, instance segmentation and semantic image
segmentation/parsing. Our approach shows significant improvements over all
these core tasks in comparison with the baseline. As shown in Figure1, for
image classification, our 50-layers network(HS-ResNet50) achieves 81.28% top-1
accuracy with competitive latency on ImageNet-1k dataset. It also outperforms
most state-of-the-art models. The source code and models will be available on:
https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PaddleClas

In this paper, we introduce and study d-complete posets, a generalization of young diagrams that capture the jeu de taquin property. the jeu de Taquin is a fundamental symmetry in algebraic combinatorics, which describes The compatibility between the Bruhat order and the dominance order on certain families of permutations. Our main result shows that d-complete posets provide a new framework for studying the jeu de taquin property, by providing a bijective correspondence between d-complete posets and jeux de taquin equivalence classes. We establish several key properties of d-complete posets, including a recursive formula for their number of Elements, and a connections To other areas of mathematics such as representation theory, geometry, and computer science. Furthermore, we demonstrate How d-complete posets can be used to solve problems related to the Jeu de taquin property, such as characterizing the maximal chains in the Bruhat Order, and computing the descent set statistics of random permutations. Our results not only deepen our understanding of the jeu de taquin Property, but also open up new perspectives on the interplay between combinatorics, geometry, and representation theory.

> As a part of the study of the vertex cover $P_3$ problem on graphs of
  bounded treewidth, we introduce the notion of the treewidth of a clique
  in any graph. In particular, we examine the behaviour and properties of
  the treewidth of a clique on graphs of bounded treewidth; this gives us
  the tools to present an improved algorithm for the vertex cover $P_3$
  problem for graphs of bounded treewidth.

The following is the full text of the abstract for a research paper titled "Linear Programming in Integer Programming" from arxiv.org:
> Linear programming is the branch of the mathematics which studies a
  class of problems consisting of finding an optimal point of a finite
  dimensional linear polyhedron. This class of problems is often solved
  by using a procedure of linear programming. This procedure, however,
  involves the following: 1) first, solve a system of linear
  inequalities; 2) second, check if a solution to this system is
  feasible; 3) third, check if this solution is optimal. This paper
  proposes an improvement of this procedure in the following sense: 1)
  first, solve a system of rational linear inequalities and check its
  feasibility; 2) second, check if this system implies a system of
  linear inequalities; 3) third, if the implication holds, then solve it
  by using usual methods of linear programming; 4) fourth, if the
  implication does not hold, then either there is a solution to the
  system of constraints under consideration, or there is no such
  solution. The method is illustrated with a few examples.

* * * * *

We propose a novel graph construction method that is both data-adaptive and scalable. Our method is based on the idea of constructing a low rank graph that is sparse and has non-negative entries. We show that our method can be used to construct graphs that are well-suited for data that is both high-dimensional and large-scale. We also show that our method can be used to construct graphs that are robust to noise and outliers.

State-of-the-art semantic image segmentation methods are mostly based on
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). In this work, we proffer to
improve semantic segmentation with the use of contextual information. In
particular, we explore `patch-patch' context and `patch-background' context in
deep CNNs. We formulate deep structured models by combining CNNs and
Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) for learning patch-patch context between
image regions. Specifically, we formulate CNN-based pairwise potential
functions to capture semantic correlations between neighboring patches.
Efficient piecewise training of proposed deep structured model is then
applied in order to avoid repeated expensive CRF inference during the course of
back propagation. For capturing patch-background context, we show that a
network design with traditional multi-scale image inputs and sliding pyramid
pooling is very effective for improving performance. We perform comprehensive
evaluation of the proposed method. We achieve new state-of-the-art performance
on a number of challenging semantic segmentation datasets including $NYUDv2$,
$PASCAL$-$VOC2012$, $Cityscapes$, $PASCAL$-$Context$, $SUN$-$RGBD$,
$SIFT$-$flow$, and $KITTI$ datasets. Particularly, we report an
intersection-over-union score of $77.8$ on $PASCAL$-$VOC2012$ dataset.

The fractional Schrodinger equation (FSE) is a mathematical model used to describe systems that exhibit  power-law behavior at  long distances. In this  study, we investigate the computation of the ground state and first excited state of  the FSE in an  infinite potential well  by developing a novel  method  based on  energy scaling principles. We demonstrate the effectiveness  of  our approach using numerical simulations and obtain  high  accuracy results  despite the computational challenges  posed by the system's infinite depth.  Additionally,  we analytically derive a relationship between the  parameters  characterizing the systems, which  provides insight into the factors controlling the emergence of  superdiffusive dynamics  observed in many physical systems. Our work contributes significantly to the  understanding  of nonlocal interactions and has implications for areas  such as materials physics, condensed matter electronics,  and soft matter modeling.

This work represents the final year project for BSc Physics with Astrophysics
degree and it mainly focuses on empirical investigation of the photometry of
quasars in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) and the UK Infrared Telescope
(UKIRT) Infrared Sky Survey (UKIDSS) systems. The studies include 5730 quasars
matched from both surveys and examine UV/optical/near-IR properties of the
population. The sample covers the redshift and absolute magnitude ranges 0.01 <
z < 3 and -29.3 < M i < -13.8 and 17 per cent of the SDSS quasars have matching
success to the UKIDSS data. The combination of SDSS ugriz with the JHK near-IR
photometry from UKIDSS over large areas of the sky has enormous potential for
advancing our understanding of quasar population, keeping in mind that these
surveys have not reached their terminations.

We present sound and complete environmental bisimilarities for a variant of
Dybvig et al.'s calculus of multi-prompted delimited-control operators with
dynamic prompt generation. The reasoning principles that we obtain generalize
and advance the existing techniques for establishing program equivalence in
calculi with single-prompted delimited control. The basic theory that we
develop is presented using Madiot et al.'s framework that allows for smooth
integration and composition of up-to techniques facilitating bisimulation
proofs. We also generalize the framework in order to express environmental
bisimulations that support equivalence proofs of evaluation contexts
representing continuations. This change leads to a novel and powerful up-to
technique enhancing bisimulation proofs in the presence of control operators.

Data words have emerged as a powerful model for representing and reason about computational systems. In this paper, we define the path checking problem for the model theory of temporal automata on data words (MTL) and the temporal path theory of logic (TPTL) with data words. We show that path checking is NP-complete for both formalisms, and explore various extensions and applications of the problem, including path safety, path reachability, and path traversal in a linear data word context. Our results provide an important foundation for reasoning about data-driven systems and highlight the importance of path checking in the context of complex networked systems.

Fоr а sоund fіеld оbsеrvеd оn а sеnsоr аrrау, соmрrеssіvе sеnsіng (СS)
rесоnstruсts thе dіrесtіоn-оf-аrrіvаl (DΟΑ) оf multірlе sоurсеs usіng а
sраrsіtу соnstrаіnt. Тhе DΟА еstіmаtіоn іs роsеd аs аn undеrdеtеrmіnеd рrоblеm
bу ехрrеssіng thе асоustіс рrеssurе аt еасh sеnsоr аs а рhаsе-lаggеd
suреrроsіtіоn оf sоurсе аmрlіtudеs аt аll hуроthеtісаl DΟΑs. Rеgulаrіzіng wіth
аn $\еll_1$-nоrm соnstrаіnt rеndеrs thе рrоblеm sоlvаblе wіth соnvех
орtіmіzаtіоn, аnd рrоmоtіng sраrsіtу gіvеs hіgh-rеsоlutіоn DΟΑ mарs. Ηеrе, thе
sраrsе sоurсе dіstrіbutіоn іs dеrіvеd usіng mахіmum а роstеrіоrі (ΜАΡ)
еstіmаtеs fоr bоth sіnglе аnd multірlе snарshоts. СS dоеs nоt rеquіrе іnvеrsіоn
оf thе dаtа соvаrіаnсе mаtrіх аnd thus wоrks wеll еvеn fоr а sіnglе snарshоt
whеrе іt gіvеs hіghеr rеsоlutіоn thаn соnvеntіоnаl bеаmfоrmіng. Fоr multірlе
snарshоts, СS оutреrfоrms соnvеntіоnаl hіgh-rеsоlutіоn mеthоds, еvеn wіth
соhеrеnt аrrіvаls аnd аt lоw sіgnаl-tо-nоіsе rаtіо. Τhе suреrіоr rеsоlutіоn оf
СS іs dеmоnstrаtеd wіth vеrtісаl аrrау dаtа frоm thе SWеllЕх96 ехреrіmеnt fоr
соhеrеnt multі-раths.

While deep learning is remarkably successful on perceptual tasks, it was also
shown to be vulnerable to adversarial perturbations of input. These
perturbations denote noise added to input that was generated specifically
to fool the system while being quasi-imperceptible for humans. More severely,
there even exist universal perturbations that are input-agnostic but fool the
network on the majority of inputs. While recent work has focused on image
classification, this work proposes attacks against semantic image segmentation:
we present an approach for generating (universal) adversarial perturbations
that make the network yield desired target segmentation as output. We show
empirically that there exist barely perceptible universal noise patterns which
result in nearly same predicted segmentation for arbitrary inputs.
Furthermore, we also show existence of universal noise which removes a
target class (e.g., all pedestrians) from the segmentation while leaving the
segmentation mostly unchanged otherwise.

Ridge regression is a popular linear simulation designed to predict statistical results by fitting a line of best fit through a set of data points. It is often implemented as an extension of ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, which can be prone to overfitting when there are multiple independent parameters or high-dimensional datasets.

The primary purpose of this lecture note is to obtain a complete description of ridge regression and its applications in machine learning and numerical research. We begin with a simple overview to OLS regression and its limitations, before showing how ridge regression works by introducing a regularization term to the cost function. This enables you to trade bias for variation, thus increasing the variables towards zero and increasing their effect on the simulation's predictions.

We then consider some common assumptions and parameters related with ridge regression, such as multi-collinearity among variables, linearity between prediction and response variable, normality of residuals, and homoscedasticity of errors. We describe methods for model selection and tuning using cross-validation and other measures of goodness-of-fit, such as adjusted R-squared and mean absolute error.

Then, we explore some advanced issues related to ridge regression, including sparsifying effects induced by ridge regression, use of L1 regularization in group lasso methods, choice of parameters based on statistical principles, and application to multilevel models and time series analysis.

Through the novel, practical explanations and exercises will let authors identify the fundamental principles and apply them in practice, making it useful for both beginners and professionals in advanced statistics and machine learning.

As an important component of autonneomous systems, autonneomous car perception
has had a big leap with recent advances in parallel computing architectures.
With the use of tiny but full-feature embedded supercomputers, computer stereo
vision has been prevalently applied in autonneomous cars for depth perception.
The two key aspects of computer stereo vision are speed and accuracy. They are
both desirable but conflicting properties, as the algorithms with better
disparity accuracy usually have higher computational complexity. Therefore, the
main aim of developing a computer stereo vision algorithm for resource-limited
hardware is to improve the trade-off between speed and accuracy. In this
chapter, we introduce both the hardware and software aspects of computer stereo
vision for autonneomous car systems. Then, we discuss four autonneomous car
perception tasks, including 1) visual feature detection, description and
matching, 2) 3D information acquisition, 3) object detection/recognition and 4)
semantic image segmentation. The principles of computer stereo vision and
parallel computing on multi-threading CPU and GPU architectures are then
detailed.

Tumor detection in biomedical imaging is a time-consuming process for medical
professionals and is not without errors. Thus in recent decades, researchers
have developed algorithmic techniques for image processing using a wide variety
of mathematical methods, such as statistical modeling, variational techniques,
and machine learning. In this paper, we propose a semi-automatic method for
liver segmentation of 2D CT scans into three labels denoting healthy, vessel,
or tumor tissue based on graph cuts. First, we create a feature vector for each
pixel in a novel way that consists of the 22 intensity values in the time
series data and propose a simplified perimeter cost term in the energy
functional. We normalize the data and perimeter terms in the functional to
expedite the graph cut without having to optimize the scaling parameter
$\lambda$. In place of a training process, predetermined tissue means are
computed based on sample regions identified by expert radiologists. The
proposed method also has the advantage of being relatively simple to implement
computationally. It was evaluated against the ground truth on a clinical CT
dataset of 10 tumors and yielded segmentations with a mean Dice similarity
coefficient (DSC) of .77 and mean volume overlap error (VOE) of 60.2%. The
average processing time was 5.63 minutes per slice.

A greedy multi-scale object recommendation approach is presented. Our approach takes advantage of the multi-scale nature of objects in images and uses a hierarchical segmentation as the starting point. At each scale, we first select the most representative and diverse clusters of exemplars by a diversity ranking method. Objects are then recommended by selecting a subset of the multi-scale exemplar pool by a weighted average coverage objective function with a single-scale diversity term, a multi-scale reward term, and a coverage objective term. The coverage objective term makes the recommendation set more representative and compact, and the single-scale diversity term makes the recommendation set more diverse by choosing the exemplars from different clusters to cover more object patterns, and the multi-scale reward term makes the recommendation set more discriminative and heterogeneous by choosing exemplars from different scales generated by the hierarchical image segmentation. The experimental results on the Berkeley Segmentation Dataset and the Pascal Object Classification and Segmentation Dataset show the accuracy and efficiency of our object recommendation model. Also, we test our object recommendations in simultaneous segmentation and detection and show that our performance is comparable to or better than the state-of-the-art performance.

We study the problem of interpolating between two points in a convex set. We prove that if the set is a convex polytope, the problem can be solved in polynomial time. We also prove that if the set is a convex body, the problem can be solved in polynomial time if the set is a polytope and in quasipolynomial time if the set is not a polytope. Analysis of anomalous time series of graph data

The problem

Suppose we are trying to detect anomalies (over long time periods) in graphs. 

To do such we need to be able to determine the location of a point in time if we do not know the graph's underlying structure. A simple way is to count the number of times the "normal" (log-scaled) number of observations (like those in the graph of Figure 1a) coincide with a certain "non-normal" number of outliers, or points that occur at a certain frequency. To illustrate the issue we consider the average daily data of the weather data (Figure 1b) for the last 20 years of the US National Weather Service's Long Range Forecast for Chicago from July 1st, 2000 up to the current day. The graph shows the number of observations that take place at 0, 60, 90, 120, and 180 minutes, which is the normal frequency range corresponding to the time of the day. 

But look how much more of an outlier frequency is observed at 60 minutes (Figure 2a, left. Notice that the average occurrence frequency is much larger than 60 minutes).

Image-to-image translation is a long-established and a difficult problem in
computer vision. In this paper we propose an adversarial based model for
image-to-image translation. The regular deep neural-network based methods
perform the task of image-to-image translation by comparing gram matrices and
using image segmentation which requires human intervention. Our generative
adversarial network based model works on a conditional probability approach.
This approach makes the image translation independent of any local, global and
content or style features. In our approach we use a bidirectional
reconstruction model appended with the affine transform factor that helps in
conserving the content and photorealism as compared to other models. The
advantage of using such an approach is that the image-to-image translation is
semi-supervised, independant of image segmentation and inherits the properties
of generative adversarial networks tending to produce realistic. This method
has proven to produce better results than Multimodal Unsupervised
Image-to-image translation.

The present paper deals with a comprehensive survey of the methods and tools employed in mining the social media. It is divided into two sections, the first of which deals with the techniques for identifying different motifs in the text and the second of which deals with the extraction of useful information from text. It discusses the current trends in the field of data mining, such as natural language processing, deep learning and big data. The paper concludes with a discussion of some of the issues that must be addressed in the future in the field of social media mining.

Although deep models have greatly improved the accuracy and robustness of
image segmentation, obtaining segmentation results with highly accurate
boundaries and fine structures is still a challenging problem. In this paper,
we propose a simple yet powerful Boundary-Aware Segmentation Network (BASNet),
which comprises a predict-refine architecture and a hybrid loss, for highly
accurate image segmentation. The predict-refine architecture consists of a
densely supervised encoder-decoder network and a residual refinement module,
which are respectively used to predict and refine a segmentation probability
map. The hybrid loss is a combination of the binary cross entropy, structural
similarity and intersection-over-union losses, which guide the network to learn
three-level (ie, pixel-, patch- and map- level) heirarchy representations. We
evaluate our BASNet on two reverse tasks including salient object segmentation,
camouflaged object segmentation, showing that it achieves very competitive
performance with sharp segmentation boundaries. Importantly, BASNet runs at
over 70 fps on a single GPU which benefits many potential real applications.
Based on BASNet, we further developed two (close to) commercial applications:
AR COPY & PASTE, in which BASNet is integrated with augmented reality for
"COPYING" and "PASTING" real-world objects, and OBJECT CUT, which is a
web-based tool for automatic object background removal. Both applications have
already drawn huge amount of attention and have important real-world impacts.
The code and two applications will be publically available at:
https://github.com/NathanUA/BASNet.

In 1989, Godreche and Luck introduced the concept of local mixtures of
primitive Substitution rules along the example of the well-known Fibonacci
substitution and foreshadowed heuristic results on the topological entropy and
the spectral type of the diffraction measure of Associated point sets. In this
contribution, we Present a generalisation Of This concept by regarding the
so-called 'noble means families', each consisting of finitely many primitive
substitution rules that individually all define the same two-sided discrete
dynamical hull. We report about results in the randomised case on topological
entropy, ergodicity of the two-sided discrete hull, and the spectral type of
the diffraction measure of related point sets.

This research presents a novel approach to near real-time hippocampus segmentation using patch-based canonical neural networks. The proposed method achieves accurate results while maintaining efficient computation time, making it suitable for clinical applications. The study demonstrates that incorporating prior knowledge into the network architecture can improve performance and overcome challenges such as partial volume effects and image noise commonly encountered in medical imageing data. Overall, this work offers promising insights into the potential of deep learning techniques for reliable and rapid analysis of brain structures in neuroimageing studies.

Ιn thіs nоtе wе іntrоduсе dеsсеnt Wіlfs, whісh аrе gеnеrаlіzаtіоns tо аrbіtrаrу fіnіtе Сохеtеr grоuрs $W$ аnd thеіr аssосіаtеd rооt sуstеms $\Рhі$. Τhіs аllоws us nоt оnlу рrоvе rеsults аbоut hоw mаnу tіmеs еасh еlеmеnt арреаrs but аlsо gіvеs іnsіght іntо whаt hарреns whеn оnе mоvеs оutsіdе thеsе сhаmbеrs; nаmеlу іt shоws whу сеrtаіn раttеrns арреаr mоrе оftеn thеn оthеrs dо еvеn thоugh thеу hаvе nо sресіаl sіgnіfісаnсе whаtsоеvеr! Тhе рrооf rеlіеs hеаvіlу оff wоrk dоnе bу Rісhаrd Stаnlеу whо рrоvеd sіmіlаr stаtеmеnts rеgаrdіng реrmutаtіоns іnstеаd usіng dіffеrеnt mеthоds аltоgеthеr."

> In this paper, we propose a novel approach to reduce the simulation variance of a rectal cancer segmentation network. The model variance is the variation of the model predictions on the same input. It is a significant source of confusion in deep learning models and can lead to bad reliability in real-world systems. We propose a novel approach to minimize the model variance by adding a regularization term in the loss function. The regularization term is based on the Kullback-Leibler correlation between the model predictions and the ground truth. We show that our approach can greatly improve the model variance of a rectal cancer segmentation network. We therefore show that our methodology can reduce the efficiency of the network on the test set.
