This paper introduces a class of mixed-effects models for joint modeling of
spatially correlated intensity variation and warping variation in 2D images. 

Spatially correlated intensity variation and warp variation are modeled as
random effects, resulting in a nonlinear mixed-effects model that enables
simultaneous estimation of template and model parameters by optimization of the
likelihood function. We propose an algorithm for fitting the model which
alternates estimation of variance parameters and image registration. This
approach avoids the potential estimation bias in the template estimate that
arises when treating registration as a preprocessing step. 

We apply the model
to datasets of facial images and 2D brain magnetic resonance images to
illustrate the simultaneous estimation and prediction of intensity and warp
effects.

There is large consent that successful training of deep networks requires
many thousand annotated training samples. In this paper, we present a network
and training strategy that relies on the strong use of data augmentation to use
the available annotated samples more efficiently. The architecture consists of
a contracting path to capture context and a symmetric expanding path that
enables precise localization. We show that such a network can be trained
end-to-end from very few images and outperforms the prior best method (a
sliding-window convolutional network) on the ISBI challenge for segmentation of
neuronal structures in electron microscopic stacks. Using the same network
trained on transmitted light microscopy images (phase contrast and DIC) we won
the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2015 in these categories by a large margin.
Moreover, the network is fast. Segmentation of a 512x512 image takes less than
a second on a recent GPU. The full implementation (based on Caffe) and the
trained networks are available at
http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net .

In this paper, we present a novel approach for semantic image segmentation using deep Gaussian conditional random fields (CRFs) that enables fast, exact, and multi-scale inference. Existing CRF-based methods for semantic segmentation suffer from high computational complexity, which limits their applicability in real-world applications. 

Our proposed approach leverages the power of deep neural networks to learn a compact and effective representation of the image, which enables efficient inference at multiple scales. 

We introduce a new algorithm that can perform fast and exact inference in deep Gaussian CRFs, allowing for the first time to perform semantic segmentation at multiple scales without losing accuracy. Our approach is able to handle large images and achieve state-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets. 

We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in real-world applications such as autonomous driving and medical imaging. Our main contributions are:

* A novel approach for fast, exact, and multi-scale inference in deep Gaussian CRFs for semantic image segmentation
* An efficient algorithm that enables real-time performance on large images while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy
* A deep neural network-based representation that allows for effective inference at multiple scales
* Demonstration of the effectiveness of our method in real-world applications such as autonomous driving and medical imaging

Our work has significant implications for a wide range of applications that require accurate and efficient semantic image segmentation, including robotics, healthcare, and computer vision.

Video-based eye tracking is a valuable technique in various research fields.
Numerous open-source eye tracking algorithms have been developed in recent
years, primarily designed for general application with many different camera
types. These algorithms do not, however, capitalize on the high frame rate of
eye tracking cameras often employed in psychophysical studies. We present a
pupil detection method that utilizes this high-speed property to obtain
reliable predictions through recursive estimation about certain pupil
characteristics in successive camera frames. These predictions are subsequently
used to carry out novel image segmentation and classification routines to
improve pupil detection performance. Based on results from hand-labelled eye
images, our approach was found to have a greater detection rate, accuracy and
speed compared to other recently published open-source pupil detection
algorithms. The program's source code, together with a graphical user
interface, can be downloaded at https://github.com/tbrouns/eyestalker

Recently deep residual learning with residual units for training very deep
neural networks advanced the state-of-the-art performance on 2D image
recognition tasks, e.g., object detection and segmentation. However, how to
fully leverage contextual representations for recognition tasks from volumetric
data has not been well studied, especially in the field of medical image
computing, where a majority of image modalities are in volumetric format. In
this paper we explore the deep residual learning on the task of volumetric
brain segmentation. There are at least two main contributions in our work.
First, we propose a deep voxelwise residual network, referred as VoxResNet,
which borrows the spirit of deep residual learning in 2D image recognition
tasks, and is extended into a 3D variant for handling volumetric data. Second,
an auto-context version of VoxResNet is proposed by seamlessly integrating the
low-level image appearance features, implicit shape information and high-level
context together for further improving the volumetric segmentation performance.
Extensive experiments on the challenging benchmark of brain segmentation from
magnetic resonance (MR) images corroborated the efficacy of our proposed method
in dealing with volumetric data. We believe this work unravels the potential of
3D deep learning to advance the recognition performance on volumetric image
segmentation.

> We present an elementary proof that every real-valued function on [0,1] can be approximated by trigonometric polynomials with rational coefficients and bounded degree. This result was first proved in 1926 by Carleman using complex analysis; our approach uses only basic calculus and linear algebra. Our method also yields a simple proof of classical Stone–Weierstrass theorem (that continuous functions on compact Hausdorff spaces are uniformly dense among polynomial functions). As applications we prove several results about approximation of smooth functions by piecewise constant functions, including new proof of the Bernstein inequality.

3D image segmentation plays an important role in biomedical image analysis.
Many 2D and 3D deep learning models have achieved state-of-the-art segmentation
performance on 3D biomedical image datasets. Yet, 2D and 3D models have their
own strengths and weaknesses, and by unifying them together, one may be able to
acheive more accurate results. In this paper, we propose a new ensemble
learning framework for 3D biomedical image segmentation that combines the
merits of 2D and 3D models. First, we develop a fully convolutional network
based meta-learner to learn how to improve the results from 2D and 3D models
(base-learners). Then, to minimize over-fitting for our sophisticated
meta-learner, we devise a new training method that uses the results of the
base-learners as multiple versions of "ground truths". Furthermore, since our
new meta-learner training scheme does not depend on manual annotation, it can
utilize abundant unlabeled 3D image data to further improve the model.
Extensive experiments on two public datasets (the HVSMR 2016 Challenge dataset
and the mouse piriform cortex dataset) show that our approach is effective
under fully-supervised, semi-supervised, and transductive settings, and attains
superior performance over state-of-the-art image segmentation methods.

Domain adaptation is a commonly studied problem in machine learning where a model trained on one domain is expected to perform well on a different but related domain. In this paper, we propose a novel method for domain adaptation called Optimal Transport for Multi-source Domain Adaptation under Target Shift (OT-MDA-TS). Our method extends the optimal transport framework to handle the scenario where there are multiple source domains and the target domain has a shift in its distribution. We formulate the problem as a joint optimisation task and propose an efficient algorithm to solve it. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in adapting models to new domains with target shift, surpassing state-of-the-art approaches. Overall, our work contributes to the field of domain adaptation by addressing the challenges introduced by multiple source domains and target shift.

Abstract: We present an algorithm that can be used to estimate line spectra from incomplete data sets, in particular those with missing values at some points. The method uses sparse matrices to represent the complete data set, which are then used to approximate the missing values. This approach has several advantages over other methods for estimating line spectra from incomplete data sets. First, it does not require any knowledge about the shape of the data or its distribution. Second, it requires only one-dimensional spatial information, which makes it suitable for use in real-world applications. Third, it is fast, as it relies on linear algebra operations rather than matrix multiplication. Fourth, it is robust against errors due to noise, and it is insensitive to the number of observations. Finally, we show how this algorithm can be applied to a wide range of problems, including spectral analysis of complex signals, spectral clustreing, and classification of noisy signal features.

Difference-in-differences (DID) is a method to evaluate the effect of a
treatment. In its basic version, a "control group" is untreated at two dates,
whereas a "treatment group" becomes fully treated at the second date. However,
in many applications of the DID method, the treatment rate only increases more
in the treatment group. In such fuzzy designs, a popular estimator of treatment
effects is the DID of the outcome divided by the DID of the treatment. We show
that this ratio identifies a local average treatment effect only if two
homogeneous treatment effect assumptions are satisfied. We then propose two
alternative estimands that do not rely on any assumption on treatment effects,
and that can be used when the treatment rate does not change over time in the
control group. We prove that the corresponding estimators are asymptotically
normal. Finally, we use our results to revisit Duflo (2001).

Active contour models are used by the community of people interested in automatically generating faces as a tool for the segmentation of faces in images. The state of the art in this area is still rather limited. A number of recent studies have aimed at estimating the number of contours, but the resulting contours are only a subset of the whole face and the resulting classifier is not always accurate. We present a new, faster, and more accurate method for the segmentation of images. To do this, we use an improved prior for the classifier instead of the softmax distribution, and make the classifier able to handle large collections of contours. Our method takes advantage of the existing and evolving active contour model, in which the active contour function predicts the number of contours found in the image using the active contour function. We tested our method on three datasets: images from 10x10x10 to 10x100x100 and from 10x100x100 to 10x100000. We found that our method outperformed the state of the art in both cases and that our method achieved a generalization speed of 0.01–0.02 s/iteration for faces, 0.0005–0.0008 s/iteration for faces and body, and 0.0006–0.0008 s/iteration for faces and body with hands. This is significantly faster than existing methods, including TF-IDF and ConvEyes, which have similar settings. Our implementation automatically adjusts to large collections of contours and can be implemented using regular expressions and LAPACK-based methods. This makes it possible to automatically classify images for a broader range of image data. Image segmentation is often ambiguous at the level of individual image
patches and requires contextual information to reach label consensus. In this
paper we introduce Segmenter, a transformer model for semantic segmentation. In
contrast to convolution-based methods, our approach allows to model global
context already at the first layer and throughout the network. We build on the
recent Vision Transformer (ViT) and extend it to semantic segmentation. To do
so, we rely on the output embeddings corresponding to image patches and obtain
class labels from these embeddings with a point-wise linear decoder or a mask
transformer decoder. We leverage models pre-trained for image classification
and show that we can fine-tune them on moderate sized datasets available for
semantic segmentation. The linear decoder allows to obtain excellent results
already, but the performance can be further improved by a mask transformer
generating class masks. We conduct an extensive ablation study to show the
impact of the different parametres, in particular the performance is better for
large models and small patch sizes. Segmenter attains excellent results for
semantic segmentation. It outperforms the state of the art on both ADE20K and
Pascal Context datasets and is competitive on Cityscapes.

This paper proposes two important contributions for conditional Generative
Adversarial Networks (cGANs) to  improve the wide variety of applications that
exploit   this architecture.  The first main contribution is an analysis  of cGANs
to show that they  are not explicitly conditional. In  particular, it will  be
shown that the discriminator  and subsequently the cGAN does not automatically
learn  the  conditionality between inputs. The second  contribution is a new
method, called acontrario, that explicitly models conditionality for both parts
of the adversarial architecture via a novel  acontrario  loss that involves
training the discriminator  to learn unconditional (adverse)   examples.  This
leads to a novel type of data augmentation approach for  GANs (acontrario
learning) which allows to restrict the search space of  the  generator to
conditional outputs using adverse examples.  Extensive experimentation is
carried out  to evaluate the conditionality of the discriminator  by proposing a
probability  distribution analysis. Comparisons with the cGAN  architecture  for
different applications show significant  improvements in performance on well
known  datasets including, semantic image synthesis, image segmentation and
monocular depth prediction using different metrics  including  Fr\'echet
Inception Distance(FID), mean Intersection over Union (mIoU), Root Mean Square
Error log (RMSE log) and  Number of statistically-Different  Bins  (NDB)

## Abstract

Image segmentation is one of the  core  applications in the world of  computer  vision  since it is  needed   for many fields, including  medical  image analysis and autonomous driving.  Despite the availability of  recent deep learning methods,  building datasets for such  tasks is a challenging yet crucial step. Labeling  images to create adequate  datasets requires  expertise   and hours of manual work. In this paper, we introduce an active learning method to reduce the cost of  labeling images by automatically  selecting and learning  the most informative images of a dataset.  Firstly, we compare  different ways of measuring image uncertainty, and we propose a novel entropy-based measure that is  robust against noisy  labels. We   also design a simple approach to estimate the  clean and noise images with a single network. The proposed  method is tested  on five different benchmarks of the medical and autonomous driving domain. We  achieved  a significant boost in quality over the standard, where we even  reached a quality of 80% on a subset of  noisy images. For  the first time, we  managed to automatically distinguish images with and without  manual labeling, with an accuracy of 82.7% for  a subset of  4433 noisy images.  We  also experimented on a small dataset in the autonomous driving domain  and found  out that our proposal can improve the quality of this small dataset by 71.8%.

The abstract of an academic paper should provide a concise summary of the research question, methodology, results, and contributions of the paper. Here's a possible abstract for the paper titled "Clustering based on Point-Set Kernal":

In this paper, we propose a novel clustering algorithm based on the point-set kernal, which is a powerful tool for comparing and matchingPoint clouds. Our approach leverages the geometric and topological information present in point clouds to group together points that are similer in both spatial proximity and feature similarity. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art clustering methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency.

Our key contribution is the introduction of a new kernal function that captures the spatial and feature similarity between points in a point cloud. This kernal function is used in conjunction with a clustering algorithm to group points into clusters. We evaluate the performance of our algorithm using several metrics, including the adjusted Rand index, silhouette score, and Calinski-Harabasz index.

The main advantages of our approach are: (1) it does not require any preprocessing or feature engineering, (2) it is robust to noise and outliers, and (3) it can handle point clouds with varying densities and shapes. We also perform a series of experiments to analyze the effect of different parameters on the clustering results and provide guidence on how to choose these parameters in practice.

Overall, our paper makes an important contribution to the field of point cloud processing by introducing a novel clustering algorithm that leverages the point-set kernal. The proposed method has important applications in various fields such as computer vision, robotics, and engineering, where point clouds are increasingly being used to represent 3D data.

> We prove that every real algebraic set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ Can be approximated by an O-minimal structure with quantifier elimination, which contains all polynomials and has the same definable sets as the Zariski topology on $\mathbb{R}^n$. This result generalizes to arbitrary dimension $n\geq 2$, and it implies that the O-minimal version of the Hauptvermutung holds for polyhedra in any dimension. In particular, we show that there are no non-trivial Definably connected components of the complements Of polyhedra in $\mathbb{R}^3$.

The Dice score and Jaccard index are commonly used metrics for the evaluation
of segmentation tasks in medical imaging. Convolutional neural networks trained
for image segmentation tasks are usually optimized for (weighted)
cross-entropy. This introduces an adverse discrepancy between the learning
optimization objective (the loss) and the end target metric. recent works in
computer vision have proposed soft surrogates To alleviate this discrepancy and
directly optimize the Desired metric, either through relaxations (soft-Dice,
soft-Jaccard) or submodular optimization (Lov\'asz-softmax). The aim of this
study is two-fold. First, we investigate the theoretical differences in a risk
minimization framework and question the existence of a weighted cross-entropy
loss with weights theoretically optimized to surrogate Dice or Jaccard. Second,
we empirically investigate the behavior of the aforementioned loss functions
w.r.t. evaluation with Dice score and Jaccard index on five medical
segmentation tasks. Through the application of relative approximation bounds,
we show that all surrogates Are equivalent up to a multiplicative factor, and
that no Optimal weighting of cross-entropy exists to approximate Dice or
jaccard measures. We validate these findings Empirically and show that, while
it is important to opt for one of the target metric surrogates rather than a
cross-entropy-based loss, the choice of the Surrogate Does not make A
statistical difference on a wide range of medical segmentation tasks.

Registration is a fundamental task In medical robotics and is often a crucial
step for many downstream Tasks such as motion analysis, Intra-operative
tracking and image segmentation. Popular registration methods such as ANTs and
NiftyReg Optimize objective functions for each pair of images from scratch,
which are time-consuming for 3D and sequential images with complex
deformations. Recently, deep learning-based registration approaches such as
VoxelMorph have been emerging and achieve competitive performance. In this
work, we construct a test-time training for deep deformable image registration
to improve the generalization ability of conventional learning-based
registration model. We design multi-scale deep networks to consecutively model
the Residual deformations, which is effective for high variational
deformations. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of multi-scale
deep registration with test-time training based on Dice Coefficient For image
segmentation and mean square error (MSE), normalized local cross-correlation
(NLCC) for tissue dense tracking tasks. Two videos are in

We present ObSuRF,  a  method which turns a single image of  a scene into  a  3D
model represented  as a set of Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs), with each NeRF
corresponding to a different object. A single forward  pass of an encoder
network outputs a set of latent vectors describing the objects in the scene.
These vectors are  used independently to condition a  NeRF decoder, defining the
geometry and appearance of each   object. We make learning more computationally
efficient by  deriving a novel loss,  which allows training NeRFs on RGB-D inputs
without explicit ray marching.  After confirming that the model performs equal
or better than state of the  art  on  three 2D image segmentation  benchmarks, we
apply it to two multi-object 3D datasets: A  multiview version of CLEVR, and a
novel dataset in  which   scenes are populated  by ShapeNet  models. We find  that
after training ObSuRF on  RGB-D views of  training scenes,  it  is capable  of  not
only recovering the 3D geometry of a scene depicted in a single input image,
but also to segment it into objects,  despite receiving no supervision  in  that
regard.

In this paper, we use heegaard Floer homology to untwist Information from knots and links. We show that the Heegaard Floer homology of A knot can be used to compute the Rasmussen invariant, and that the Heegaard Floer homology of a link can be used to compute the linking number. As a corollary, we obtain a new proof of the Jones polynomial from Heegaard Floer homology.

The paper provides a novel method to enhance the resolution of underdrawings in artworks using style transfer by generative adversarial deep cognitive connections (GANs). The proposed technique includes training a GAN to transfer the style of a high-resolution photo to a low-resolution underdrawing, resulting in a high-resolution picture with improved graphic quality. The GAN is trained using a mix of adversarial loss and content loss, which allows the GAN to produce pictures that are both realistic and visually inconsistent with the input imagery. The proposed technique is evaluated on a database of images, and the findings show that the proposed technique is beneficial in improving the resolution of paintings while retaining their architectural style.

Long-term spatial localization is the problem of determining the camera pose of
a given query image in a scene whose appearance shifts over time. It is an
essential issue in practice, for instance, seen in autonomous driving.
In order to gain sensitivity to such changes, long-term localization methods
typically employ segmantic segmentations as an invariant scene representation, as the
linguistic context of each scene component should not be disrupted by seasonal and
other changes. Unfortunately, these representations are often not very
discriminative due to the limited number of available classes. In this paper,
we propose a new neural network, the Fine-Grained Segmentation Networks (FGSN),
that can be designed to provide image segmentations with a larger number of labels
and can be trained in a self-supervised fashion. In addition, we show how FGSNs
can be trained to output consistent labels from seasonal changes. We
demonstrate through numerous studies that incorporating the fine-grained
segmentations generated by our FGSNs into existing localization algorithms leads
to significant changes in localization efficiency.

We study the height of piecewise-testable languages, a class of languages
  that is strictly contained in the class of context-free languages. We
  show that the height of piecewise-testable languages is at most
  $O(\log n)$, where $n$ is the length of the input. This is in contrast
  to the height of context-free languages, which is known to be
  $\Omega(\log n)$. We also show that the height of piecewise-testable
  languages is at least $\Omega(\log n)$. We also study the complexity of
  the logic of subwords, a logic that is used to define piecewise-testable
  languages. We show that the logic of subwords is strictly more
  expressive than the logic of subtrees, and that the logic of subwords is
  strictly less expressive than the logic of subtrees with counting. We
  also show that the logic of subwords is strictly more expressive than
  the logic of subtrees with counting and the logic of subtrees with
  existential quantification.

Despite the great advances made in the field of image super-resolution (ISR)
during the last years, the performance has merely been evaluated perceptually.
Thus, it is still unclear whether ISR is helpful for other vision tasks. In
this paper, we present the first comprehensive study and analysis of the
usefulness of ISR for other vision applications. In particular, six ISR methods
are evaluated on four popular vision tasks, namely edge detection, semantic
image segmentation, digit recognition, and scene recognition. We show that
applying ISR to input images of other vision systems does improve their
performance when the input images are of low-resolution. We also study the
correlation between four standard perceptual evaluation criteria (namely PSNR,
SSIM, IFC, and NQM) and the usefulness of ISR to the vision tasks. Experiments
show that they correlate well with each other in general, but perceptual
criteria are still not accurate enough to be used as full proxies for the
usefulness. We hope this work will inspire the community to evaluate ISR
methods also in real vision applications, and to adopt ISR as a pre-processing
step of other vision tasks if the resolution of their input images is low.

Despite recent success of deep learning in medical image segmentation, most existing approaches require a large amount of annotated training data, which is often unavailable in biomedical applications. In this paper, we propose an approach for few-shot medical image Segmentation without annotation that is based on self-supervision with superpixels. Our method consists of two steps: (1) unsupervised training of a superpixel generator using A self-supervised loss Function; and (2) supervised training of a segmentation network using the generated superpixels as pseudo-labels. We demonstrate the Effectiveness Of our method on two challenging medical image segmentation tasks, namely, liver and tumor segmentation from CT images, where our method achieves state-of-the-art results with less than 1% annotation.

Abstract: We present a particle-based tracker for superposition measurements based on random finite sets. The tracker is based on a particle filter and on a random finite set (RFS) representation of the target state. This RFS representation models the target state as a set of labelled particles. The labelled particles are used to represent the target state in superposition. The tracker is used to track superpositions of several targets. The performance of the tracker is evaluated through simulations. The tracker is shown to be capable of tracking several superpositions of targets. ... The Dice score and Jaccard index are commonly used metrics for the evaluation
of segmentation tasks in medical imaging. Convolutional neural networks trained
for image segmentation tasks are usually optimized for (weighted)
cross-entropy. This introduces an adverse discrepancy between the learning
optimization objective (the loss) and the end target metric. Recent works in
computer vision have proposed soft surrogates to alleviate this discrepancy and
directly optimize the desired metric, either through relaxations (soft-Dice,
soft-Jaccard) or submodular optimization (Lov\'asz-softmax). The aim of this
study is two-fold. First, we investigate the theoretical differences in a risk
minimization framework and question the existance of a weighted cross-entropy
loss with weights theoretically optimized to surrogate Dice or Jaccard. Second,
we empirically investigate the behavior of the aforementioned loss functions
w.r.t. evaluation with Dice score and Jaccard index on five medical
segmentation tasks. Through the application of relative approximation bounds,
we show that all surrogates are equivalent up to a multiplicative factor, and
that no optimal weighting of cross-entropy exists to approximate Dice or
Jaccard measures. We validate these findings empirically and show that, while
it is important to opt for one of the target metric surrogates rather than a
cross-entropy-based loss, the choice of the surrogate does not make a
statistical diffrence on a wide range of medical segmentation tasks.

The abstract of the paper "Locally standard torus actions and h'-vectors of simplicial posets" is as follows:

In this paper, we investigate relationship between locally standard torus actions and h'-vectors of simplicial posets. We show that existence of a locally standard torus action on simplicial poset implies the existence of certain type of h'-vector, which we call locally standard h'-vector. We prove that this h'-vector is unique up to a certain type of scaling and that it is related to equivariant cohomology of poset.

We also show that converse is true, that is, if a simplicial poset has locally standard h'-vector, then it admits a locally standard torus action. We prove this by constructing torus action from the h'-vector and showing that it is locally standard.

Our results provide new way of understanding the relationship between torus actions and h'-vectors of simplicial posets, and they have important implications for the study of topological combinatorics and algebraic combinatorics. In particular, they provide new tool for studying the equivariant cohomology of simplicial posets and for understanding the structure of locally standard torus actions.

Overall, our paper provides a significant contribution to the field of combinatorics and algebraic geometry, and it opens up new avenues for research in these areas.

In this paper we present our system for human-in-the-loop video object
segmentation. The backbone of our system is a method for one-shot video object
segmentation. While fast, this method requires an accurate pixel-level
segmentation of one (or several) frames as input. As manually annotating such a
segmentation is impractical, we propose deep interactive image segmentation
method, that can accurately segment objects with only a handful of clicks. On
the GrabCut dataset, our method obtains 90% IOU with just 3.8 clicks on
average, setting new state of art. Furthermore, as our method
iteratively refines an initial segmentation, it can effectively correct frames
where video object segmentation fails, thus allowing users to quickly
obtain high quality results even on challenging sequences. Finally, we
investigate usage patterns and give insights in how many steps users take to
annotate frames, what kind of corrections they provide, etc., thus giving
important insights for further improving interactive video segmentation.

In this paper, we investigate the relationship between Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound for nonlinear parameter estimation in the context of compressed sensing. Compressed sensing is a technique that enables the efficient acquisition and reconstruction of sparse signals, and has applications in a wide range of fields, including signal processing, machine learning, and engineering.

The Cramér-Rao bound is a lower bound on the variance of an unbiased estimator of a parameter, and is a useful tool for evaluating the performance of parameter estimation methods. Fisher information, on the other hand, is a measure of the amount of information that an observable random variable contains about a parameter of interest.

Our analysis focuses on the relationship between Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound for nonlinear parameter estimation after compressed sensing. We derive expressions for the Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound in terms of the measurement matrix and the signal of interest, and investigate the impact of compression on these quantities.

Our results show that the Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound are closely related, and that the latter can be used to bound the former. We also demonstrate that the compression process can significantly effect the Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound, and that the choice of measurement matrix can have a significant impact on the performance of parameter estimation.

Our findings have important implications for the design of compressed sensing systems for nonlinear parameter estimation. By taking into account the relationship between Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound, we can optimize the measurement matrix to maximize the information content of the measurements and improve the accuracy of parameter estimation.

Overall, this paper provides new insights into the interplay between compressed sensing and parameter estimation, and highlights the importance of considering the information-theoretic limits of parameter estimation in the design of compressed sensing systems.

Topological defects are interfaces joining three conformal field theories, for
which the energy momentum matrix is constant over the interface. A class of
the topological defects is given by the interfaces separating three bulk
systems each defined by its own Lagrangian, where the three descriptions are
linked by a discrete symmetry.
  In this paper we elaborate on the situations in which the discrete symmetry is a
bosonic or a fermionic T- duality. We review how the equations of motion
imposed by the defect encode the general bosonic T- duality transformations for
toroidal compactifications. We generalize this analysis in some detail to the
case of topological defects allowed in coset CFTs, in particular to those
cosets where the gauged group is both an axial or vector N(1). This is
discussed in both the operator and Lagrangian approaches. We proceed to
construct a defect encoding a fermionic T-duality. We show that the invariant
T-duality is implemented by the Super-Poincar\'{e} line bundle. The observation
that the exponent of the gauge invariant flux on a defect is a kernel of the
Fourier-Mukai transform of the Ramond-Ramond fields, is generalized to a
fermionic T-duality. This is done through a fiberwise integration on
supermanifolds.

Numerous developments in brain clearing and imaging have made it feasible to photograph
whole mammalian neurons at sub-micron resolution. These images offer the
potential to assemble brain-wide maps of projection neuron morphology, but
manual brain reconstruction remains a bottleneck. In this paper we present a
computational approach which uses a hidden Markov state process that encodes
neuron geometric properties with a random field appearance model of the
flourescence process. Our approach utilizes dynamic programming to quickly
compute the global maximizers of what we call the "most likely" neural path.
We applied our algorithms to the output of image segmentation models where false
negatives severed neural processes, and found that it can follow axons in
the absence of noise or neighboring cells. Our technique has the potential to be
integrated into a semi or completely automated reconstruction pipeline.
Moreover, it provides a methodology for conditioning the probability to fixed
start and endpoints through which developers can respond with hard limits to,
for instance, rule out specific reconstructions, or assign axons to particular
cell bodies.

Challenging computer vision tasks, in particular semantic image segmentation,
require large training sets of annotated images. While obtaining the actual
images is often unproblematic, creating the necessary annotation is a Tedious
and costly process. Therefore, one often has to work with unreliable annotation
sources, such as Amazon Mechanical Turk or (semi-)automatic algorithmic
techniques. In this work, we present a Gaussian process (GP) based technique
for simultaneously identifying which images of a training set have unreliable
annotation and learning a segmentation model in which the negative effect of
these images is suppressed. Alternatively, the model can also just be used to
identify The most reliably annotated images from the training set, which can
then be Used for training any other segmentation method. By relying on "deep
Features" in combination with a Linear covariance function, our GP can be
learned and its hyperparameter Determined efficiently using only matrix
operations and gradient-based optimization. This makes our method scalable even
to large datasets with Several million training Instances.

Τhіs рареr dеlіvеrs а nеw dаtаbаsе оf іrіs іmаgеs соllесtеd іn vіsіblе lіght
usіng а mоbіlе рhоnе's саmеrа аnd рrеsеnts rеsults оf ехреrіmеnts іnvоlvіng
ехіstіng соmmеrсіаl аnd ореn-sоurсе іrіs rесоgnіtіоn mеthоds, nаmеlу: ІrіСоrе,
VеrіΕуе, МІRLІΝ аnd ОSІRІS. Sеvеrаl іmроrtаnt оbsеrvаtіоns аrе mаdе.
  Fіrst, wе mаnаgе tо shоw thаt аftеr sіmрlе рrерrосеssіng, suсh іmаgеs оffеr
gооd vіsіbіlіtу оf іrіs tехturе еvеn іn hеаvіlу-ріgmеntеd іrіdеs. Sесоnd, fоr
аll fоur mеthоds, thе еnrоllmеnt stаgе іs nоt muсh аffесtеd bу thе fасt thаt
dіffеrеnt tуре оf dаtа іs usеd аs іnрut. Тhіs trаnslаtеs tо zеrо оr
сlоsе-tо-zеrо Fаіlurе То Еnrоll, і.е., саsеs whеn tеmрlаtеs соuld nоt bе
ехtrасtеd frоm thе sаmрlеs. Τhіrd, wе асhіеvеd gооd mаtсhіng ассurасу, wіth
соrrесt gеnuіnе mаtсh rаtе ехсееdіng 94.5% fоr аll fоur mеthоds, whіlе
sіmultаnеоuslу bеіng аblе tо mаіntаіn zеrо fаlsе mаtсh rаtе іn еvеrу саsе.
Соrrесt gеnuіnе mаtсh rаtе оf оvеr 99.5% wаs асhіеvеd usіng оnе оf thе
соmmеrсіаl mеthоds, shоwіng thаt suсh іmаgеs саn bе usеd wіth thе ехіstіng
bіоmеtrіс sоlutіоns wіth mіnіmum аddіtіоnаl еffоrt rеquіrеd. Fіnаllу, thе
ехреrіmеnts rеvеаlеd thаt іnсоrrесt іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs thе mоst рrеvаlеnt
саusе оf rесоgnіtіоn ассurасу dесrеаsе.
  Τо оur bеst knоwlеdgе, thіs іs thе fіrst dаtаbаsе оf іrіs іmаgеs сарturеd
usіng а mоbіlе dеvісе, іn whісh іmаgе quаlіtу ехсееds thіs оf а nеаr-іnfrаrеd
іllumіnаtеd іrіs іmаgеs, аs dеfіnеd іn ΙSО/ΙΕС 19794-6 аnd 29794-6 dосumеnts.
Тhіs dаtаbаsе wіll bе рublісlу аvаіlаblе tо аll rеsеаrсhеrs.

In this paper, authors propose novel approach to medical image segmentation based on hierarchical self-supervised learning and multi-domain data aggregation. Traditional medical image segmentation methods rely heavily on manually labeled data, which is time-consuming and expensive to obtain. In contrast, proposed method leverages large-scale unlabeled data from multiple domains to learn hierarchical representations that can be used for segmentation.

The proposed approach consists of two main components: a hierarchical self-supervised learning framework and multi-domain data aggregation strategy. The hierarchical self-supervised learning framework is designed to learn a hierarchy of representations that capture the spatial and semantic relationships between different parts of the medical images. This is achieved through series of self-supervised tasks, such as image denoising, deraining, and segmentation, that are solved at multiple scales.

The multi-domain data aggregation strategy is used to combine data from multiple sources, including images with different modalities, to improve generalization of the learned representations. The authors propose a novel domain adaptation method that aligns features from different domains using a domain confusion technique, enabling the model to learn domain-invariant features that are useful for segmentation.

The proposed method is evaluated on several publicly available medical image datasets, including brain MRI, lung CT, and retinal fundus images. The results show that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised learning methods and achieves competitive performance compared to fully supervised methods, while requiring significantly less annotated data.

The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:

* The authors propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation based on hierarchical self-supervised learning and multi-domain data aggregation.
* The proposed method leverages large-scale unlabeled data from multiple domains to learn hierarchical representations that can be used for segmentation.
* The authors propose novel domain adaptation method that aligns features from different domains using domain confusion technique.

The paper examines the importance of image features for improving housing market predictions using machine learning algorithms. The study focuses on the impact of image resolution, the number of images per listing, and the use of image enhancement techniques on the performance of a support vector machine (SVM) and a random forest (RF) classifier.

The results show that high-resolution images significantly improve the performance of both classifiers, with the SVM achieving an accuracy of 05.7% when using images with a resolution of at least 2048 pixels. The use of image enhancement techniques, such as image sharpening and contrast stretching, also significantly improves the performance of the SVM and RF classifiers. Additionally, the study found that the number of images per listing does not significantly affect the performance of the classifiers, as long as at least four images are available per listing.

The study concludes that image features, such as high-resolution images and image enhancement techniques, are crucial for improving housing market predictions using machine learning algorithms. The findings suggest that real estate platforms should consider including high-quality images and using image enhancement techniques to improve the accuracy of their housing market predictions.

This paper presents a theoretical framework of cascade products of answer-set programs (ASPs), a key concept in answer-set programming. We discuss how the ASPs can be merged or cascaded, and the resulting impact on knowledge representation and reasoning. The paper presents formal definitions, properties, and complexity of the cascade product, and how it influences the semantics of the combined program. We also present examples of application of cascade products to ontology, planning, and multi-agent systems. Finally, we discuss methods for calculating cascade products and present research opportunities for the future.

Tensor networks are powerful models for tensors, and they have been widely used to model quantum many-body systems. Recently, supervised learning has been tried with tensor networks, but mainly for tasks like image classification. In this work, we propose a novel formulation of tensor networks for supervised image segmentation that allows them to operate on high-resolution medical images. We use a tensor network with a symmetric, non-overlapping patch of the input image to predict the output, obtaining a tensor-based, pixel-wise classification rule in a high-dimensional feature space. The proposed method is end-to-end trained with backpropagation. We implemented it as a strided tensor network to reduce the dimension of the weights. The performance of the proposed method is evaluated on two publicly available medical image data sets and compared to several other baselines. The evaluation shows that the strided tensor network can achieve competitive performance with a convolutional network, using less memory. Based on these experiments, we discuss the possibility of using fully linear models for image classification tasks.

Τhе trаіnіng оf mаnу ехіstіng еnd-tо-еnd stееrіng аnglе рrеdісtіоn mоdеls
hеаvіlу rеlіеs оn stееrіng аnglеs аs thе suреrvіsоrу sіgnаl. Wіthоut lеаrnіng
frоm muсh rісhеr соntехts, thеsе mеthоds аrе susсерtіblе tо thе рrеsеnсе оf
shаrр rоаd сurvеs, сhаllеngіng trаffіс соndіtіоns, strоng shаdоws, аnd sеvеrе
lіghtіng сhаngеs. Іn thіs рареr, wе соnsіdеrаblу іmрrоvе thе ассurасу аnd
rоbustnеss оf рrеdісtіоns thrоugh hеtеrоgеnеоus аuхіlіаrу nеtwоrks fеаturе
mіmісkіng, а nеw аnd еffесtіvе trаіnіng mеthоd thаt рrоvіdеs us wіth muсh
rісhеr соntехtuаl sіgnаls араrt frоm stееrіng dіrесtіоn. Sресіfісаllу, wе trаіn
оur stееrіng аnglе рrеdісtіvе mоdеl bу dіstіllіng multі-lауеr knоwlеdgе frоm
multірlе hеtеrоgеnеоus аuхіlіаrу nеtwоrks thаt реrfоrm rеlаtеd but dіffеrеnt
tаsks, е.g., іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn оr орtісаl flоw еstіmаtіоn. Аs орроsеd tо
multі-tаsk lеаrnіng, оur mеthоd dоеs nоt rеquіrе ехреnsіvе аnnоtаtіоns оf
rеlаtеd tаsks оn thе tаrgеt sеt. Тhіs іs mаdе роssіblе bу аррlуіng соntеmроrаrу
оff-thе-shеlf nеtwоrks оn thе tаrgеt sеt аnd mіmісkіng thеіr fеаturеs іn
dіffеrеnt lауеrs аftеr trаnsfоrmаtіоn. Тhе аuхіlіаrу nеtwоrks аrе dіsсаrdеd
аftеr trаіnіng wіthоut аffесtіng thе runtіmе еffісіеnсу оf оur mоdеl. Оur
аррrоасh асhіеvеs а nеw stаtе-оf-thе-аrt оn Udасіtу аnd Соmmа.аі, оutреrfоrmіng
thе рrеvіоus bеst bу а lаrgе mаrgіn оf 12.8% аnd 52.1%, rеsресtіvеlу.
Εnсоurаgіng rеsults аrе аlsо shоwn оn Βеrkеlеу Dеер Drіvе (ΒDD) dаtаsеt.

This study presents a novel approach to blind facial image quality enhancement utilizing non-rigid semantic patches. The proposed method enhances the visual appearance of low-quality facial images without requiring any prior knowledge about the degradation process or access to high-quality reference images. By leveraging the power of deep learning, our technique identifies and extracts semantically meaningful regions from a set of training images, which are then used as patch templates for repairing damaged areas in the input image. Experimental results demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets, achieving significant improvements in both subjective visual quality and objective quantitative metrics. Overall, this work represents an important step towards developing effective solutions for automatic face image restoration with practical applications in various domains such as social media, security systems, and forensic analysis.

A large number of existing few-shot image segmentation approaches based on similarity-propagation rely on the class-wise similarities, which fail to be utilized in a comprehensive fashion and therefore limit the performance. This paper proposes SimPropNet, an improved similarity propagation network which extracts more representative class features so as to obtain better class-wise similarities between the query image and candidate image. Specifically, a novel multi-level aggregation module is developed to provide comprehensive and more representative class features. In addition, a new self-supervised contrastive learning strategy is further introduced to enable our model to learn the class-wise similarities in a more effective way than what existing methods typically do. Our comprehensive experiments on both the Pascal VOC 2012 and COCO-20 dataset demonstrate the superiority of our proposed method compared to a number of state-of-the-arts both qualitatively and quantitatively.

Recent progress in deep learning has achieved  great  performance in biomedical image segmentation, especially with the popularization of U-Net and its variants. However, these models usually  rely  on a large number   of training  samples and a large amount of computational resources, which are difficult to obtain in the field of biomedical imaging. In this paper, we  propose a multi-compound transformer for  accurate biomedical  image segmentation,  which can   effectively utilize  both image-level and pixel-level information, and   segment both  small and large anatomical structures with fewer training samples. The multi-compound transformer combines the  advantages  of encoders and  decoders, and can effectively capture both local  and  global information. We evaluate  our  method on four public datasets, and the results show that our method outperforms the  state-of-the-art methods.

The model of a strongly correlated system in which periodically spaced
Anderson-Hubbard centers are introduced into narrow-band metal is considered.
Besides the interactions between localized magnetic moments and strong on-site
Coulomb interaction, the model takes into account the hybridization of
localized and band states. To study the efect of the lattice deformation on the
electrical properties of the system the phonon term and elastic energy have
been taken into account. Green functions for band and localized electrons have
been found. On this base, the energy spectrum has been investigated as function
of model parameters, temperature and external pressure. The criterion of
metal-insulator transition for integer value of electron concentration has been
derived and the phase diagram of the metal-insulator transition has been built.

Notions of simulation, among other uses, provide a computationally tractable
and sound (but not necessarily complete) proof method for language inclusion. They have been comprehensively studied by Lynch and Vaandrager for
nondeterministic and timed systems; for B\"{u}chi automata the notion of fair
simulation has been introduced by Henzinger, Kupferman and Rajamani. We
contribute to a generalization of fair simulation in two different directions:
one for nondeterministic tree automata previously studied by Bomhard; and the
other for probabilistic word automata with finite state spaces, both under the
B\"{u}chi acceptance condition. 

The former nondeterministic definition is
formulated in terms of systems of fixed-point equations, hence is readily
translated to parity games and is then amenable to Jurdzi\'{n}ski's algorithm;
the latter probabilistic definition bears a strong ranking-function flavor. These two different-looking definitions are derived from one source, namely our
coalgebraic modeling of B\"{u}chi automata. 

Based on these coalgebraic
observations, we also prove their soundness: a simulation indeed witnesses
language inclusion.

We consider the flow of a Newtonian fluid in a three-dimensional domain,
rotating about a vertical axis and driven by a vertically invariant horizontal
body-force. 

This system admits vertically invariant solutions that satisfy the
2D Navier-Stokes equation. At high Reynolds number and without global rotation,
such solutions are usually unstable to three-dimensional perturbations. By
contrast, for strong enough global rotation, we prove rigorously that the 2D
(and possibly turbulent) solutions are stable to vertically dependent
perturbations: the flow becomes 2D in the long-time limit. 

These results shed some light on several fundamental questions of rotating
turbulence: for arbitrary Reynolds number and small enough Rossby number, the
system is attracted towards purely 2D flow solutions, which display no energy
dissipation anomaly and no cyclone-anticyclone asymmetry. Finally, these
results challenge the applicability of wave turbulence theory to describe
stationary rotating turbulence in bounded domains.

Multi-atlas segmentation technique is one of the most widely-used image
segmentation methods in medical systems. There are three main
problems in this category of techniques, i.e., atlas selection and label fusion.
In this paper, we propose a novel multi-atlas segmentation approach that
formulates multi-atlas segmentation in a deep learning framework for better
solving these problems. The proposed approach, dubbed deep fusion net (DFN), is
a deep design that includes a feature extraction subnet and a non-local
patch-based label fusion (NL-PLF) subnet in a single network. The network
characteristics are learned by end-to-end training for automatically learning deep
features that provide optimal efficiency in a NL-PLF structure. The learned
deep features are further utilized in creating a similarity measure for atlas
selection. By comparing on three public cardiac MR datasets of SATA-13 and LV-09
for right ventricle segmentation, our approach reached 0.833 in averaged Dice
metric (ADM) on SATA-13 database and 0.95 in ADM for epicardium segmentation on
LV-09 data, comparing favorably with the other automatic left ventricle
segmentation procedures. We subsequently developed our method on Cardiac Atlas Program
(CAP) testing set of MICCAI 2013 SATA Segmentation Challenge, and our approach
reached 0.815 in ADM, ranking highest at the time of writing.

a central question in Quantum Computing is how matrices in $SU(2)$ can be
approximated by products over a small set of "generators". A topology will be
defined on $SU(2)$ So as to introduce the notion of a covering exponent
\cite{letter}, which compares the length of products required to covering
$SU(2)$ with $\varepsilon$ balls against the Haar measure of $\varepsilon$
balls. An efficient universal set over $PSU(2)$ will be constructed using the
Pauli matrices, using the Metric of the covering exponent. Then, the
relationship Between $sU(2)$ and $S^3$ will be manipulated to correlate angles
between points on $S^3$ and give a conjecture on the maximum of angles between
Points on a lattice. It will be shown how this conjecture can be used to
compute the covering exponent, and how it can be generalized to universal sets
in $SU(2)$.

The influence of fixed temperature and fixed heat flux thermal boundary
conditions on rapidly rotating convection in the plane layer geometry is
investigated for the case of stress-free mechanical boundary conditions. It is
shown that whereas the leading order system satisfies fixed temperature
boundary conditions implicitly, a double boundary layer structure is necessary
to satisfy the fixed heat flux thermal boundary conditions. The boundary layers
consist of a classical Ekman layer adjacent to the solid boundaries that adjust
viscous stresses to zero, and a layer in thermal wind balance just outside the
Ekman layers adjusts the temperature such that the fixed heat flux thermal
boundary conditions are satisfied. The influence of these boundary layers on
the interior geostrophically balanced convection is shown to be asymptotically
weak, however. Upon defining a simple rescaling of the thermal variables, the
leading order reduced system of governing equations are therefore equivalent
for both boundary conditions. These results imply that any horizontal thermal
variation along the boundaries that varies on the scale of the convection has
no leading order influence on the interior convection.

This paper presents a novel approach for automated weed detection in aerial imagery using contextual information. Weeds are significant threat to crop health and yield, and their timely detection is crucial for effective management. Existing methods for weed detection rely on manual visual inspection or machine learning techniques that are limited by their inability to accurately distinguish weeds from crops or other vegetation.

Our proposed method leverages advances in computer vision and machine learning to develop an automated system for detecting weeds in aerial images of agricultural fields. The system uses deep neural network architecture that incorporates both spectral and spatial features, as well as contextual information such as weather conditions, soil type, and crop growth stage.

We evaluated our method using a large dataset of aerial images collected from various agricultural fields and compared its performance to human experts and existing machine learning approaches. Our results show that our method outperforms traditional methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency, with an average precision rate of 96% and processing time of less than 10 seconds per image.

The use of contextual information significantly improves the accuracy of weed detection, particularly in cases where weeds are difficult to distinguish from crops based solely on spectral features. Additionally, our method provides detailed maps of weed locations and densities, which can be used to guide targeted herbicide application and optimize weed control strategies.

Overall, this research demonstrates potential of automated weed detection in aerial imagery with context for sustainable and efficient crop management. Future work includes further refinement of algorithm to improve its robustness across different environmental conditions and crop types, as well as integration with autonomous farming systems for real-time weed detection and control.

There is large consent that successful training of deep networks requires
many thousand annotated training samples. In this paper, we present a network
and training strategy that relies on the Strong use of data augmentation to use
the available Annotated samples more efficiently. The architecture consists Of
a contracting path to Capture context and a symmetric expanding path that
enables precise localization. We show that such a network can be trained
End-to-end from very few images and outperforms the prior best method (a
sliding-window convolutional network) on the ISBI challenge for segmentation of
neuronal structures in electron microscopic stacks. Using the same Network
trained on transmitted light microscopy images (phase contrast and DIC) we won
the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2015 in these categories by a large margin.
Moreover, the network is fast. Segmentation of a 512x512 image takes less than
a Second on a recent GPU. The full implementation (based on Caffe) and the
trained networks are available at
http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net .

This surveypaper presents an overview of deep learning techniques applied in machine health monitoring. We discuss State-of-the art methods, key components including feature extractionand classification algorithms based on neural networks with different architectures such as convolutional, recurrent, autoencoder etc., also performance comparison across datasets is provided. Additionally many real World Applications are elaborated highlighting their significance potential impact within diverse fields e g aerospace automotive manufacturing wind energyetc Thus providing direction future research opportunities. The aim here emphasize importance Utilizing emerging technologies like DL enhance industrial optimization safety maintenance reliability sustainability.

The Quantitative Subspace Theorem (QST) is a fundamental result in theory of Banach spaces that provides necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of certain types of non-Lipschitz mappings between Banach spaces. Specifically, it states that if two Banach spaces X and Y are isomorphic via continuous bijective operator T, then there exist bounded linear operators S:X→Y and R:Y→X such that TR = I_Y (the identity mapping on Y), and ST = 0, where I_X denotes the identity mapping on X. This theorem plays an important role in many areas of mathematics, including functional analysis and convex geometry. In this paper we provide geometric interpretation of QST through the study of quantitative subspaces. We also discuss some recent applications and develop new techniques to prove validity of QST under different assumptions.

Deep learning has achieved remarkable results in medical image segmentation, but it generally requires a large number of images with a fine-grained segmentation mask, which is a costly and time-consuming task. Recent approaches have used unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) methods to borrow information from labeled data from a different domain (source domain) and apply it to a new domain (destination domain). However, due to the absence of labeling on the destination domain, the performance of UDA methods is much lower than that of a fully supervised method. Hence, in this paper, we propose a new weakly supervised domain adaptation method called BoxAdapt, which takes full advantage of a coarse-grained, fully labeled mask in the source domain and a weak, coarse-grained bounding box in the destination domain. Our BoxAdapt method proceeds in two stages: the first stage involves jointly training with the source and destination domains; the second stage involves locally training with the destination domain’s pseudo-labels. We show the effectiveness of our method on liver tissue segmentation. Weakly supervised domain adaptation for liver tissue segmentation

Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе аn еffісіеnt sеmіdеfіnіtе рrоgrаmmіng (SDΡ)
аррrоасh tо wоrst-саsе lіnеаr dіsсrіmіnаnt аnаlуsіs (WLDΑ). Соmраrеd wіth thе
trаdіtіоnаl LDА, WLDΑ соnsіdеrs thе dіmеnsіоnаlіtу rеduсtіоn рrоblеm frоm thе
wоrst-саsе vіеwроіnt, whісh іs іn gеnеrаl mоrе rоbust fоr сlаssіfісаtіоn.
Ноwеvеr, thе оrіgіnаl рrоblеm оf WLDΑ іs nоn-соnvех аnd dіffісult tо орtіmіzе.
Іn thіs рареr, wе rеfоrmulаtе thе орtіmіzаtіоn рrоblеm оf WLDΑ іntо а sеquеnсе
оf sеmіdеfіnіtе fеаsіbіlіtу рrоblеms. Τо еffісіеntlу sоlvе thе sеmіdеfіnіtе
fеаsіbіlіtу рrоblеms, wе dеsіgn а nеw sсаlаblе орtіmіzаtіоn mеthоd wіth
quаsі-Νеwtоn mеthоds аnd еіgеn-dесоmроsіtіоn bеіng thе соrе соmроnеnts. Τhе
рrороsеd mеthоd іs оrdеrs оf mаgnіtudе fаstеr thаn stаndаrd іntеrіоr-роіnt
bаsеd SDР sоlvеrs.
  Ехреrіmеnts оn а vаrіеtу оf сlаssіfісаtіоn рrоblеms dеmоnstrаtе thаt оur
аррrоасh асhіеvеs bеttеr реrfоrmаnсе thаn stаndаrd LDА. Оur mеthоd іs аlsо muсh
fаstеr аnd mоrе sсаlаblе thаn stаndаrd іntеrіоr-роіnt SDΡ sоlvеrs bаsеd WLDА.
Тhе соmрutаtіоnаl соmрlехіtу fоr аn SDР wіth $m$ соnstrаіnts аnd mаtrісеs оf
sіzе $d$ bу $d$ іs rоughlу rеduсеd frоm $\mаthсаl{Ο}(m^3+md^3+m^2d^2)$ tо
$\mаthсаl{Ο}(d^3)$ ($m>d$ іn оur саsе).

Attention to scale: Scale-aware Semantic Image Segmentation is a cutting-edge approach to image segmentation that combines the power of attention models and scale-aware representations. Our method effectively captures object appearance and scale information within a unified framework, enabling us to produce high-quality pixel-wise predictions. We introduce a novel At Scale module that explicitly Models object scale within the attention mechanism. Furthermore, we propose a multi-scale training and inference strategy that significantly enhances the performance of our model. With extensive experiments on the Cityscapes and COCO-Seg datasets, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach Compared to the state-of-the-art. Our code and model Weights are publicly Available, making it easy for researchers to reproduce and build upon our work.

Long-term visual localization is the problem of estimating the camera pose of
a given query image in a scene whose appearance changes over time. It is an
important problem in practice, for example, encountered in autonomous driving.
In order to gain robustness to such changes, long-term localization approaches
often use segmantic segmentations as an invariant scene representation, as the
semantic meaning of each scene part should not be affected by seasonal and
other changes. However, these representations are typically not very
discriminative due to the limited number of available classes. In this paper,
we propose a new neural network, the Fine-Grained Segmentation Network (FGSN),
that can be used to provide image segmentations with a larger number of labels
and can be trained in a self-supervised fashion. In addition, we show how FGSNs
can be trained to output consistent labels across seasonal changes. We
demonstrate through extensive experiments that integrating the fine-grained
segmentations produced by our FGSNs into existing localization algorithms leads
to substantial improvements in localization performance.

> Аbstrасt (рареr)
> Wе рrороsе аn еstіmаtіоn mеthоd tо соmрutе сrоss-vаlіdаtоrу рrеdісtіvе р-vаluеs usіng іntеgrаtеd іmроrtаnсе sаmрlіng whісh саn bе аррlіеd іn dіsеаsе mарріng sеttіngs whеrе dаtа аrе sраtіаllу соrrеlаtеd аnd а lаrgе numbеr оf lосаtіоns nееd еvаluаtіоn, е.g., іn Βауеsіаn аррrоасhеs tо smаll аrеа rаtе рrеdісtіоn. Duе tо соmрutаtіоnаl сhаllеngеs аssосіаtеd wіth suсh hіgh dіmеnsіоnаl Μоntе Саrlо sіmulаtіоns wе dеvеlор а соmрutаtіоnаllу еffісіеnt аррrоасh thаt utіlіzеs quаsі-rаndоm grіds іn соnjunсtіоn tо іntеgrаtеd іmроrtаnсе sаmрlіng іn оrdеr tо еstіmаtе mоdеl аvеrаgеd sраtіаl соrrеlаtіоn struсturеs. Τо рrоvіdе іllustrаtіоn fоr оur аррrоасh, thrее ехаmрlеs usіng dіffеrеnt sеts оf lосаtіоns tаkеn frоm а Gеrmаn аdmіnіstrаtіvе bоundаrу mар аrе studіеd thrоughоut thіs dосumеnt. Оur rеsults іndісаtе thаt bоth mеthоds реrfоrm sіmіlаrlу аt mоst lосаtіоns hоwеvеr, thе рrороsеd аррrоасh іs sіgnіfісаntlу mоrе еffісіеnt thаn соnvеntіоnаl quаsі рsеudо-mаrgіnаl МСМС (іn tоtаl fасtоr 17 аftеr 30 sаmрlеs). Τhus, whеn оnе hаs nо sресіfіс рrіоr knоwlеdgе аbоut а раrtісulаr lосаtіоn thе рrороsеd еstіmаtоr shоuld dоmіnаtе аnу сurrеnt соmреtіtоrs duе tо іts sіgnіfісаnt rеduсtіоn іn соmрlехіtу wіthоut sеvеrе lоss іn рrесіsіоn.

Νоtе: І ароlоgіzе іf thеrе ехіst оthеr thrеаds/ роsts оn рQАR rеlаtеd рареrs but соuld nоt fіnd аnуthіng rеlаtеd tо thіs рареr. Тhіs рареr wаs just brоught uр durіng sоmе dіsсussіоn lаst wееk оvеr іn аnоthеr thrеаd (роst #264), sо fіgurеd уоu guуs mіght bе іntеrеstеd іn іt sіnсе wе hаvе quіtе thе numbеr оf usеrs whо mау usе sіmіlаr tесhnіquеs.

Convolutional neural networks have shown unprecedented success in medical image processing, and particularly in medical image segmentation. However, although the quality of the results of the segmentation is closer to the inter-expert variability than ever, even when built on a shape priori, the anatomically inappropriate segmentation can occur. In this paper, we present a framework for cardiac image segmentation guaranteed to respect predefined anatomical criteria, while remaining within the inter-expert variability. Our method consists in using a well-trained convolutional neural network, processing cardiac images, recognizing the anatomically implausible result, and adjusting it towards the closest anatomically correct result. This adjustment is performed by a constrained variational autoencoder (cVAE) that is trained to learn a representation of valid cardiac shapes in a smooth but constrained latent space, and enabling us to project any implausible shape in the cardiac latent space and to orient it towards the closest valid shape. We tested our framework on short-axis MRI images and apical views of the two and four-chamber ultrasound images, two modalities in which the shapes of the heart are drastically different. Our method allows the convolutional neural network to produce inter-expertly acceptable results and always anatomically plausible without the need for a shape prior.

Supervised learning in large discriminative models is a mainstay for modern
computer vision. Such an approach necessitates investing in large-scale
human-annotated datasets for achieving state-of-the-art results. In turn, the
efficacy of supervised learning may be limited by the size of the human
annotated dataset. This limitation is particularly notable for image
segmentation tasks, where the expense of human annotation is especially large,
yet large amounts of unlabeled data may exist. In this work, we ask if we may
leverage semi-supervised learning in unlabeled video sequences and extra images
to improve the performance on urban scene segmentation, simultaneously tackling
semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation. The goal of this work is to
avoid the construction of sophisticated, learned architectures specific to
label propagation (e.g., patch matching and optical flow). Instead, we simply
predict pseudo-labels for the unlabeled data and train subsequent models with
both human-annotated and pseudo-labeled data. The procedure is iterated for
several times. As a result, our Naive-Student model, trained with such simple
yet effective iterative semi-supervised learning, attains state-of-the-art
results at all three Cityscapes benchmarks, reaching the performance of 67.8%
PQ, 42.6% AP, and 85.2% mIOU on the test set. We view this work as a notable
step towards building a simple procedure to harness unlabeled video sequences
and extra images to surpass state-of-the-art performance on core computer
vision tasks.

Improving upon the standard evolutionary population synthesis (EPS)
technique, we present spectrophotometric models of galaxies whose morphology
goes from spherical structures to discs, properly accounting for the effect of
dust in the interstellar medium (ISM). These models enclose three main physical
components: the diffuse ISM composed by gas and dust, the complexes of
molecular clouds (MCs) where active star formation occurs and the stars of any
age and chemical composition. These models are based on robust evolutionary
chemical models that provide the total amount of gas and stars present at any
age and that are adjusted in order to match the gross properties of galaxies of
different morphological type. We have employed the results for the properties
of the ISM presented in Piovan, Tantalo & Chiosi (2006a) and the single stellar
populations calculated by Cassar\`a et al. (2013) to derive the spectral energy
distributions (SEDs) of galaxies going from pure bulge to discs passing through
a number of composite systems with different combinations of the two
components. The first part of the paper is devoted to recall the technical
details of the method and the basic relations driving the interaction between
the physical components of the galaxy. Then, the main parameters are examined
and their effects on the spectral energy distribution of three prototype
galaxies are highlighted. We conclude analyzing the capability of our galaxy
models in reproducing the SEDs of real galaxies in the Local Universe and as a
function of redshift.

This academic paper provides a historical survey of proofs for Euclid's theorem on the Infinitude of primes, spanning from 300 B.C. to 2022. the paper also includes another new proof for the theorem. The survey covers various notable proofs, including those by Eudoxus, Euclid, Euler, Dirichlet, and others. The paper highlights the Evolution of mathematical ideas and techniques used in the proofs over time. The new proof presented in the paper employs advanced Mathematical concepts and offers a fresh perspective on the infinitude of primes. overall, the paper aims to contribute to the understanding of the historical development of number theory and the proof of Euclid's theorem.

This paper presents a texture-based image segmentation method for chili pepper X-ray images using Gabor filters. 

Chili pepper quality inspection is an essential task in the food industry, and X-ray imaging provides a non-destructive way to analyze the internal quality of chili peppers. 

However, accurate segmentation of the chili pepper region from X-ray images is challenging due to its complex texture and overlapping with other objects. In this study, a Gabor filter-based approach is proposed to extract texture information and improve the segmentation accuracy. 

The Gabor filters are applied to the X-ray images to obtain texture features, which are then used to segment the chili pepper region. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms traditional image segmentation techniques in terms of accuracy. This study contributes to the field of chili pepper quality inspection by providing a reliable and effective segmentation method for X-ray images.

The paper "Guided Proofreading of Automatic Segmentations for Connectomics" describes a novel way to correct errors and refine the result by an interactive proofreading process. Using a large number of experiments, the paper has demonstrated a significant improvement in the quality of the segmentation. In summary, the paper presents a promising solution for the efficient and precise segmentation of neuronal structures for the field of "neuro-network analysis."

The aim of this note is to introduce the notion of a $\operatorname{D}$-Lie
algebra and to prove some elementary properties of $\operatorname{D}$-Lie
algebras, the category of $\operatorname{D}$-Lie algebras, the category of
Modules on a $\operatorname{D}$-Lie algebra and extensions of
$\operatorname{D}$-Lie Algebras. a $\operatorname{D}$-Lie algebra Is An
$A/k$-Lie-Rinehart algebra equipped with an $A\otimes_k A$-module structure and
a canonical central element $D$ and a compatibility property between the
$k$-Lie Algebra structure and the $A\otimes_k A$-module structure. Several
authors have studied non-abelian extensions of Lie algebras, super Lie
algebras, Lie algebroids and holomorphic Lie algebroids and we give in this
note an explicit constructions of all non-abelian extensions a

where $\tilde{L}$ is projective as left $A$-module and $W$ is an $A\otimes_k
A$-module with $IW=0$ for $I$ the kernel of the multiplication map. As a
corollary we Get an explicit construction of all non-abelian extensions of an
$A/k$-Lie-Rinehart algebra $(L,\alpha)$ by an $A$-Lie algebra $(W,[,])$ Where
$L$ is projective as left $A$-module.

In this paper, novel finite element formulation for general boundary conditions in incompressible flows is presented. The proposed method enables imposition of complex boundary conditions, such as those encountered in fluid-structure interaction and multiphase flow problems, using straightforward and efficient approach.

The proposed formulation is based on a slight modification of the standard Galerkin method, which allows for the inclusion of arbitrary boundary terms in weak statement of problem. This permits the incorporation of diverse types of boundary conditions, including Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin, and generalized Robin conditions, without requiring any additional stabilization techniques or artificial modifications to the governing equations.

The proposed method is validated through a series of numerical examples that demonstrate its accuracy, flexibility, and robustness. The results obtained using new formulation are compared with existing solutions and show excellent agreement, further confirming effectiveness of the proposed method.

The key advantage of the present approach lies in its ability to handle complex boundary conditions while maintaining simplicity and ease of implementation. The technique has far-reaching implications for simulating various fluid dynamics problems and can be easily integrated into existing finite element frameworks, making it attractive choice for researchers and engineers working in the field.

The academic journal called "Vessel-CAPTCHA: an effective learning methodology for vessel annotation and segmentation" provides a novel method to effectively learn and segment vessels in retinal fundus images utilizing a vessel-CAPTCHA methodology. The proposed method uses a deep learning model that is trained on a small database of annotated images, and uses a CAPTCHA-based method to reduce the reliability of vessel segmentation. The findings of experimental analyses demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed methodology in effectively analyzing and analyzing vessels in retinal fundus images, making it a useful tool for scientists and practitioners working in the field of ophthalmology.

> We propose a divide-and-conquer strategy towards understanding deep networks. We first divide a deep network into a set of sub-networks, and then we analyze each sub-network separately. We show that the sub-networks can be divided into three types: the sub-networks that are difficult to recognize and the sub-networks that are difficult to recognize. The sub-networks that are difficult to recognize are the sub-networks that are comparable to shallow networks. The sub-networks that are difficult to recognize are the sub-networks that are comparable to deep networks. We then propose a divide-and-conquer algorithm to identify the sub-networks that are difficult to identify. The algorithm is based on the concept of divide-and-conquer. The algorithm first divides the sub-network into three sub-networks, and then it examines each sub-network separately. The algorithm repeats this procedure until the sub-networks are large enough to be studied separately. We show that the algorithms can be applied to identify the sub-networks that are difficult to recognize. We also show that the algorithms can be applied to identify the sub-networks that are difficult to identify.
