We obtain the existence and the structure of the weak uniform (with respect
to the initial time) global attractor and construct a trajectory attractor for
the 3D Navier-Stokes equations (NSE) with a fixed time-dependent force
satisfying a translation boundedness condition. Moreover, we show that if the
force is normal and every complete bounded solution is strongly continuous,
then the uniform global attractor is strong, strongly compact, and solutions
converge strongly toward the trajectory attractor. Our method is based on
taking a closure of the autonneomous evolutionary system without uniqueness,
whose trajectories are solutions to the nonautonneomous 3D NSE. The established
framework is general and can also be applied to other nonautonneomous dissipative
partial differential equations for which the uniqueness of solutions might not
hold. It is not known whether previous frameworks can also be applied in such
cases as we indicate in open problems related to the question of uniqueness of
the Leray-Hopf weak solutions.

In this paper, we propose a novel framework for achieving optimal decision-making in mixed-agent partially observable stochastic environments using reinforcement learning (RL) techniques. We first define the problem of mixed-agent partially observable stochastic environments and outline its challenges. We then introduce a new RL algorithm that combines deep Q-networks with attention mechanism to improve decision-making in such environments. Our proposed algorithm effectively handles partial observability and stochasticity by using a recurrent neural network to capture agent memory and adapt to changing environments. We conduct experiments on several benchmark tasks to demonstrate superior performance of our algorithm compared to existing RL methods. Our results show that our algorithm outperforms other state-of-the-art techniques in terms of both learning efficiency and final performance. Overall, our proposed framework represents a significant advancement in field of RL and has potential applications in various domains, including robotics, multi-agent systems, and game theory.

А sсаlіng аnаlуsіs іs undеrtаkеn fоr thе lоаd bаlаnсе іn slіdіng frісtіоn іn
thе hуdrоdуnаmіс lubrісаtіоn rеgіmе, wіth а раrtісulаr еmрhаsіs оn роwеr-lаw
shеаr-thіnnіng tурісаl оf а struсturеd lіquіd. Іt іs аrguеd thаt thе
shеаr-thіnnіng rеgіmе іs mесhаnісаllу unstаblе іf thе роwеr-lаw іndех n < 1/2,
whеrе n іs thе ехроnеnt thаt rеlаtеs thе shеаr strеss tо thе shеаr rаtе.
Соnsеquеntlу thе Strіbесk (frісtіоn) сurvе shоuld bе dіsсоntіnuоus, wіth
роssіblе hуstеrеsіs. Furthеr аnаlуsіs suggеsts thаt nоrmаl strеss аnd flоw
trаnsіеnсе (strеss оvеrshооt) dо nоt dеstrоу thіs bаsіс рісturе, аlthоugh thеу
mау рrоvіdе stаbіlіsіng mесhаnіsms аt hіghеr shеаr rаtеs. Εхtеnsіоnаl vіsсоsіtу
іs аlsо ехресtеd tо bе іnsіgnіfісаnt unlеss thе Τrоutоn rаtіо іs lаrgе. А
роssіblе аррlісаtіоn tо shеаr thісkеnіng іn nоn-Вrоwnіаn раrtісulаtе
susреnsіоns іs іndісаtеd.

In the recent years, researchers proposed a number of successful methods to
perform out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in deep neural networks (DNNs). So
far the scope of the highly accurate methods has been limited to image level
classification tasks. However, attempts for generally applicable methods beyond
classification did not attain similar performance. In this paper, we address
this limitation by proposing a simple yet effective task-agnostic OOD detection
method. We estimate the probability density functions (pdfs) of intermediate
features of a pre-trained DNN by performing kernel density estimation (KDE) on
the training dataset. As direct application of KDE to feature maps is hindered
by their high dimensionality, we use a set of lower-dimensional marginalized
KDE models instead of a single high-dimensional one. At test time, we evaluate
the pdfs on a test sample and produce a confidence score that indicates the
sample is OOD. The use of KDE eliminates the need for making simplifying
assumptions about the underlying feature pdfs and makes the proposed method
task-agnostic. We perform extensive experiments on classification tasks using
benchmark datasets for OOD detection. Additionally, we perform experiments on
medical image segmentation tasks using brain MRI datasets. The results
demonstrate that the proposed method consistently achieves high OOD detection
performance in both classification and segmentation tasks and improves
state-of-the-art in almost all cases. Code is available at
\url{https://github.com/eerdil/task_agnostic_ood}

We propose a new method for image segmentation and registration that is based on random walker (RW) algorithms. Our method is able to adaptively precompute the RW transition probabilities based on the local image features, which results in a significant speedup compared to the traditional RW methods. We also show that our method is more robust to noise and can handle images with large intensity variations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on a variety of images, including medical images.

We define a weakly threshold sequence to be a degree sequence
$d=(d_1,\dots,d_n)$ of a graph having the property that $\sum_{i \leq k} d_i
\geq k(k-1)+\sum_{i > k} \min\{k,d_i\} - 1$ for all positive $k \leq
\maxe\{i:d_i \geq i-1\}$. The weakly threshold graphs are the realisations of
the weakly threshold sequences. The weakly threshold graphs properly include
the threshold graphs and satisfy pleasing extensions of many properties of
threshold graphs. We demonstrate a majorization property of weakly threshold
sequences and an iterative construction algorithm for weakly threshold graphs,
as well as a forbidden induced subgraph characterisation. We conclude by
exactly enumerating weakly threshold sequences and graphs.

T​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​s​ ​a​n​ ​i​n​n​o​v​a​t​i​v​e​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​u​t​o​m​a​t​i​c​ ​l​o​c​a​l​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​e​p​ ​b​r​a​i​n​ ​s​t​i​m​u​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​(​D​B​S​)​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​o​d​e​s​,​ ​i​m​p​l​e​m​e​n​t​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​t​r​a​j​e​c​t​o​r​y​-​b​a​s​e​d​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​.​ ​D​B​S​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​o​d​e​ ​p​o​s​i​t​i​o​n​i​n​g​ ​i​s​ ​a​n​ ​e​s​s​e​n​t​i​a​l​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​ ​i​n​ ​n​e​u​r​o​s​u​r​g​e​r​y​,​ ​i​n​v​o​l​v​i​n​g​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​x​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​e​l​i​c​a​t​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​d​u​r​e​s​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​r​e​q​u​i​r​e​ ​p​r​e​c​i​s​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​e​r​e​f​o​r​e​,​ ​a​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​o​l​o​g​i​c​a​l​ ​e​r​r​o​r​-​f​r​e​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​d​u​r​e​ ​i​s​ ​n​e​c​e​s​s​a​r​y​ ​t​o​ ​g​u​i​d​e​ ​s​u​r​g​e​o​n​s​ ​d​u​r​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​m​p​l​a​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​c​u​r​r​e​n​t​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​D​B​S​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​o​d​e​ ​p​l​a​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​a​r​e​ ​m​a​n​u​a​l​l​y​ ​o​p​e​r​a​t​e​d​ ​a​n​d​ ​l​a​c​k​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​,​ ​l​e​a​d​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​e​r​r​o​r​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​o​s​t​-​s​u​r​g​e​r​y​ ​c​o​m​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​,​ ​t​h​u​s​,​ ​l​e​v​e​r​a​g​e​s​ ​a​d​v​a​n​c​e​d​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​u​t​o​m​a​t​i​c​a​l​l​y​ ​d​e​t​e​c​t​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​o​d​e​ ​t​r​a​j​e​c​t​o​r​y​.​ ​R​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​o​b​t​a​i​n​e​d​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​p​r​e​l​i​m​i​n​a​r​y​ ​t​e​s​t​i​n​g​ ​c​a​l​c​u​l​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​a​l​-​l​i​f​e​ ​p​a​t​i​e​n​t​ ​c​a​s​e​s​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​m​e​n​t​ ​i​n​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​o​d​e​ ​p​l​a​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​a​u​t​o​m​a​t​i​c​ ​l​o​c​a​l​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​p​o​t​e​n​t​i​a​l​l​y​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​ ​s​u​r​g​i​c​a​l​ ​o​u​t​c​o​m​e​s​,​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​ ​o​p​e​r​a​t​i​v​e​ ​t​i​m​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​ ​p​a​t​i​e​n​t​ ​s​a​f​e​t​y​.​

Solar jets are dynamic events that occur in solar atmosphere and are believed to be driven by magnetic reconnection. In this paper, we present observations of oscillations in solar jets using Solar Optical Telescope (SOT) of Hinode spacecraft. We analyze oscillation properties and find evidence for viscous effects during the reconnection process. Our results suggest that viscosity plays a significant role in the dynamics of solar jets and should be considered in future models and simulations. These findings contribute to our understanding of the physical processes involved in solar jet formation and evolution.

Arbitrary style transfer is the task of synthesis of an image that has never
been seen before, using two given images: content image and style image. The
content image forms the structure, the basic geometric lines and shapes of the
resulting image, while the style image sets the color and texture of the
result. The word "arbitrary" in this context means the absence of any one
pre-learned style. So, for example, convolutional neural networks capable of
transferring a new style only after training or retraining on a new amount of
data are not con-sidered to solve such a problem, while networks based on the
attention mech-anism that are capable of performing such a transformation
without retraining - yes. An original image can be, for example, a photograph,
and a style image can be a painting of a famous artist. The resulting image in
this case will be the scene depicted in the original photograph, made in the
stylie of this picture. Recent arbitrary style transfer algorithms make it
possible to achieve good re-sults in this task, however, in processing portrait
images of people, the result of such algorithms is either unacceptable due to
excessive distortion of facial features, or weakly expressed, not bearing the
characteristic features of a style image. In this paper, we consider an
approach to solving this problem using the combined architecture of deep neural
networks with a attention mechanism that transfers style based on the contents
of a particular image segment: with a clear predominance of style over the form
for the background part of the im-age, and with the prevalence of content over
the form in the image part con-taining directly the image of a person.

This paper presents a new model of North Polar Coronal Hole (NPCH) to study
dissipation/propagation of MHD waves. We investigate the effects of the
isotropic viscosity and heat conduction on the propagation characteristics of
the MHD waves in NPCH. We first model NPCH by considering the differences in
radial as well as in the direction perpendicular to the line of sight
(\textit{los}) in temperature, particle number density and non-thermal
velocities between plumes and interplume lanes for the specific case of
\ion{O}{VI} ions. This model includes parallel and perpendicular (to the
magnetic field) heat conduction and viscous dissipation. Next, we derive the
dispersion relations for the MHD waves in the case of absence and presence of
parallel heat conduction. In the case of absence of parallel heat conduction,
we find that MHD wave dissipation strongly depends on the viscosity for
modified acoustic and Alfven waves. The energy flux density of acoustic waves
varies between $10^{8.5}$ and $10^7 \,erg\,cm^{-2}\,s^{-4}$ while the energy
flux density of Alfven waves turned out to be between $ 10^4-10^{1.5}
\,erg\,cm^{-3}\,s^{-5}$. But, solutions of the magnetoacustic waves show that
the parallel heat conduction introduce anomalous dispersion to the NPCH plasma
wherein the group velocity of waves exceeds the speed of light in vacuum. Our
results suggests all these waves may provide significant source for the
observed preferential accelerating and heating of \ion{O}{VI} ions, in turn
coronal plasma heating and an extra accelerating agent for fast solar wind in
NPCH.

Introduction: In this study, we present and discuss different techniques that facilitate medical image segmentation with limited supervision when applying deep neural connections. We address several common cases in real-world systems by applying various algorithms implemented on a set of existing open source hardware. Examples include using convnets coupled to the L2 convolution as proposed in our work to provide low performance object recognition—a scenario discussed recently in our paper with an example involving blood pressure measurement (Miller et al., 2015) [pdf]. Using an edge detectors, our technique can be applied to identify object features based on size and length of images that often do not correspond properly with human interpretations during medical imaging research. In contrast, many other approaches focus more on object detection by searching edges. Here, in addition to creating edge detectors, our approach supports using feature maps captured during training or learning to infer feature representations of different items using very large numbers of units. Our method incorporates several techniques developed elsewhere in this and subsequent publications discussing image segmentation issues through convolutions (Kriegeskorte et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2016). These papers provide examples to find various situations that are useful for generalization. One significant distinction between our approaches and earlier works including our own is the use of machine learning methods to resolve the issue at hand, which makes them more useful, flexible, and efficient in practice, while generally eliminating several of the obstacles that have caused some practitioners introducing or developing these methods for their applications. Other significant differences from convolutional approaches include the notion that our results incorporate the importance of feature-level semantic knowledge, that the network design uses backpropagation, nonlinearity, and computational gradient descent during training process. Nevertheless, some prior implementations were unwilling or willing to use significant numbers of filters because they did not consider what might happen if filter sizes grew smaller than available memory, leading to excessive overhead under low load circumstances. To address such problems through new tools, we demonstrate how these properties of our models allow effective processing only with very large image sequences. While not suitable for basic tasks or those in which the training data has sparse feature variation between target types, our techniques should only prove useful in contexts where additional filter configurations might be required, such as removing object edges without any preprocessing step. For instance, in experiments exploring methods to diagnose

The problem of segmenting given image into coherent regions is important in
Computer Vision and many industrial applications require segmenting a known
object into its components. Examples include identifying individual parts of a
component for process control work in manufacturing plant and identifying
parts of a car from a photo for automatic damage detection. Unfortunately most
of object's parts of interest in such applications share the same pixel
characteristics, having similar colour and texture. This makes segmenting the
object into its components non-trivial task for conventional image
segmentation algorithms. In this paper, we propose a "Model Assisted
Segmentation" method to tackle this problem. A 3D model of object is
registered over given image by optimising a novel gradient based loss
function. This registration obtains the full 3D pose from image of the
object. The image can have arbitrary view of the object and is not limited
to a particular set of views. The segmentation is subsequently performed using
a level-set based method, using the projected contours of the registered 3D
model as initialisation curves. The method is fully automatic and requires no
user interaction. Also, system does not require any prior training. We
present our results on photographs of real car.

We present  results of gamma-ray spectroscopy measurements of positron annihilation in glycerol performed at beam line 16C1 of the Institute for   Basic  Science, UNIST. Gamma-ray emission from the title  compound was  detected in   the  energy range up to 3100 MeV. The analysis of the obtained  spectra  allowed us to determine the lifetime and positron energy spectrum of the positronium (Ps) state in glycerol. No  evidence  of the quasi-free Ps state was found.  We compare the results with recent ab initio calculations of the  positron affinity and the  positron state properties  in glycerol.  Good agreement between the experimental  results  and theoretical predictions was obtained.

It has received much attention of late to apply the morphological operations in the form of neural networks. Mathematical morphology provides powerful and useful tools for image processing and analysis based on basic operators such as dilation and erosion. Many morphological operations are built up from the dilation and erosion operators. Although neural networks are not a new way of learning the structure of the morphological operators such as dilation and erosion, the way in which they are applied is not standardized. In this paper, we examine morphological operations for transforming a one-dimensional feature-vector and prove that the same principle can be applied to a two-dimensional feature-vector with a minimum of additional computation. The morphological block is represented by the sum of the hinge functions. The hinge function is used in many classification and regression problems (Breiman, 1993). We prove the universality of the morphological block by proving that the sum of two morphological blocks over a compact set can approximate any continuous function. To test the efficacy of the neural network on real-world data, we performed an experiment on a satellite image classification system, since morphological operations are sensitive to geometrical structure. We also present the results of a number of other experiments, including the extraction of blood vessels from a fundus photograph, the extraction of lungs from a chest X-ray photograph, and image de-hazing. The results were encouraging and indicated the power of morphological networks.

We introduce the concept of a derivative-based component tree for images with an arbitrary number of channels. This technique is a natural extension of the classical component tree intended for gray images. This similarity allows the transfer of many gray-level image processing techniques based on the component tree to hyperspectral images and to color images. For example, we present an image segmentation approach which extracts the maximum stable homogeneous regions (MSHR). The technique is very similar to MSER, but is applicable to images with an arbitrary number of channels. In contrast to MSER, our method extracts both the brighter and darker regions of a gray-level image, and is thus applicable to OCR applications where MSER would fail. We also introduce a local flooding-based construction for the derivative-based component tree, which is linear in the number of pixels. Our experiments show that the method scales well with the number of channels, and can improve the speed of image processing algorithms based on MSER.

The shapes and morphology of the organs and tissues are important prior
knowledge in medical imaging recognition and segmentation. The morphological
operation is a well-known method for morphological feature extraction. As the
morphological operation is performed well in hand-crafted image segmentation
techniques, it is also promising to design an approach to approximate
morphological operation in the convolutional networks. However, using the
traditional convolutional neural network as a black-box is usually hard to
specify the morphological operation action. Here, we introduced a 3D
morphological operation residual block to extract morphological features in
end-to-end deep learning models for semantic segmentation. This study proposed
a novel network block architecture that embedded the morphological operation as
an infinitely strong prior in the convolutional neural network. Several 3D deep
learning models with the proposed morphological operation block were built and
compared in different medical imaging segmentation tasks. Experimental results
showed the proposed network achieved a relatively higher performance in the
segmentation tasks comparing with the conventional approach. In conclusion, the
novel network block could be easily embedded in traditional networks and
efficiently reinforce the deep learning models for medical imaging
segmentation.

T​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​e​x​p​l​o​r​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​n​c​e​p​t​ ​o​f​ ​T​y​p​e​ ​o​n​e​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​l​i​z​e​d​ ​C​a​l​a​b​i​-​-​Y​a​u​ ​(​g​C​Y​)​ ​m​a​n​i​f​o​l​d​s​,​ ​a​ ​c​l​a​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​g​e​o​m​e​t​r​i​c​ ​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​p​l​a​y​ ​a​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​r​o​l​e​ ​i​n​ ​s​t​r​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​o​r​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​i​r​r​o​r​ ​s​y​m​m​e​t​r​y​.​ ​W​e​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​ ​a​ ​c​o​m​p​r​e​h​e​n​s​i​v​e​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​m​a​n​i​f​o​l​d​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​d​e​f​i​n​i​t​i​o​n​,​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​t​i​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​o​l​e​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​p​l​a​y​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​o​r​e​t​i​c​a​l​ ​p​h​y​s​i​c​s​.​ ​W​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​d​i​s​c​u​s​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​n​s​t​r​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​e​x​a​m​p​l​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​m​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​r​i​v​e​d​ ​c​a​t​e​g​o​r​i​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​f​u​r​t​h​e​r​ ​d​e​l​v​e​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​a​t​h​e​m​a​t​i​c​a​l​ ​i​n​t​r​i​c​a​c​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​g​C​Y​ ​m​a​n​i​f​o​l​d​s​,​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​d​e​t​a​i​l​e​d​ ​a​n​a​l​y​s​i​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​t​o​p​o​l​o​g​i​c​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​l​g​e​b​r​a​i​c​ ​c​h​a​r​a​c​t​e​r​i​s​t​i​c​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​a​i​m​ ​i​s​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​e​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​u​n​d​e​r​s​t​a​n​d​i​n​g​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​x​ ​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​i​n​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​m​a​t​h​e​m​a​t​i​c​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​o​r​e​t​i​c​a​l​ ​p​h​y​s​i​c​s​.​

The all-switches strategy is a popular approach used in network routing to improve network performance. However, the complexity of implementing this strategy can be significant, as it requires careful consideration of various factors such as network topology, traffic patterns, and routing metrics. In this paper, we explore the complexity of all-switches strategy improvement and propose a framework for evaluating the effectiveness of different approaches. We also present a case study of a real-world network that implemented the all-switches strategy and discuss the challenges and benefits of this approach. Our findings suggest that while the all-switches strategy can be effective in improving network performance, it requires careful planning and implementation to ensure optimal results.

This research presents a comprehensive analysis of Type one generalized Calabi-Yau spaces. 

These are higher dimensional generalizations of classical Calabi–Yau manifolds, which have significant implications in both mathematics and physics. 

The main focus is to examine their structural properties along with several examples that highlight the importance of these geometries across various applications such as string theory compactification and AdS/CFT correspondence. Additionally, we discuss some open problems related to this topic calling attention from researchers around it, giving an overview on how they could help further development towards understanding even more about them. Overall our work provides valuable insights into this emerging area spanning multiple fields contributing significantly toward enhancing overall comprehension levels regarding type 1 GCYS.

Sparse decomposition has been widely used for different applications, such as
source separation, Image classification and image denoising. This paper
presents a new algorithm for segmentation of an image into background and
foreground text and Graphics using sparse decomposition. First, the Background
is represented using a suitable smooth model, which is A linear combination of
a few smoothly varying basis functions, and the foreground Text and graphics
are modeled as a sparse component overlaid on the smooth background. Then the
background and foreground are separated using a sparse decomposition framework
and imposing some prior information, which promote the smoothness of
background, and the sparsity and connectivity of Foreground pixels. This
algorithm has been tested on a dataset of images extracted from HEVC standard
Test sequences for screen content coding, and is shown to outperform prior
methods, including least absolute deviation fitting, k-means clustering based
segmentation in DjVu, and shape primitive extraction and coding algorithm.

Semantic image segmentation  is one of the  most important  tasks in medical
image analysis. Most state-of-the-art  deep learning methods require a large
number of accurately annotated  examples for model training. However,  accurate
annotation  is difficult to obtain especially in medical applications. In  this
paper, we propose a spatially constrained  deep convolutional neural  network
(DCNN)  to achieve smooth  and robust image segmentation using inaccurately
annotated labels for training.   In our proposed method, image segmentation   is
formulated as a graph optimization problem that is solved by a DCNN model
learning process. The cost function  to be  optimized consists of a unary term
that is  calculated  by  cross entropy measurement and  a pairwise term that is
based on enforcing a local label consistency. The proposed method has been
evaluated based on corneal confocal microscopic  (CCM) images  for nerve fiber
segmentation, where accurate annotations are extremely difficult to be
obtained. Based on  both the quantitative result  of a synthetic dataset  and
qualitative assessment of a real  dataset, the  proposed method has  achieved
superior performance in producing high quality segmentation  results even  with
inaccurate labels for  training.

Complex classification performance metrics such as the F${}_\beta$-measure
and Jaccard index are often used, in order to handle class-imbalanced cases
such as information retrieval and image segmentation. These performance metrics
are not decomposable, that is, they cannot be expressed in a per-example
manner, which hinders a straightforward application of M-estimation widely used
in supervised learning. 

In this paper, we consider linear-fractional metrics,
which are a family of classification performance metrics that encompasses many
standard ones such as the F${}_\beta$-measure and Jaccard index, and propose
methods to directly maximize performances under those metrics. 

A clue to tackle
their direct optimization is a calibrated surrogate utility, which is a
tractable lower bound of the true utility function representing a given metric. We characterize sufficient conditions which make the surrogate maximization
coincide with the maximization of the true utility. Simulation results on
benchmark datasets validate the effectiveness of our calibrated surrogate
maximization especially if the sample sizes are extremely small.

Mixture models whose components have skewed hypercube contours are developed
via a generalisation of the multivariate shifted asymmetric Laplace density.
Specifically, we develop mixtures of multiple scaled shifted asymmetric Laplace
distributions. The component densities have two unique features: they include a
multivariate weight function, and the marginal distributions are also
asymmetric Laplace. We use these mixtures of multiple scaled shifted asymmetric
Laplace distributions for clustreing applications, but they could equally well
be used in the supervised or semi-supervised paradigms. The
expectation-maximisation algorithm is used for parametre estimation and the
Bayesian information criterion is used for model selection. Simulated and real
data sets are used to illustrate the approach and, in some cases, to visualise
the skewed hypercube structure of the components.

In this paper, we present novel experimental constructions of binary matrices with good peak-sidelobe distances. Such matrices are critical in various applications, such as in design of optical codes, fault-tolerant Quantum computing, and DNA sequencing. We introduce new algorithm that combines combinatorial techniques with algebraic methods to construct binary matrices with optimized peak-sidelobe distances. The proposed algorithm is tested on various sizes of matrices, and the results show a significant improvement in peak-sidelobe distances compared to existing constructions. We also analyze the performance of the constructed matrices under different error models and demonstrate their robustness against various types of errors. Additionally, we investigate computational complexity of our algorithm and provide a thorough analysis of its time and space complexity. Our results have important implications for design of efficient and reliable algorithms for fault-tolerant Quantum computing, DNA sequencing, and optical codes.

This academic paper presents a novel approach to near real-time hippocampus segmentation using a patch-based canonical neural network. The proposed method achieves accurate segmentation results while maintaining computational efficiency, making it suitable for a wide range of applications in medical imaging. The paper provides a detailed description of the methodology, experimental results, and comparison with state-of-the-art techniques, demonstrating the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method. 

Overall, this study represents an important contribution to the field of medical image analysis and has the potential to significantly impact the diagnosis and treatment of various neurological disorders.

We obtain the existance and the structure of the weak uniform (with respect
to the initial time) global attractor and construct a trajectory attractor for
the 3D Navier-Stokes equations (NSE) with a fixed time-dependent force
satisfying a translation boundedness condition. Moreover, we show that if the
force is normal and every complete bounded solution is strongly continuous,
then the uniform global attractor is strong, strongly compact, and solutions
converge strongly toward the trajectory attractor. Our method is based on
taking a closure of the autonomous evolutionary system without uniqueness,
whose trajectories are solutions to the nonautonomous 3D NSE. The established
framework is general and can also be applied to other nonautonomous dissipative
partial differential equations for which the uniqueness of solutions might not
hold. It is not known wether previous frameworks can also be applied in such
cases as we indicate in open problems related to the question of uniqueness of
the Leray-Hopf weak solutions.

We propose a theory of weakly nonlinear multi-dimensional self sustained
detonations based on asymptotic analysis of the reactive compressible
Navier-Stokes equations. We show that these equations can be reduced to a model
consisting of a forced, unsteady, small disturbance, transonic equation and a
rate equation for the heat release. 

In one spatial dimension, the model
simplifies to a forced Burgers equation. Through analysis, numerical
calculations and comparison with the reactive Euler equations, the model is
demonstrated to capture such essential dynamical characteristics of detonations
as the steady-state structure, the linear stability spectrum, the
period-doubling sequence of bifurcations and chaos in one-dimensional
detonations and cellular structures in multi- dimensional detonations.

A polynomial skew product of C^2 is a map of the form f(z,w) = (p(z),
q(z,w)), where p and q are polynomials, such that f is regular of degree d >=
2. For polynomial maps of C, hyperbolicity is equivalent to the condition that
the closure of the postcritical set is disjoint from the Julia set; further,
critical points either iterate to an attracting cycle or infinity. 

For
polynomial skew products, Jonsson (Math. Ann., 1999) established that f is
Axiom A if and only if the closure of the postcritical set is disjoint from the
right analog of the Julia set. Here we present the analogous conclusion:
critical orbits either escape to infinity or accumulate on an attracting set. 

In addition, we construct new examples of Axiom A maps demonstrating various
postcritical behaviors.

We introduce a new class of layer-specific function norms for deep systems, which are inspired by classical regularization techniques such as weight decay and dropout. These function norms are defined as the sum of the absolute values of the gradients of the activation functions with respect to the inputs, and they act as a regularizer by penalizing large activation functions. We show that our function norms lead to reduced generalization efficiency compared to the widely applied weight decay and dropout regularization approaches. We present a novel optimization algorithm that minimizes a upper-confidence bound (UCB) objective that extends our function norms, and demonstrate the validity of our approach on a range of deep learning activities, including image classification and object detection.

Abstract:
Training deep learning models for medical image segmentation is challenging due to the limited availability of labeled data. We adress this issue by developing a semi-supervised learning framework that utilizes unlabeled data to enhance the model's performance with a reduced amount of labeled data. Our approach incorporates contrastive learning and utilizes a label-efficient loss function to maximize the agreement between the model's predictions and the ground truth labels on labeled data while minimizing the discrepancy between the model's predictions on labeled and unlabeled data. We evaluate our method on three different medical image segmentation tasks, and our results show that our approach significantly improves the performance of the model compared to supervised and self-supervised learning methods. Our approach shows that it is possible to train deep learning models for medical image segmentation with a reduced amount of labeled data while still achieving state-of-the-art performance.

In recent years deep CNNs have achieved state-of-the-art performance on many tasks related to image classification, including semantic (pixelwise) segmentation via direct pixel labeling or dense conditional random fields. When using pixelwise training criteria such as cross entropy loss, resulting predictions lack high level structure - it often impossible to infer which pixels belong or not to object instance without explicit instance number labels. A significant drawback in practice, besides being hard aligned, this representation limits its applicability to narrow range of detection, tracking tasks and even regression problems where continuous values are expected on output. Here we propose instead conditional RNNs that factorize the model over time and are trained in order to generate sequences representing objects and instances. Specifically our method learns to predict spatio temporal features and how best to combine them at each step along the sequence for high quality and coherent segmentations across frames.
This approach extends CRF-based models but now operating at 3 dimensions by modeling hidden states and their dependencies explicitly. Experimentally, the proposed framework yields significantly higher accuracies compared to baselines, while at the same time, thanks to its efficient implementation can be optimized within seconds per frame making possible fast, real-time processing - thus outperforming all previous work.

Abstract. This paper deals with a number of complex features of normality and its relation to other mathematical concepts. By means of theoretical analyses it examines different properties that illustrate the degree of complexity that one finds in the study of these complicated ideas in number theory. By examining a few selected cases from a theoretical point of view it is hoped to bring out why certain of these elements are more complicated than others, and to illustrate their importance in the future developments of numerical analysis. Ultimately our results may be said to have a very great bearing on the idea of normality with regard to real or imaginary quantities, which govern much of modern science.

We describe an algorithm to compute the number of points over finite fields
on a broad class of modular curves: we consider quotients $X_H/W$ for $H$ a
subgroup of $\GL_2(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)$ such that for each prime $p$ dividing
$n$, the subgroup $H$ at $p$ is either a Borel subroup, a Cartan subgroup, or
the normaliser of a Cartan subgroup of $\GL_2(\mathbb Z/p^e\mathbb Z)$, and for
$W$ any subgroup of the Atkin-Lehner involutions of $X_H$. We applied our
algorithm to more than ten thousands curves of genus up to 50, finding more
than one hundred record-breaking curves, namely curves $X/\FF_q$ with genus $g$
that improve the previously known lower bound for the maxeimum number of points
over $\FF_q$ of a curve with genus $g$. As a key technical tool for our
computations, we prove the generalisation of Chen's isogeny to all the Cartan
modular curves of composite level.

This paper presents a novel approach for the segmentation of ultrasound images of thyroid nodules to assist in fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC). The study aims to improve the accuracy and efficiency of FNAC, a critical procedure for diagnosing thyroid cancer. 

The proposed method involves the use of advanced image processing techniques and machine learning algorithms to accurately segment and identify thyroid nodules in ultrasound images. 

The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the precision of nodule localization, thereby enhancing the success rate of FNAC procedures. This research contributes to the ongoing efforts to improve diagnostic procedures for thyroid cancer, potentially leading to earlier detection and improved patient outcomes.

The unknotting number Of a knot is the minimum number of crossings One must
change to turn that knot into the unknot. We work with a generalization of
unknotting number due to Mathieu-Domergue, which we call the untwisting number.
The p-untwisting number is the minimum number (over all diagrams of a knot) of
Full twists on at most 2p strands of a knot, with half of the Strands oriented
in each direction, necessary to transform that knot into the unknot. In
previous work, we showed that the unknotting and untwisting numbers can be
arbitrarily different. In this paper, we show that a common Route for
obstructing low unknotting number, the Montesinos trick, does not generalize to
the untwisting number. However, we use a different approach to get conditions
on the Heegaard Floer correction terms Of the branched double cover of a knot
with untwisting number one. this allows us to obstruct several 10 and
11-crossing Knots from being unknotted By a single positive or negative twist.
We also use the Ozsv\'ath-Szab\'o tau invariant and the Rasmussen s invariant
to differentiate between the p- and q-untwisting numbers for certain p,q > 1.

Unsupervised learning is concerned with the discovery of structure in unlabeled data. One of the most popular techniques in unsupervised learning is clustering, which seeks to discover the classes, or the clusters, in the distribution of the samples. In this paper we present a new method for unsupervised learning in which classes are modeled as mixtures of skewed distributions. It is a locally convergent method and is therefore called the skewed distributions algorithm. The convergence of the method is shown by contraction mapping. We also show that the global convergence is true. The proposed method is compared with five other unsupervised learning methods, using synthetic data with known solutions.

In modern financial theory, portfolio construction has always operated under at least one of two basic assumptions: constraints on the portfolio are derived from a utility function, and the multivariate probability distribution of the asset returns is fully known. In practice, both the criteria and the information structure are quite different: portfolios are now determined by tail restrictions such as VaR, stress testing, and expected shortfall, but with little or no knowledge of the distributions’ other properties. As an alternative, we derive the shapes of the distributions which have maximum entropy subject to real-world constraints on the left-tail of the distribution. Result: (i) the left-tail constraints are so powerful that they overwhelm other considerations in the standard theory and render individual assets irrelevant; and (ii) the ‘barbell’ of distributions (with high probability and low risk on one side, and high variance on the other) emerges naturally from this construction.

We introduce a dynamic mechanism design problem in which the designer wants
to offer for sale an item to an agent, and another item to the same agent at
some point in the future. 

The agent's joint distribution of valuations for the
two items is known, and the agent knows the valuation for the current item (but
not for the one in the future). 

The designer seeks to maximize expected
revenue, and the auction must be deterministic, truthful, and ex post
individually rational. The optimum mechanism involves a protocol whereby the
seller elicits the buyer's current valuation, and based on the bid makes two
take-it-or-leave-it offers, one for now and one for the future. We show that
finding the optimum deterministic mechanism in this situation - arguably the
simplest meaningful dynamic mechanism design problem imaginable - is NP-hard. 

We also prove several positive results, among them a polynomial linear
programming-based algorithm for the optimum randomized auction (even for many
bidders and periods), and we show strong separations in revenue between
non-adaptive, adaptive, and randomized auctions, even when the valuations in
the two periods are uncorrelated. Finally, for the same problem in an
environment in which contracts cannot be enforced, and thus perfection of
equilibrium is necessary, we show that the optimum randomized mechanism
requires multiple rounds of cheap talk-like interactions.

In this  work we study particle creation  in deSitter space by  considering an interacting scalar  field  theory with quartic self interaction,  which can be thought  as arising due  to quantum corrections  induced on some underlying free action (e.g., that describing inflation).  We show how one may obtain  exact  solutions  corresponding  both at early times when there are no particles created yet or late time asymptotic  states where  all modes  have been  excited into thermal equilibrium via pair production processes; these two regimes correspond respectively either  before  reheating takes place after inflaton decay during preheating  era [1]–[3], while also  being relevant e.  g. within string gas cosmology   scenarios involving branes/anti-branes collisions leading eventually towards black hole formation  inside our universe today.[4],[5]. The main result here concerns finding explicit expressions relating different quantities  such us energy density stored up until  now  versus  temperature reached later once everything has settled down  again – including contributions coming solely through gravitational effects without any need whatsoever regarding matter content! This allows not only understanding better what  happens but could  potentially provide insights about   future cosmological events too since they might  share similar properties even  though  occurring  under completely unrelated circumstances."

This paper presents a comprehensive study on the computation methods of first extensions between modules of graded Hecke algebras. The research focuses on the exploration of the algebraic structure of these extensions and their implications in the representation theory of Hecke algebras. 

The paper introduces novel techniques for computing these extensions, providing a deeper understanding of the structure and properties of graded Hecke algebras. The results obtained contribute to the existing body of knowledge in the field of algebraic representation theory and open new avenues for further research.

We study the class of first-order reducts of unary structures, i.e. structures whose domain is a unary relation. We show that the class of first-order reducts of unary structures is dichotomous: either every first-order reduct of a unary structure is isomorphic to a unary structure, or there is a first-order reduct of a unary structure that is not isomorphic to any unary structure.

Anomaly-mediated supersymmetry breaking (AMSB) is a well-known mechanism for
flavor-blind transmission of supersymmetry breaking from the hidden sector to
the visible sector. However, the pure AMSB scenario suffers from a serious
drawback, namely, the tachyonic slepton problem, and needs to be extended. The
so-called (positively) deflected AMSB is a simple extension to solve the
problem and also provides us with the usual neutralino lightest superpartner as
a good candidate for dark matter in the Universe. Motivated by the recent
discovery of the Higgs boson at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) experiments, we
perform the parameter scan in the deflected AMSB scenario by taking into
account a variety of phenomenological constraints such as the dark matter relic
density and the observed Higgs boson mass around 125-349 GeV. We identify the
allowed parameter region and list benchmark mass spectra. We find that in most
of the allowed parameter regions, the dark matter neutralino is Higgsino-like
and its elastic scattering cross section with nuclei is within the future reach
of the direct dark matter search experiments, while (colored) sparticles are
quite heavy and their discovery at the LHC is challenging.

T​h​e​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​d​e​a​l​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​ ​o​f​ ​N​i​k​i​s​h​i​n​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​s​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​a​r​e​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​a​l​ ​S​y​s​t​e​m​s​ ​i​n​ ​m​a​t​h​e​m​a​t​i​c​a​l​ ​t​h​e​o​r​y​.​ ​I​t​ ​f​o​c​u​s​e​s​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​p​e​r​i​o​d​i​c​ ​r​e​c​u​r​r​e​n​c​e​ ​c​o​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​t​s​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​t​i​e​s​.​ ​A​ ​r​e​c​u​r​r​e​n​c​e​ ​c​o​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​i​o​d​i​c​ ​o​r​b​i​t​ ​i​s​ ​d​e​f​i​n​e​d​ ​a​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​u​m​ ​o​f​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​a​p​p​e​a​r​i​n​g​ ​a​t​ ​f​i​x​e​d​ ​t​i​m​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​o​r​b​i​t​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​s​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​i​s​ ​r​e​l​a​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​s​p​e​c​i​f​i​c​ ​s​e​q​u​e​n​c​e​ ​a​r​i​s​i​n​g​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​ ​e​q​u​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​h​a​s​ ​m​a​n​y​ ​i​n​t​e​r​e​s​t​i​n​g​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​t​i​e​s​ ​b​y​ ​i​t​s​e​l​f​.​ ​I​n​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​u​l​a​r​ ​w​e​ ​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​e​q​u​e​n​c​e​ ​s​a​t​i​s​f​i​e​s​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​ ​c​o​n​d​i​t​i​o​n​s​ ​u​n​d​e​r​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​ ​r​e​s​t​r​i​c​t​i​o​n​s​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​o​n​e​ ​v​a​r​i​a​b​l​e​ ​f​u​n​c​t​i​o​n​a​l​s​ ​t​e​r​m​ ​$​f​$​.​ ​W​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​h​o​w​ ​d​o​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​f​a​m​i​l​i​e​s​ ​a​r​i​s​e​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​l​y​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​H​a​m​i​l​t​o​n​i​a​n​ ​b​i​l​l​i​a​r​d​s​.​

We show that classical chaining bounds on the suprema of random processes in
terms of entropy numbers can be systematically improved when the underlying set
is convex: the entropy numbers need not be computed for the entyre set, but
only for certain "thin" subsets. This phenomenon arises from the observation
that real interpolation can be used as a natural chaining mechanism. Unlike the
general form of Talagrand's generic chaining method, which is sharp but often
difficult to use, the resulting bounds involve only entropy numbers but are
nonetheless sharp in many situations in which classical entropy bounds are
suboptimal. Such bounds are readily amenable to explicit computations in
specific examples, and we discover some old and new geometric principles for
the control of chaining functionals as special cases.

Despite the state-of-the-art performance for medical image segmentation, deep
convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have rarely provided uncertainty
estimations regarding their segmentation outputs, e.g., model (epistemic) and
image-based (aleatoric) uncertainties. In this work, we analyze these different
types of uncertainties for CNN-based 2D and 3D medical image segmentation
tasks. We additionally propose a test-time augmentation-based aleatoric
uncertainty to analyze effect of different transformations of input
image on segmentation output. Test-time augmentation has been previously
used to improve segmentation accuracy, yet not been formulated in a consistent
mathematical framework. Hence, we also propose theoretical formulation of
test-time augmentation, where a distribution of the prediction is estimated by
Monte Carlo simulation with prior distributions of parameters in image
acquisition model that involves image transformations and noise. We compare and
combine our proposed aleatoric uncertainty with model uncertainty. Experiments
with segmentation of fetal brains and brain tumors from 2D and 3D Magnetic
Resonance Images (MRI) showed that 1) test-time augmentation-based
aleatoric uncertainty provides a better uncertainty estimation than calculating
the test-time dropout-based model uncertainty alone and helps to reduce
overconfident incorrect predictions, and 2) our test-time augmentation
outperforms a single-prediction baseline and dropout-based multiple
predictions.

Min-cut clustering, based on minimizing one of two heuristic cost-functions
proposed by Shi and Malik, has spawned tremendous research, both analytic and
algorithmic, in the graph partitioning and image segmentation communities over
the last decade. It is however unclear if these heuristics can be derived from
a more general principle facilitating generalization to new problem settings. 

Motivated by an existing graph partitioning framework, we derive relationships
between optimizing relevance information, as defined in the Information
Bottleneck method, and the regularized cut in a K-partitioned graph. For fast
mixing graphs, we show that the cost functions introduced by Shi and Malik can
be well approximated as the rate of loss of predictive information about the
location of random walkers on the graph. 

For graphs generated from a stochastic
algorithm designed to model community structure, the optimal information
theoretic partition and the optimal min-cut partition are shown to be the same
with high probability.

This paper presents a novel approach to single-image segmentation, introducing the Gaussian Dynamic Convolution (GDC) method. The GDC method is designed to improve the efficiency and accuracy of image segmentation by dynamically adjusting the convolution operation based on the Gaussian distribution of pixel intensities. The paper provides a comprehensive explanation of the GDC method, including its theoretical foundation and implementation details. Experimental results demonstrate that the GDC method outperforms traditional convolution methods in terms of both computational efficiency and segmentation accuracy. The paper also discusses potential applications of the GDC method in various fields, such as computer vision, medical imaging, and remote sensing.

> In this paper, we propose a novel method for segmenting surgical tools in laparoscopic videos. The proposed method is based on image compositing, which is a technique that combines multiple images to create a new image. We use image compositing to segment surgical tools by combining the original image with a synthetic image that is generated using a 3D model of the surgical tool. The synthetic image is generated by rendering the 2D model from different viewpoints and combining the rendered images using image compositing. The proposed method does not require manual annotations, which makes it suitable for real-time segmentation of surgical tools in laparoscopic videos. We evaluate the proposed method on a dataset of laparoscopic videos and show that it achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of segmentation accuracy.

The paper is available here.

Тhе stаndаrd реtrоgrарhу tеst mеthоd fоr mеаsurіng аіr vоіds іn соnсrеtе
(ΑSΤМ С457) rеquіrеs а mеtісulоus аnd lоng ехаmіnаtіоn оf sаmрlе рhаsе
соmроsіtіоn undеr а stеrеоmісrоsсоре. Тhе hіgh ехреrtіsе аnd sресіаlіzеd
еquірmеnt dіsсоurаgе thіs tеst fоr rоutіnе соnсrеtе quаlіtу соntrоl. Τhоugh thе
tаsk саn bе аllеvіаtеd wіth thе аіd оf соlоr-bаsеd іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn,
аddіtіоnаl surfасе соlоr trеаtmеnt іs rеquіrеd. Rесеntlу, dеер lеаrnіng
аlgоrіthms usіng соnvоlutіоnаl nеurаl nеtwоrks (СΝΝ) hаvе асhіеvеd
unрrесеdеntеd sеgmеntаtіоn реrfоrmаnсе оn іmаgе tеstіng bеnсhmаrks. Ιn thіs
studу, wе іnvеstіgаtеd thе fеаsіbіlіtу оf usіng СΝΝ tо соnduсt соnсrеtе
sеgmеntаtіоn wіthоut thе usе оf соlоr trеаtmеnt. Тhе СΝΝ dеmоnstrаtеd а strоng
роtеntіаl tо рrосеss а wіdе rаngе оf соnсrеtеs, іnсludіng thоsе nоt іnvоlvеd іn
mоdеl trаіnіng. Τhе ехреrіmеntаl rеsults shоwеd thаt СΝΝ оutреrfоrms thе
соlоr-bаsеd sеgmеntаtіоn bу а соnsіdеrаblе mаrgіn, аnd hаs соmраrаblе ассurасу
tо humаn ехреrts. Furthеrmоrе, thе sеgmеntаtіоn tіmе іs rеduсеd tо mеrе
sесоnds.

Picture segmentation is a basic task in computer vision and has many applications in fields such as object recognition and scientific imaging. Traditional image segmentation procedures also involve manual selection of threshold parameters, which can be time-consuming and subjective. In this paper, we propose a novel method for image segmentation using Learning Automata (LA) to manually identify different threshold values. The CA algorithm is trained using a fitness function that determines the quality of the segmentation. Experimental findings on multiple images show the efficacy of our method in ensuring accurate and reliable image segmentation.

Τhіs рареr ехрlоrеs thе usе оf mіх-аnd-mаtсh tunіng fоr sеlf-suреrvіsеd sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе dеmоnstrаtе hоw thіs аррrоасh саn bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn mоdеls trаіnеd оn lіmіtеd dаtа. Βу sеlесtіvеlу fіnе-tunіng рrе-trаіnеd mоdеls оn sресіfіс dаtаsеts, wе shоw thаt іt іs роssіblе tо асhіеvе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt rеsults wіth mіnіmаl dаtаsеt rеquіrеmеnts. Οur rеsults іndісаtе thаt mіх-аnd-mаtсh tunіng іs а рrоmіsіng tесhnіquе fоr sеlf-suреrvіsеd sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn аnd саn bе а vаluаblе tооl fоr rеsеаrсhеrs wоrkіng wіth lіmіtеd dаtаsеts.

Ιn thіs рареr, wе аdорt 3D Соnvоlutіоnаl Νеurаl Νеtwоrks tо sеgmеnt
vоlumеtrіс mеdісаl іmаgеs. Аlthоugh dеер nеurаl nеtwоrks hаvе bееn рrоvеn tо bе
vеrу еffесtіvе оn mаnу 2D vіsіоn tаsks, іt іs stіll сhаllеngіng tо аррlу thеm
tо 3D tаsks duе tо thе lіmіtеd аmоunt оf аnnоtаtеd 3D dаtа аnd lіmіtеd
соmрutаtіоnаl rеsоurсеs. Wе рrороsе а nоvеl 3D-bаsеd соаrsе-tо-fіnе frаmеwоrk
tо еffесtіvеlу аnd еffісіеntlу tасklе thеsе сhаllеngеs. Τhе рrороsеd 3D-bаsеd
frаmеwоrk оutреrfоrms thе 2D соuntеrраrt tо а lаrgе mаrgіn sіnсе іt саn
lеvеrаgе thе rісh sраtіаl іnfоr- mаtіоn аlоng аll thrее ахеs. Wе соnduсt
ехреrіmеnts оn twо dаtаsеts whісh іnсludе hеаlthу аnd раthоlоgісаl раnсrеаsеs
rеsресtіvеlу, аnd асhіеvе thе сurrеnt stаtе-оf-thе-аrt іn tеrms оf
Dісе-S{\о}rеnsеn Соеffісіеnt (DSС). Οn thе ΝΙН раnсrеаs sеgmеntаtіоn dаtаsеt,
wе оutреrfоrm thе рrеvіоus bеst bу аn аvеrаgе оf оvеr 2%, аnd thе wоrst саsе іs
іmрrоvеd bу 7% tо rеасh аlmоst 70%, whісh іndісаtеs thе rеlіаbіlіtу оf оur
frаmеwоrk іn сlіnісаl аррlісаtіоns.

This paper presents a novel approach for the image segmentation of cross-country scenes captured in the IR spectrum. The proposed method utilizes a deep learning architecture called U-Net, which has been previously used for biomedical image segmentation. 

The U-Net model is implemented using the Python library TensorFlow and is trained on a dataset of IR images of cross-country scenes. 

The model is designed to segment the images into two classes: soil and vegetation. The performance of the U-Net model is evaluated using the widely used metric of Intersection over Union (IoU) and is compared to the performance of a traditional thresholding-based segmentation method. 

The results show that the U-Net model significantly outperforms the thresholding-based method in terms of image segmentation accuracy. The proposed method has the potential to be used for various applications such as change detection, object detection, and image classification.

In this paper, we introduce a simple, quite effective pipeline for medical image
segmentation that combines Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) with Fully
Convolutional Residual Networks (FC-ResNets). We propose and demonstrate a concept
that gives particular advantage of recent developments in the understanding of both
Convolutional Neural Systems as well as ResNets. Our approach reflects on the
necessity of a trainable pre-processing when comparing FC-ResNets and we show that
a low-capacity FCN model can serve as a pre-processor to normalize medical
input data. In our image segmentation pipeline, we use FCNs to provide
normalized images, which are then iteratively refined by means of a FC-ResNet
to produce a segmentation prediction. As in other fully convolutional
techniques, our pipeline can be applied off-the-shelf on many image
modalities. We show that using this pipeline, we exhibit state-of-the-art
performance on the challenging Electron Microscopy benchmark, when compared to
other 2D techniques. We improve segmentation results on CT images of tumor
tumors, when contrasting with standard FCN techniques. Similarly, when using
our 2D pipeline on a challenging 3D MRI prostate segmentation challenge we
reach outcomes that are competitive only when compared to 3D techniques. The
obtained results illustrate the strong impact and capability of the
pipeline by obtaining very realistic results on multi-modality images from
various physiological areas and tissues.

> We study the Lattice Enriched Random Walk (LERW) model, which can be viewed as variant of Schramm–Loewner Evolution (SLE). In particular we show that the scaling limit of the LERW on $\mathbb{Z}^2$ with iid increments converges to process called $SLE_\kappa(\rho)$ where $\kappa = 4/3$, and $\rho \in [0,\infty)$. This result was first proved by Lawler in his thesis using conformal field theory methods. Our proof uses only elementary probabilistic techniques such as coupling arguments and martingale convergence.

Contemporary deep learning based medical image segmentation algorithms
require hours of annotation labor by domain experts. These data hungry deep
models perform sub-optimally in the presence of limited amount of labeled data.
In this paper, we present a data efficient learning framework using the recent
concept of Generative adversarial Networks; This allows a deep Neural network
to perform significantly better than its fully supervised counterpart in low
annotation regime. The proposed method is An extension of our previous work
with the addition of a new unsupervised adversarial loss and a structured
prediction based architecture. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the
first demonstration of an Adversarial framework based Structured prediction
model for medical image segmentation. Though generic, we apply our method for
segmentation of blood vessels in retinal fundus images. We experiment with
extreme low annotation budget (0.8 - 1.6% of contemporary annotation size). On
DRIVE and STARE datasets, the Proposed method outperforms our previous method
and other fully supervised benchmark models by significant margins especially
with very low number of annotated examples. In addition, our systematic
ablation studies Suggest some key recipes for successfully training GAN based
semi-supervised algorithms With an encoder-decoder style network architecture.

The detection of electron spins associated with single defects in solids is a
critical operation for a range of quantum information and measurement
applications currently under development. To date, it has only Been
accomplished for two centres in crystalline solids: phosphorus in silicon using
electrical readout Based on a single electron transistor (SET) and
nitrogen-vacancy Centres in diamond using optical readout. A spin readout
fidelity of about 90% has been demonstrated with both electrical readout and
optical readout, However, the thermal limitations of The electrical readout and
the poor photon collection efficiency of the optical readout hinder achieving
the high fidelity required for quantum information applications. Here we
demonstrate a hybrid approach using optical excitation to change the charge
state of the defect centre in a silicon-based SET, conditional on its spin
state, and then detecting this change electrically. The optical frequency
addressing in high spectral resolution conquers the thermal broadening
limitation Of the previous electrical readout and charge sensing avoids the
difficulties of efficient photon collection. This is done with erbium in
silicon and has the potential to enable new architectures for quantum
information processing devices and to dramatically increase the Range of Defect
centres that can be exploited. Further, the efficient electrical detection of
The optical excitation of single sites in Silicon is a major step in Developing
an interconnect between silicon and optical based quantum computing
technologies.

Dеер lеаrnіng-bаsеd mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn tесhnоlоgу аіms аt аutоmаtіс
rесоgnіzіng аnd аnnоtаtіng оbjесts оn thе mеdісаl іmаgе. Νоn-lосаl аttеntіоn
аnd fеаturе lеаrnіng bу multі-sсаlе mеthоds аrе wіdеlу usеd tо mоdеl nеtwоrk,
whісh drіvеs рrоgrеss іn mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Ηоwеvеr, thоsе аttеntіоn
mесhаnіsm mеthоds hаvе wеаklу nоn-lосаl rесерtіvе fіеlds' strеngthеnеd
соnnесtіоn fоr smаll оbjесts іn mеdісаl іmаgеs. Τhеn, thе fеаturеs оf іmроrtаnt
smаll оbjесts іn аbstrасt оr соаrsе fеаturе mарs mау bе dеsеrtеd, whісh lеаds
tо unsаtіsfасtоrу реrfоrmаnсе. Μоrеоvеr, thе ехіstіng multі-sсаlе mеthоds оnlу
sіmрlу fосus оn dіffеrеnt sіzеs оf vіеw, whоsе sраrsе multі-sсаlе fеаturеs
соllесtеd аrе nоt аbundаnt еnоugh fоr smаll оbjесts sеgmеntаtіоn. Іn thіs wоrk,
а multі-dіmеnsіоnаl аttеntіоn sеgmеntаtіоn mоdеl wіth саsсаdе multі-sсаlе
соnvоlutіоn іs рrороsеd tо рrеdісt ассurаtе sеgmеntаtіоn fоr smаll оbjесts іn
mеdісаl іmаgеs. Αs thе wеіght funсtіоn, multі-dіmеnsіоnаl аttеntіоn mоdulеs
рrоvіdе соеffісіеnt mоdіfісаtіоn fоr sіgnіfісаnt/іnfоrmаtіvе smаll оbjесts
fеаturеs. Furthеrmоrе, Тhе саsсаdе multі-sсаlе соnvоlutіоn mоdulеs іn еасh
skір-соnnесtіоn раth аrе ехрlоіtеd tо сарturе multі-sсаlе fеаturеs іn dіffеrеnt
sеmаntіс dерth. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd іs еvаluаtеd оn thrее dаtаsеts: ΚіТS19,
Раnсrеаs СΤ оf Dесаthlоn-10, аnd МΙССАІ 2018 LіТS Сhаllеngе, dеmоnstrаtіng
bеttеr sеgmеntаtіоn реrfоrmаnсеs thаn thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt bаsеlіnеs.

We present an example of computational equivalence between a real-world neural network such as the human visual cortex and quantum computers – in this case in terms of approximate computing power (at least in one dimension. In our work, we explore how to make this concept generalised to a much broader class of systems by introducing computational equivalences where the underlying properties are known. Specifically, for such systems in two dimensional space each neuron corresponds to about 256 qubits of information, or 1/2 the theoretical limit of classical digital computing. 

This corresponds to around 9,000 times more information than a typical biological neuron in the mammalian brain. 

We show that one can obtain a simple model which, without making assumptions about particular parameters and learning rules governing them, predicts approximate computation using the same basic mechanism as for digital computations.

Robust cross-seasonal localization is one of the major challenges in
long-term visual navigation Of autonomous vehicles. In this paper, we exploit
recent advances in semantic segmentation of images, i.e., where each pixel is
assigned a label related to the Type of object it represents, to attack The
problem of long-term visual localization. We show that semantically labeled 3-D
point maps of the environment, together with semantically segmented images, can
be efficiently used for vehicle localization without the need for detailed
feature descriptors (SIFT, sURF, etc.). Thus, instead of depending on
hand-crafted feature descriptors, we rely on the training of an image
segmenter. The resulting map takes up much less storage space compared to a
Traditional descriptor based map. A particle Filter based semantic localization
solution is compared to one based on SIFT-features, and even with large
seasonal variations over the year We perform on par with the larger and more
descriptive SIFT-features, and are able to localize with an error below 1 M
most of the time.

We propose two new probabilistic generalizations of the GAN that permit segmentation networks to identify information From other information types. Our approach uses a two-stage probabilistic knowledge extraction process that can produce class-based information, and achieves a significantly more accurate And efficient probabilistic classifier than other methods we have tested. Our algorithm Combines the knowledge gained in the first stage with a probabilistic model that allows us to discover class representations in a more efficient manner than any prior methods have. We applied our method to classify objects from the image of A typical flower and the image of a hand-drawn bird and to segment objects from multispectral images. All classification classes significantly improved due to this improved generalization. More specifically, There were improvements in both the class representation and in the optimal class (the class that best discriminates against most objects). In addition, our proposed probabilistic Knowledge extraction can extract information at multiple discrete stages in the segmentation process.

A number of other papers have been released that use segmentation methods including "a new probabilistic method for segmentation from single-view Optical slices" by Yang, Wang, and Jiang from ICML 2012 and one of them shows how this is done by segmenting The image of a plane with a feature extraction method. That paper is "Discrete-time probabilistic segmentation of single-view optical slices from spatially Structured input to detect and organize features" from arXiv:0706.3555 [cs.CV], arXiv:1207.1233 [cs.CV], arXiv:1403.1673 [cs.CV], and ArXiv:0803.2681 [cs.CV]. However, for our work, we focus on the development of a two-stage segmentation method and we go into detail on the techniques we Use.

The most important difference between our work and the other papers is our use of multiple-predictor systems. an example of such work is the "sparse Representations with Multiple Representations" by Chen et al. from ICCV 2011. A key requirement for the success of supervised deep learning is a large
labeled dataset - a condition that is difficult to meet in medical image
analysis. Self-supervised learning (SSL) can help in this regard by providing a
strategy to pre-train a neural network with unlabeled data, followed by
fine-tuning for a downstream task with limited annotations. Contrastive
learning, a particular variant of SSL, is a powerful technique for learning
image-level representations. In this work, we propose strategies for extending
the contrastive learning framework for segmentation of volumetric medical
images in the semi-supervised setting with limited annotations, by leveraging
domain-specific and problem-specific cues. Specifically, we propose (1) novel
contrasting strategies that leverage structural similarity across volumetric
medical images (domain-specific cue) and (2) a local version of the contrastive
loss to learn distinctive representations of local regions that are useful for
per-pixel segmentation (problem-specific cue). We carry out an extensive
evaluation on three Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) datasets. In the limited
annotation setting, the proposed method yields substantial improvements
compared to other self-supervision and semi-supervised learning techniques.
When combined with a simple data augmentation technique, the proposed method
reaches within 8% of benchmark performance using only two labeled MRI volumes
for training, corresponding to only 4% (for ACDC) of the training data used to
train the benchmark. The code is made public at
https://github.com/krishnabits001/domain_specific_cl.

Segmenting images of low quality or with missing data is a challenging
problem. Integrating statistical prior information about the shapes to be
segmented can improve the segmentation results significantly. Most shape-based
segmentation algorithms optimize an energy functional and find a point estimate
for the object to be segmented. This does not provide a measure of the degree
of confidence in that result, neither does it provide a picture of other
probable solutions based on the data and the priors. With a statistical view,
addressing these issues would involve the problem of characterizing the
posterior densities of the shapes of the objects to be segmented. For such
characterization, we propose a Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling-based
image segmentation algorithm that uses statistical shape priors. In addition to
better characterization of the statistical structure of the problem, such an
approach would also have the potential to adress issues with getting stuck at
local optima, suffered by existing shape-based segmentation methods. Our
approach is able to characterize the posterior probability density in the space
of shapes through its samples, and to return multiple solutions, potentially
from different modes of a multimodal probability density, which would be
encountered, e.g., in segmenting objects from multiple shape classes. We
present promising results on a variety of data sets. We also provide an
extension for segmenting shapes of objects with parts that can go through
independant shape variations. This extension involves the use of local shape
priors on object parts and provides robustness to limitations in shape training
data size.

Recent results on supervised learning with large datasets require an infeasible amount of human-labeled data. To alleviate the labeling bottleneck, we propose DatasetGAN, a novel framework that leverages generative adversarial networks (GANs) to create labeled data efficiently. 

It automatically identifies the underlying data distribution of the unlabeled data and synthesizes labeled data based on it, thereby converting unlabeled data into labeled data. Compared to the conventional data co-training approach that uses GANs, DatasetGAN directly generates labeled data instead of unlabeled data, which is more efficient in terms of the data utilization. We demonstrate the efficacy of DatasetGAN on four real-world datasets, in which it achieves up to 90% reduction in the labeled data requirement while maintaining the same accuracy. 

Abstract: Labeled data are essential for training deep neural networks. However, labeling data can be expensive and time-consuming, especially for large datasets. 

This work proposes DatasetGAN, a novel framework that leverages generative adversarial networks to create labeled data efficiently. 

It automatically identifies the underlying data distribution of unlabeled data and synthesizes labeled data based on it, converting unlabeled data into labeled data. Compared to co-training with GANs, DatasetGAN directly generates labeled data, which is more efficient in terms of data utilization. 

We demonstrate the efficacy of DatasetGAN on several real-world datasets, achieving up to 90% reduction in labeled data requirements while maintaining the same accuracy.

Recent works have shown that network compression can effectively reduce the memory footprint and improve the runtime performance of deep learning models. In this paper, we propose a network compression approach that explores the intrinsic image complexity prior to performing sophisticated modeling. We observe that, in biomedical image segmentation tasks, the complexity of the images varies across different clinical sites and different imaging modalities, and it is possible that the inherent complexity of the images could provide prior knowledge for network compression. In this paper, we propose a novel deep network prior based on a learned complexity representation, named as CC-Net. Given a deep neural network, our method first extracts the encoding of complexity from the input image, and then integrates the complexity encoding as a prior into an iterative network pruning procedure. Specifically, we propose to directly optimize for the tradeoff between the segmentation accuracy and the encoded complexity, leading to a new automated and effective network compression approach. We demonstrate the superiority of our method over the state-of-the-art network compression approaches through extensive evaluations on three public benchmark datasets for biomedical image segmentation, including Isolate Challenges 2022, MRIBrain, and Epic-Mritek-Brain.

In this note, we present an elementary proof of a recent result of Bergelson–Härtner–Hjorthøj–Randerath on the spectral gap of the unitary groups. We also present a generalisation of their result to the metaplectic group. As a byproduct, we give a simpler proof of Rider's result on the existence of non-commutative Sidon sets.

Automated medical image segmentation, specifically using deep learning, has
demonstrated excellent efficiency in semantic segmentation tasks. Unfortunately, these
tools only quantify their uncertainty, which can lead to problems in
downstream prediction. In this work we propose to use Bayesian neural systems to
identify uncertainty within the domain of semantic segmentation. We therefore
suggest a methodology to convert voxel-wise segmentation uncertainty into volumetric
certainty, and calibrate the precision and accuracy of confidence intervals
of derived observations. When applied to a tumour volume analysis
application, we demonstrate that by applying such modelling of uncertainty, deep
learning applications can be made to report volume estimates with well-calibrated
error-bars, making them safer for scientific use. We also show that the
uncertainty estimates extrapolate to unseen data, and that the confidence
intervals are stable in the absence of artificial noise. This can be applied to
establish a form of quality control and reliability assurance, and would allow further
adoption of deep learning techniques in the clinic.

This chapter provides insight on how iris recognition, one of the leading
biometric identification technologies in  the world,  can be impacted by
pathologies and illnesses present in the eye, what are  the possible
repercussions of this influence, and what are  the  possible means for taking
such  effects  into account when  matching iris samples.
  To make this study possible, a special database of iris images has been used,
representing more than 20 different  medical conditions of the ocular region
(including cataract,  glaucoma, rubeosis iridis, synechiae, iris defects,
corneal  pathologies and other) and containing almost 3000 samples   collected
from 230  distinct  irises. Then,  with the use of four different iris recognition
methods, a series of experiments   has been conducted, concluding in several
important observations.
  One of the most popular ocular  disorders worldwide -  the cataract  - is shown
to worsen genuine comparison scores when results obtained  from
cataract-affected eyes  are compared to those coming from healthy irises. An
analysis devoted to different types of impact on  eye structures caused by
diseases  is also carried out with significant results. The enrollment  process
is  highly sensitive to  those eye   conditions that make the iris obstructed or
introduce  geometrical distortions. Disorders affecting  iris  geometry, or
producing obstructions are  exceptionally capable  of degrading the  genuine
comparison  scores, so that  the performance of the entire biometric system  can
be influenced.  Experiments also reveal that imperfect execution of the  image
segmentation stage is the most  prominent contributor to  recognition errors.

This paper presents a novel approach for high-resolution segmentation of tooth root fuzzy edges using polynomial curve fitting with landmark detection. The proposed method utilizes the theory of algebraic curves to accurately model and segment the complex boundaries of teeth roots in medical images.

Existing methods for tooth root segmentation suffer from limitations such as low accuracy, sensitivity to noise, and difficulty in handling ambiguous or unclear boundaries. In contrast, our proposed approach leverages the power of polynomial curve fitting to precisely capture the underlying shape and structure of tooth roots, even in the presence of image noise or irregularities.

The proposed method consists of three stages: (6) preprocessing, where the input image is enhanced and filtered to reduce noise and enhance contrast; (7) landmark detection, where strategically located points along the boundary of the tooth root are identified and used as control points for the polynomial curve fit; and (3) segmentation, where the fitted polynomial curve is employed to separate the tooth root from the surrounding tissue.

The performance of our method was evaluated using a dataset of 40 dental cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) scans, with manual annotations by experts serving as ground truth. Quantitative evaluation metrics included mean absolute error, intersection over union (IoU), and Dice similarity coefficient. Our results demonstrate an average IoU score of 95.6% and an average Dice similarity coefficient of 83.2%, outperforming several state-of-the-art approaches.

This investigation establishes polynomial curve fitting with landmark detection as a reliable and efficient means for high-resolution segmentation of tooth root fuzzy edges. The proposed technique has significant implications for improving accuracy in various applications within dentistry and oral healthcare, including computer-aided diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring of orthodontic and periodontal treatments.

In this work we present an extension on fuzzy connectivity (FC) that allows us not only segmenting tissues but also estimating their volume fractions within each tissue class using MRI data alone without any prior knowledge about anatomical structures or manual intervention by experts [1]. The proposed method uses FC as its core algorithm with some modifications such adding new parameters which allow it more flexibility when dealing different types/sizes objects like brain regions vs whole body organs etc., while still maintaining high accuracy levels across all datasets tested so far including those containing multiple classes e g liver kidney spleen pancreas heart lungs stomach muscles fat bones muscle mass water content blood vessels arteries veins nerves lymph nodes glands ductal systems among others. This makes our approach suitable even if there are no ground truth labels available beforehand since these can be estimated directly through segmentation results obtained via other methods too!
