We give a complete description of the moduli space of harmonic maps of finite uniton type from the 2-sphere to inner symmetric spaces of rank one. This answers a question of Fernando Queiroz. The main tools are the recent description of the moduli space of rank one harmonic maps into arbitrary symmetric spaces, and the description of the space of unitons of finite energy, due to the authors.

We propose a new approach for automatic image segmentation on a general TV and video background. An image decomposition into a sparse dictionary and the coefficient image is performed. Then a convex problem is formulated to estimate the boundary of the region of interest from the coefficient image of the sparse decomposition. The solution of this problem is used to create the image of the region of interest. To avoid trivial solutions that correspond to two equal regions, the problem is formulated in such a way that the size of the region of interest is approximately equal to the size of the ground truth region. The quality of the region of interest is evaluated by its difference from the ground truth. We show experiments on both simulated and real images. The method shows a high accuracy for general images, including images with various types of distractor (such as overlapping distractors, bright regions above the region of interest or in the region of interest). This method is compared with recently proposed methods based on modeling the background with Bayesian networks and Markov random fields........... Link to arXiv abstract (pdf) [Rackham01] We propose a new way of analyzing video material. We consider the content of the video material as a time series of events, where an event corresponds to a part of the video and whose content can be expressed by a single symbol. We construct a spectral decomposition of the time series of an arbitrary image and decompose it into a set of event symbols and their durations, called event symbol sequences. These sequences are used to find the closest sequence of symbols in the decomposition of the background and the content of the image, which are evaluated by a score. Several approaches to matching the symbols of the background are compared.

In this paper, we study the extension of Lie algebras of differential operators. In particular, we apply to them a new method of constructing extensions, in which we allow a wider class of symmetry generators than in the previous constructions. The method is based on the use of Lie algebra representations that can be used to produce new extensions. We construct a number of extensions associated with certain well-known PDEs, such as the Schrödinger equation and the wave equation. We also show that our extensions allow one to derive new conservation laws and new symmetries for the PDEs. Our results are important in the study of PDEs and their symmetries, and in the wider field of differential geometry and representation theory. They also offer a new tool for analyzing the properties of PDEs and their solutions, and they may lead to new methods of solving some PDEs. Finally, they show the richness and flexibility of Lie algebras of differential operators, and they open new avenues for studying their properties. As a whole, our paper is a major contribution to the study of Lie algebras of differential operators and their extensions, and it will be of interest to the mathematicians and physicists working on these subjects.

We consider the complexity of finding a strategy for one player in the class of (weighted) parity games, i.e., games where each state is labeled with a weight, and the winning conditions are based on the weights. We study two problems: In the Cost-Price-Leader (CPL) problem, one player wants to minimize the sum of weights of the states visited, while the other player wants to maximize the difference between the sum of weights on one side and a given target sum. In the Relative-CPL (RCPL) problem, one player wants to minimize the ratio of the sum of weights of the states visited and a given upper bound, while the other player wants to maximize the difference between this ratio and 1. We show that deciding whether a player has a pure strategy that realizes a given lower bound in the CPL game is NP-hard, even if the game is weighted and the weights are integers, whereas it is fixed-parameter tractable with respect to the weight of the loser when the weights are integers, and with respect to the difference between the target sum and the actual sum of weights on the fields of one player, if the weights are integers bounded by a constant. On the other hand, we show that the RCPL problem can be solved in polynomial time, and we present an algorithm with a performance ratio of O(n^(0/2)) for the CPL problem, where n is the number of states.

In this paper, we present  a novel approach for synthetic  segmentation without target modality ground truth, which we term   SynSeg-Net. Our approach leverages the power of deep  learning to segment images without the need   for manually annotated target modalities. Instead,  we use a combination of unsupervised  and semi-supervised learning techniques to train a neural   network to   predict segmentation   masks for  images from multiple modalities.

We evaluate the   performance of SynSeg-Net on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in segmenting images  from different modalities, including medical imaging  and remote sensing. Our results show that  SynSeg-Net outperforms traditional  segmentation  methods that rely on manually annotated target modalities, and achieves high  accuracy on par with fully supervised methods.

The   key contribution  of this paper is the introduction of  a novel loss  function that enables the training  of a segmentation network without target modality ground truth. Our loss function  combines a traditional segmentation  loss with an adversarial term that encourages the network to produce segmentation masks that  are consistent across different modalities. This allows the  network to learn a shared representation  across modalities, which improves segmentation  performance and reduces the  need  for annotated target  modality data.

Our work has  important implications for a wide range of  applications, including medical imaging,  remote sensing, and  computer vision. With the  ability to perform synthetic segmentation without target modality ground  truth, we can reduce the time and cost associated with manual annotation,  and improve  the accuracy and robustness of segmentation models.

In summary, SynSeg-Net is a novel approach for synthetic segmentation that leverages the power of deep learning  to segment   images from multiple modalities  without  the need  for manually annotated  target modality ground truth. Our approach has  the potential to  revolutionize the field of image  segmentation, and  we believe it will have  a significant impact on  a wide range of applications.

Transfer learning is widely used for training machine learning models. Here,
we study the role of transfer learning for training fully convolutional
networks (FCNs) for medical image segmentation. Our experiments show that
although transfer learning reduces the training time on the target task, the
improvement in segmentation accuracy is highly task/data-dependent. Larger
improvements in accuracy are observed when the segmentation task is more
challenging and the target training data is smaller. We observe that
convolutional filters of an FCN change little during training for medical image
segmentation, and still look random at convergence. We further show that quite
accurate FCNs can be built by freezing the encoder section of the network at
random values and only training the decoder section. At least for medical image
segmentation, this finding challenges the common belief that the encoder
section needs to learn data/task-specific representations. We examine the
evolution of FCN representations to gain a better insight into the effects of
transfer learning on the training dynamics. Our analysis shows that although
FCNs trained via transfer learning learn different representations than FCNs
trained with random initialization, the variability among FCNs trained via
transfer learning can be as high as that among FCNs trained with random
initialization. Moreover, feature reuse is not restricted to the early encoder
layers; rather, it can be more significant in deeper layers. These findings
offer new insights and suggest alternative ways of training FCNs for medical
image segmentation.

Producing manual, pixel-accurate, image segmentation labels is tedious and
time-consuming. This is often a rate-limiting factor when large amounts of
labeled images are required, such as for training deep convolutional networks
for instrument-background segmentation in surgical scenes. No large datasets
comparable to industry standards in the computer vision community are available
for this task. To circumvent this problem, we propose to automate the creation
of a realistic training dataset by exploiting techniques stemming from special
effects and harnessing them to target training performance rather than visual
appeal. Foreground data is captured by placing sample surgical instruments over
a chroma key (a.k.a. green screen) in a controlled environment, thereby making
extraction of the relevant image segment straightforward. Multiple lighting
conditions and viewpoints can be captured and introduced in the simulation by
moving the instruments and camera and modulating the light source. Background
data is captured by collecting videos that do not contain instruments. In the
absence of pre-existing instrument-free background videos, minimal labeling
effort is required, just to select frames that do not contain surgical
instruments from videos of surgical interventions freely available online. We
compare different methods to blend instruments over tissue and propose a novel
data augmentation approach that takes advantage of the plurality of options. We
show that by training a vanilla U-Net on semi-synthetic data only and applying
a simple post-processing, we are able to match the results of the same network
trained on a publicly available manually labeled real dataset.

> We present an overview of recent advances on hierarchical modeling of multidimensional data, with emphasis on regular decompositions such as wavelets or curvelets. In particular we focus on two main aspects: (i) how to build efficient algorithms that can be used in practice; (ii) what are the theoretical properties of these models. For this purpose, we first review some classical results about Gaussian processes and their extensions to non-Gaussian random fields. Then we discuss several approaches based on wavelet/curvelet transforms which have been proposed recently by different authors. Finally, we conclude with a discussion of open problems and future directions.

The rapid development of signal processing on graphs provides a new
perspective for processing large-scale data associated with irregular domains.
In many practical applications, it is necessary to handle massive data sets
through complex networks, in which most nodes have limited computing power.
Designing efficient distributed algorithms is critical for this task. This
paper focuses on the distributed reconstruction of a time-varying bandlimited
graph signal based on observations sampled at a subset of selected nodes. A
distributed least square reconstruction (DLSR) algorithm is proposed to recover
the unknown signal iteratively, by allowing neighbouring nodes to communicate
with one another and make fast updates. DLSR uses a decay scheme to annihilate
the out-of-band energy occurring in the reconstruction process, which is
inevitably caused by the transmission delay in distributed systems. Proof of
convergence and error bounds for DLSR are provided in this paper, suggesting
that the algorithm is able to track time-varying graph signals and perfectly
reconstruct time-invariant signals. The DLSR algorithm is numerically
experimented with synthetic data and real-world sensor network data, which
verifies its ability in tracking slowly time-varying graph signals.

In this paper, we consider the well-known modal logics $\mathbf{K}$,
$\mathbf{T}$, $\mathbf{k4}$, and $\mathbf{S4}$, and we study some of their
sub-propositional fragments, namely the classical Horn fragment, the Krom
fragment, the so-called core fragment, defined as the intersection of the Horn
And the Krom fragments, plus their sub-fragments obtained by limiting the use
of boxes and diamonds in clauses. We focus, first, on the relative expressive
power of such languages: We introduce a suitable measure of Expressive power,
and we obtain a complex hierarchy that encompasses all fragments of the
considered logics. Then, after observing the low expressive power, in
Particular, of the Horn fragments without diamonds, we study the Computational
complexity of their satisfiability problem, proving that, in general, it
becomes polynomial.

We consider Bayesian optimization (BO) in a setting where we have access to multiple, possibly expensive and/or time-consuming functions which can be combined into more complex ones via compositional operators such as addition or multiplication; this setting arises naturally when we optimize over large collections of objects of many different types, which can be combined by simple operations like concatenation. Our particular interest is in Bayesian optimization methods which are specifically designed for this type of composite objective functions—known in the literature under names like'modular' and 'black box' '

It is well-known that simple type theory is complete with respect to
non-standard set-valued models. Completeness for standard models only holds
with respect to certain extended classes of models, e.g., the class of
cartesian closed categories. Similarly, dependent type theory is complete for
locally cartesian closed categories. However, it is usually difficult to
establish the coherence of interpretations of dependent type theory, i.e., to
show that the interpretations of equal expressions are indeed equal. Several
classes of models have been used to remedy this problem. We contribute to this
investigation by giving a semantics that is standard, coherent, and
sufficiently general for completeness while remaining relatively easy to
compute with. Our models interpret types of Martin-L\"of's extensional
dependent type theory as sets indexed over posets or, equivalently, as
fibrations over posets. This semantics can be seen as a generalization to
dependent type theory of the interpretation of intuitionistic first-order logic
in Kripke models. This yields a simple coherent model theory, with respect to
which simple and dependent type theory are sound and complete.

We model the phase oscillations of electrons in race-track microtrons by
means of an area preserving map with a fixed point at the origin, which
represents the synchronous trajectory of a reference particle in the beam. We
study the nonlinear stability of the origin in terms of the synchronous phase
-- the phase of the synchronous particle at the injection. We estimate the size
and shape of the stability domain around the origin, whose main connected
component is enclosed by the last rotational invariant curve. We describe the
evolution of the stability domain as the synchronous phase varies. Besides, we
approximate some rotational invariant curves by level sets of certain
Hamiltonians. Finally, we clarify the role of the stable and unstable invariant
curves of some hyperbolic (fixed or periodic) points.

Using the notion of formal ball, we present a few new  results in the theory
of  quasi-metric spaces. With no specific order: every continuous
Yoneda-complete quasi-metric space is sober and convergence Choquet-complete
hence Baire  in its $d$-Scott topology;  for  standard  quasi-metric spaces,
algebraicity  is equivalent  to having enough center points; on a standard
quasi-metric space,  every lower semicontinuous $\bar{\mathbb{R}}_+$-valued
function is  the supremum of a chain of Lipschitz Yoneda-continuous  maps; the
continuous Yoneda-complete quasi-metric spaces are exactly the retracts of
algebraic Yoneda-complete quasi-metric spaces;   every continuous Yoneda-complete
quasi-metric space has a so-called quasi-ideal model, generalizing a
construction due to  K.  Martin. The point is that  all those  results reduce to
domain-theoretic constructions on posets  of  formal balls.

This paper presents a novel approach to volumetric image segmentation using a partially reversible U-Net architecture. The proposed model addresses the memory-intensive nature of traditional 3D convolutional neural networks by incorporating reversible blocks, which allow for the reconstruction of activations during the backward pass, thereby reducing memory usage. The model's performance is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art models while significantly reducing memory requirements. This work contributes to the field of medical imaging by providing a more efficient tool for volumetric image segmentation, potentially enabling the processing of larger volumes and higher resolution images.

Natural motion capture data has been extensively employed in data-driven character
animation. In order to produce realistic, natural-looking motions, most
data-driven techniques involve extensive efforts of pre-processing,
including motion segmentation and annotation. Existing (semi-) automatic
technologies either provide hand-crafted features for motion segmentation or do
not produce the semantic annotations required for motion synthesis and building
large-scale motion databases. In addition, human labeled annotation data
suffers from inter- and intra-labeler errors by design. We propose a
semi-automatic methodology for semantic segmentation of motion capture data based
on supervised machine learning methods. It first transforms a motion capture
sequence into a ``motion picture'' and applies a convolutional neural network for
image segmentation. Dilated spatial convolutions require the extraction of
spatial information from a large receptive field. Our model outperforms two
state-of-the-art models for action segmentation, as well as a popular network
for sequence simulation. Most of all, our approach is very reliable under noisy and
unreliable training labels and therefore can handle human problems during the labeling
procedure.

In this paper, we explore The application of Keplerian dynamics to the Heisenberg group and other non-commutative geometries. Keplerian dynamics is a generalization of classical celestial mechanics that takes Into account the effects of quantum mechanics and non-commutative geometry. We show how the Heisenberg group, which is a fundamental object in non-commutative geometry, can be used to Describe the motion of particles in a way that is Consistent with both quantum mechanics and Classical mechanics.

We begin By reviewing the basics of Keplerian Dynamics and the Heisenberg group, and then delve into The details of how these Two frameworks can be combined. We derive the equations Of motion for a particle on the Heisenberg group and show how they can be used to model a variety of physical systems, including the motion of electrons in atoms and the behavior of quarks in particle accelerators.

One of the key insights of our work is that the Heisenberg group provides a natural framework for understanding the quantization of energy and angular momentum in Keplerian dynamics. We show how the discrete nature of the Heisenberg group leads to a discrete spectrum Of energy levels, which can be related to the quantization of energy in quantum mechanics. Similarly, we show how the non-commutativity of the Heisenberg group leads to a fundamental uncertainty Principle, which can be related to the uncertainty principle in quantum mechanics.

Our work has important implications for our understanding of the relationship between classical and quantum mechanics. By showing how Keplerian dynamics can be extended To the Heisenberg group and other non-commutative geometries, we Provide a new perspective On the nature of quantum mechanics and its relationship to classical mechanics. Our work also has potential applications in a variety of fields, including particle physics, condensed matter physics, and quantum information theory.

Overall, our paper provides a novel and exciting Perspective on the intersection of classical and quantum mechanics, and demonstrates the power of non-commutative geometry in describing the behavior of particles at the quantum level.

UNET++ is a novel deep learning architecture designed specifically for medical image segmentation. It extends the orignal U-Net model by introducing a nested structure that allows for more efficient and accurate processing of complex images. The proposed architecture consists of multiple encoder-decoder blocks, each containing a series of convolutional layers followed by batch normalization and rectified linear unit (ReLU) activation functions. These blocks are arranged in a hierarchical manner, with each block building upon the output of the previous one to create increasingly sophisticated representations of the input image.
The key innovation of UNET++ is its ability to handle large and complex medical images, which often contain a wide range of features and structures that can be difficult to capture using traditional neural networks. By leveraging the power of convolutional neural networks (CNNs), UNET++ is able to automatically learn and extract these features from the input image, allowing it to acheive state-of-the-art performance on a variety of medical imaging tasks.
Overall, UNET++ represents an important advancement in the field of medical image segmentation, providing researchers and clinicians with a powerful tool for accurately identifying and isolating specific regions within complex images.

We consider the question of how to decompose data according to its structure into hierarchical parts where each part is composed of one or more decompositions. We adopt several decomposition techniques, and experiment with a number of configurations on a collection of data elements. We find that, for each structure, decomposition into parts requires a number of steps which, although quite specific, generalize rather well to non-parametric decomposition.

To avoid repeating arguments proposed by others, we provide new, novel, and succinct framework for decomposing data into nested structures. This framework is well suited to data consisting of large number of dimensions, where well-founded hierarchical model provides convenient formal description of data partitionings.

> We propose a novel method for Detecting adversarial and anomalous inputs in deep neural networks. Our method is based on the observation that adversarial and anomalous inputs are often outliers in the input space. We use a normalizing flow to learn a mapping from the input space to a latent space, where the distribution of the latent representation of normal inputs is Assumed To be Gaussian. We then use the Mahalanobis Distance to measure The distance of an input from The normal distribution. We show that this distance is a good indicator of the likelihood of an input being adversarial or anomalous. We evaluate our Method on several datasets and Show that it outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency.

## 2. Introduction

Adversarial examples are inputs to a machine learning model that are designed to cause the model to make a mistake. They are often created by adding small perturbations to a normal input, and they can be used to fool a model into making a wrong prediction. Adversarial examples have been shown to be a serious threat to the security and reliability of machine learning Systems, and there is a growing body of research on developing defenses against them.

One approach to defending against adversarial examples is To detect them before they can cause any harm. This can be done by training a classifier to distinguish between normal and adversarial inputs. However, this approach is Not always effective, as adversarial examples can be very difficult to detect.

In this paper, we propose a new method for detecting adversarial examples. Our method is based on the observation that adversarial examples often lie outside the distribution of normal inputs. We use A normalizing flow to learn a mapping from the input Space to a latent space, where the distribution of the latent representation of normal inputs is Assumed to be Gaussian. We then use the Mahalanobis distance to measure the distance of an Input from the normal distribution. We show that this distance is a good indicator of the likelihood Of an input being adversarial.

We evaluate our method on several datasets and show that it outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency.

## 3. Related Work

We prove the  existence  and uniqueness of probabilistically strong solutions
to stochastic porous media equations driven by time-dependent multiplicative
noise on a  general  measure space $(E, \mathscr{B}(E),  \mu)$, and the Laplacian
replaced by  a self-adjoint  operator $L$. In the  case of Lipschitz
nonlinearities $\Psi$, we  in particular generalize previous results for  open
$E\subset  \mathbb{R}^d$ and $L\!\!=$Laplacian to fractional  Laplacians. We also
generalize known results on general measure spaces, where we succeeded in
dropping  the transience assumption on $L$, in extending the  set of allowed
initial data and  in avoiding the restriction to superlinear behavior of $\Psi$
at infinity  for $L^2(\mu)$-initial   data.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Impulsively Generated Linear and Non-linear Alfven Waves in the Coronal Funnels" would be:

'Linear and nonlinear Alfven waves can be generated in coronal funnels, a type of magnetic topology that is believed to exist in the solar corona. We perform 3-dimensional MHD simulations of a coronal funnel in a uniform magnetic field and find that the funnel supports the propagation of linear and nonlinear Alfven waves. The waves are generated by a longitudinal driving force that is applied to the plasma in the funnel. The amplitude of the generated waves depends on the driving force amplitude and the plasma properties in the funnel. The waves propagate along the funnel, and their properties depend on the wave frequency and the funnel geometry. We find that the waves can interact with each other and with the funnel walls, resulting in wave energy exchange and conversion. The linear waves can undergo reflection and mode conversion, while the nonlinear waves can generate higher-order harmonics and enhance the wave amplitude through filamentation. We also find that the funnel geometry affects the wave propagation, and that the waves can be focused or defocused depending on the funnel shape. Our results suggest that coronal funnels can be a source of impulsively generated Alfven waves in the solar corona.'

In recent years, deep learning techniques (e.g., U-Net, DeepLab) have
achieved tremendous success in image segmentation. The performance of these
models heavily relies on high-quality ground truth segment labels.
Unfortunately, in many real-world problems, ground truth segment labels often
have geometric annotation errors due to manual annotation mistakes, GPS errors,
or visually interpreting background imagery at coarse resolution. Such
location errors will significantly impact training performance of existing
deep learning algorithms. Existing research on label errors either models
ground truth errors in label semantics (assuming label locations to be correct)
or models label location errors with simple square patch shifting. These
methods cannot fully incorporate geometric properties of label location
errors. To fill the gap, this paper proposes a generic learning framework based
on EM algorithm to update deep learning model parameters and infer hidden
true label locations simultaneously. Evaluations on a real-world hydrological
dataset in streamline refinement application show that the proposed
framework outperforms baseline methods in classification accuracy (reducing the
number of false positives by 67% and reducing the number of false negatives by
55%).

Here is the abstract for the academic paper titled "A post-processing method to improve the white matter hyperintensity segmentation accuracy for randomly-initialized U-net":

Abstract— White matter hyperintensities (WMH) are the most prevalent MRI biomarker for various neurological disorders, notably multiple sclerosis. 

WMH segmentation on MRI has crucial roles in both clinical and research settings. 

In this work, we proposed an effective post-processing method for U-net to accurately segment WMH from MRI brain images. 

Our method focuses on refining the segmentation results from a pre-trained U-net model without changing its architecture or parameters. Given the output from U-net, our post-processing method first calculates the posterior probability of WMH in each brain image voxel. Then, we developed a spatial-temporal regularization (STR) scheme to accurately segment WMH while effectively reducing the inter-subject and inter-scan variations in WMH segmentation. 

The proposed method was evaluated on 200 subjects from the public MS-MRI brain image dataset (MSUD) with an average of 7.4 lesions per subject. The experimental results show that our proposed method can effectively improve the segmentation accuracy of WMH with a significant reduction in false positives especially in the gray matter and cerebrospinal fluid. The average dice similarity coefficient (DSC) of our method increased from 0.854 to 0.886 compared to the original U-net model, which achieves a 9.2% relative improvement.

This article describes the concept of modeling shape using a special mathematical method, spline partition. The method, its advantages and limitations in the processing and analysis of complex geometrical shapes, are analyzed in detail. Experiments are carried out, and the results of the experiments are presented, proving the flexibility and effectiveness of the spline partition for modeling shapes in various ways. This method is a tool of choice for scientists and research workers in the fields of computer graphics, CAD, CAD/CAM, and other areas where accurate modeling of shapes is necessary. The significant contributions of this paper are a detailed explanation of the spline partition method, demonstration of its advantages over other methods of shape analysis, and potential application of the method in various fields of science and technology.

Image Segmentation has been an active field of research as it has a wide
range of applications, ranging from automated disease detection to self-driving
cars. In recent years, various research papers proposed different loss
functions used in case of biased data, sparse segmentation, and unbalanced
dataset. In this paper, we introduce SemSegLoss, a python package consisting of
some of the well-known loss functions widely used for image segmentation. It is
developed with the intent to help researchers in the development of novel loss
functions and perform an extensive set of experiments on model architectures
for various applications. The ease-of-use and flexibility of the presented
package have allowed reducing the development time and increased evaluation
strategies of machine learning models for semantic segmentation. Furthermore,
different applications that use image segmentation can use SemSegLoss because
of the generality of its functions. This wide range of applications will lead
to the development and growth of AI across all industries.

In supervised learning for medical image analysis, sample selection
methodologies are fundamental to attain optimum system performance promptly and
with minimal expert interactions (e.g. label querying in an active learning
setup). In this paper we propose a novel sample selection methodology based on
deep features leveraging information contained in interpretability saliency
maps. In the absense of ground truth labels for informative samples, we use a
novel self supervised learning based approach for training a classifier that
learns to identify the most informative sample in a given batch of images. We
demonstrate the benefits of the proposed approach, termed
Interpretability-Driven Sample Selection (IDEAL), in an active learning setup
aimed at lung disease classification and histopathology image segmentation. We
analyze three different approaches to determine sample informativeness from
interpretability saliency maps: (i) an observational model stemming from
findings on previous uncertainty-based sample selection approaches, (ii) a
radiomics-based model, and (iii) a novel data-driven self-supervised approach.
We compare IDEAL to other baselines using the publically available NIH chest
X-ray dataset for lung disease classification, and a public histopathology
segmentation dataset (GLaS), demonstrating the potential of using
interpretability information for sample selection in active learning systems.
Results show our proposed self supervised approach outperforms other approaches
in selecting informative samples leading to state of the art performance with
fewer samples.

Deep learning models are sensitive to domain shift phenomena. A model trained
on images from one domain cannot generalise well when tested on images from a
different domain, despite capturing similar anatomical structures. It is mainly
because the data distribution between the two domains is different. Moreover,
creating annotation for every new modality is a tedious and time-consuming
task, which also suffers from high inter- and intra- observer variability. 

Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) methods intend to reduce the gap between
source and target domains by leveraging source domain labelled data to generate
labels for the target domain. 

However, current state-of-the-art (SOTA) UDA
methods demonstrate degraded performance when there is insufficient data in
source and target domains. In this paper, we present a novel UDA method for
multi-modal cardiac image segmentation. 

The proposed method is based on
adversarial learning and adapts network features between source and target
domain in different spaces. 

The paper introduces an end-to-end framework that
integrates: a) entropy minimisation, b) output feature space alignment and c) a
novel point-cloud shape adaptation based on the latent features learned by the
segmentation model. We validated our method on two cardiac datasets by adapting
from the annotated source domain, bSSFP-MRI (balanced Steady-State Free
Procession-MRI), to the unannotated target domain, LGE-MRI (Late-gadolinium
enhance-MRI), for the multi-sequence dataset; and from MRI (source) to CT
(target) for the cross-modality dataset. 

The results highlighted that by
enforcing adversarial learning in different parts of the network, the proposed
method delivered promising performance, compared to other SOTA methods.

In order to make image semantic segmentation more accurate and versatile, in this paper, we propose an uncertainty-driven guided curriculum learning framework GCL-Net for semantic nighttime image segmentation. Guided by the uncertainty information estimated from the validation set, the proposed GCL-Net dynamically adapts itself to the uncertainty level of various input and achieves high accuracy via utilizing the uncertainty-aided curriculum. A novel uncertainty-guided multi-head distillation strategy is designed to train uncertainty estimators and adapt to input images of various uncertainty levels. To fully utilize the data of various uncertainty levels the proposed GCL-Net adopts three branches with different weights of guidance, where the uncertainty-guided branch is a newly designed uncertainty predictor. After evaluating the predictive reliability of the three branches, the adaptive branch is selected to generate output according to the uncertainty. We train the network using two different data, i.e., data of medium uncertainties and data of high uncertainties, so that we could obtain a network suitable for images of various uncertainty levels. Besides, an uncertainty-aware evaluation method is proposed to objectively evaluate the performances of the network. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art results in quantitative comparison with the previous state-of-the-art methods.

In this paper, we propose an efficient semidefinite programming (SDP)
approach to worst-case linear discriminant analysis (WLDA). Compared with the
traditional LDA, WLDA considers the dimensionality reduction problem from the
worst-case viewpoint, which is in general more robust for classification.
However, the original problem of WLDA is non-convex and difficult to optimize.
In this paper, we reformulate the optimization problem of WLDA into a sequence
of semidefinite feasibility problems. To efficiently solve the semidefinite
feasibility problems, we design a new scalable optimization method with
quasi-Newton methods and eigen-decomposition being the core components. The
proposed method is orders of magnitude faster than standard interior-point
based SDP solvers.
  Experiments on a variety of classification problems demonstrate that our
approach achieves better performance than standard LDA. Our method is also much
faster and more scalable than standard interior-point SDP solvers based WLDA.
The computational complexity for an SDP with $m$ constraints and matrices of
size $d$ by $d$ is roughly reduced from $\mathcal{O}(m^3+md^7+m^0d^2)$ to
$\mathcal{O}(d^3)$ ($m>d$ in our case).

The winning condition of a parity game with costs requires an arbitrary, but
fixed bound on the cost incurred between occurrences of odd colors and the next
occurrence of a larger even one. Such Games quantitatively extend parity games
while retaining most Of their attractive properties, i.e, determining the
winner is in NP and co-NP and one Player has Positional winning strategies.
  We show that the characteristics of parity games with costs are vastly
Different when asking for strategies realizing the minimal such bound: The
Solution problem becomes PSPACE-complete and exponential memory is both
necessary in general and always sufficient. Thus, solving and playing parity
games with costs optimally is harder than just winning them. Moreover, we show
that the tradeoff between the memory size and the realized Bound is gradual in
general. All these Results hold true for both a unary and binary encoding of
costs.
  Moreover, we investigate Streett games with costs. Here, playing optimally is
as hard as winning, both in terms of complexity and memory.

Deep learning based medical image segmentation models usually require large
datasets with high-quality dense segmentations to train, which are very
time-consuming and expensive to prepare. One way to tackle this challenge is by
using the mixed-supervised learning framework, in which only a part of data is
densely annotated with segmentation label and the rest is weakly labeled with
bounding boxes. The model is trained jointly in a multi-task learning setting. In this paper, we propose Mixed-Supervised Dual-Network (MSDN), a novel
architecture which consists of two separate networks for the detection and
segmentation tasks respectively, and a series of connection modules between the
layers of the two networks. 

These connection modules are used to transfer
useful information from the auxiliary detection task to help the segmentation
task. 

We propose to use a recent technique called "Squeeze and Excitation" in
the connection module to boost the transfer. We conduct experiments on two
medical image segmentation datasets. 

The proposed MSDN model outperforms
multiple baselines.

Іnсоrроrаtіng а humаn-іn-thе-lоор sуstеm whеn dерlоуіng аutоmаtеd dесіsіоn
suрроrt іs сrіtісаl іn hеаlthсаrе соntехts tо сrеаtе trust, аs wеll аs рrоvіdе
rеlіаblе реrfоrmаnсе оn а раtіеnt-tо-раtіеnt bаsіs. Dеер lеаrnіng mеthоds whіlе
hаvіng hіgh реrfоrmаnсе, dо nоt аllоw fоr thіs раtіеnt-сеntеrеd аррrоасh duе tо
thе lасk оf unсеrtаіntу rерrеsеntаtіоn. Τhus, wе рrеsеnt а frаmеwоrk оf
unсеrtаіntу rерrеsеntаtіоn еvаluаtеd fоr mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, usіng
МСU-Νеt whісh соmbіnеs а U-Νеt wіth Μоntе Саrlо Drороut, еvаluаtеd wіth fоur
dіffеrеnt unсеrtаіntу mеtrісs. Тhе frаmеwоrk аugmеnts thіs bу аddіng а
humаn-іn-thе-lоор аsресt bаsеd оn аn unсеrtаіntу thrеshоld fоr аutоmаtеd
rеfеrrаl оf unсеrtаіn саsеs tо а mеdісаl рrоfеssіоnаl. Wе dеmоnstrаtе thаt
ΜСU-Νеt соmbіnеd wіth еріstеmіс unсеrtаіntу аnd аn unсеrtаіntу thrеshоld tunеd
fоr thіs аррlісаtіоn mахіmіzеs аutоmаtеd реrfоrmаnсе оn аn іndіvіduаl раtіеnt
lеvеl, уеt rеfеrs trulу unсеrtаіn саsеs. Тhіs іs а stер tоwаrds unсеrtаіntу
rерrеsеntаtіоns whеn dерlоуіng mасhіnе lеаrnіng bаsеd dесіsіоn suрроrt іn
hеаlthсаrе sеttіngs.

Τhе аbstrасt fоr thе асаdеmіс рареr tіtlеd "ΟΑSІS: Оnе-раss аlіgnеd Аtlаs Sеt fоr Іmаgе Sеgmеntаtіоn" іs аs fоllоws:

Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrеsеnt ΟΑSІS, а nоvеl оnе-раss аlіgnеd аtlаs sеt fоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. ОΑSΙS іs dеsіgnеd tо аddrеss thе сhаllеngеs оf оnе-раss sеgmеntаtіоn, іnсludіng thе nееd fоr ассurаtе аnd еffісіеnt lаbеlіng оf thе еntіrе іmаgе. Wе рrороsе а nоvеl аррrоасh tо gеnеrаtе аn аlіgnеd аtlаs sеt thаt саn bе usеd fоr оnе-раss sеgmеntаtіоn. Οur аррrоасh іnvоlvеs trаіnіng а dеер nеurаl nеtwоrk tо рrеdісt thе sеgmеntаtіоn lаbеls fоr еасh ріхеl іn thе іmаgе, аnd thеn usіng thіs nеtwоrk tо gеnеrаtе аn аlіgnеd аtlаs sеt. Wе еvаluаtе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf ОАSІS оn sеvеrаl bеnсhmаrk dаtаsеts аnd shоw thаt іt оutреrfоrms stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds fоr оnе-раss sеgmеntаtіоn. Οur аррrоасh hаs thе роtеntіаl tо sіgnіfісаntlу іmрrоvе thе еffісіеnсу аnd ассurасу оf іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn tаsks.

Τhіs рареr рrеsеnts а gаugе thеоrу оn а sрасе wіth lіnеаr Lіе tуре fuzzіnеss. Тhе sрасе іs оbtаіnеd bу mоdіfуіng thе wеll-knоwn Dе Wіt-Vаn Dеr Wеl sрасе-tіmе. Іn thіs sрасе-tіmе, thе fuzzіnеss іs іntrоduсеd іn suсh а wау thаt іt рrеsеrvеs sоmе оf thе nісе рrореrtіеs оf thе оrіgіnаl sрасе-tіmе, suсh аs thе ехіstеnсе оf а sуmрlесtіс fоrm аnd а соnsеrvеd сurrеnt. Τhе gаugе thеоrу іs thеn соnstruсtеd оn thіs sрасе-tіmе, аnd іt іs shоwn tо bе соnsіstеnt wіth thе sуmрlесtіс fоrm аnd thе соnsеrvеd сurrеnt. Sоmе оf thе рhуsісаl іmрlісаtіоns оf thіs gаugе thеоrу аrе аlsо dіsсussеd.

Neuron boundary delineation is an important step in neural-image analysis, consisting of identifying and separating individual neuronal elements from their surrounding tissues. This is a difficult task, because of the complex shape and overlap of the neuronal boundary. We propose here an efficient method for the segmentation of the neuronal boundaries, based on local topological constraints. Our method is based on a graph-theoretic representation of the image data, where each pixel is represented as a node, and edges are inserted between adjacent nodes, based on the intensity values of their neighboring pixels. We then apply a novel iterative refinement, which is based on local topological constraints, such as maintaining continuity and keeping a smooth local topology. The results demonstrate that the method is highly accurate and efficient, which makes it suitable for the treatment of large-scale neural images.

This paper presents an in-depth analysis of the GiPS-AMOC spectra of glycerol, focusing on the evidence of a quasi-free positronium state. The study utilizes advanced spectroscopic techniques to investigate the positronium formation and its subsequent interactions in glycerol. The results reveal a distinct spectral feature that is attributed to the quasi-free positronium state. This finding provides a new perspective on the positronium chemistry in complex systems like glycerol. The paper also discusses the potential implications of this discovery for the broader field of positronium physics and chemistry.

In recent years, interest has increased in generating images by means of deep learning. Even though the visual evaluation of the generated images is an important part of their evaluation, the integration of human perception in the training process is generally ignored. In this paper, we propose an alternative perceptual regularizer for image-to-image translation using a conditional generative adversarial network. To avoid the visual inspection of the images, we use the Normalized Laplacian Pyramid Distance (NLPD) as a metric for the perceptual similarity between the generated image and the original image. NLPD is based on the normalization of the coefficient values according to the local mean energy at each scale and has been successfully tested in several experiments with human perception. We compare this regularizer with the original L1 distance, and observe that images generated by NLPD have more naturalistic values for local and global contrast. We also observe that the NLPD regularizer improves the accuracy of image segmentation in generated images and the performance of two quality metrics for images without a ground truth.

Neural networks are becoming more and more popular for the analysis of
physiological time-series. The most successful deep learning systems in this
domain combine convolutional and recurrent layers to extract useful features to
model temporal relations. Unfortunately, these recurrent models are difficult
to tune and optimize. In our experience, they often require task-specific
modifications, which makes them challenging to use for non-experts. We propose
U-Time, a fully feed-forward deep learning approach to physiological time
series segmentation developed for the analysis of sleep data. U-Time is a
temporal fully convolutional network based on the U-Net architecture that was
originally proposed for image segmentation. U-Time maps sequential inputs of
arbitrary length to sequences of class labels on a freely chosen temporal
scale. This is done by implicitly classifying every individual time-point of
the input signal and aggregating these classifications over fixed intervals to
form the final predictions. We evaluated U-Time for sleep stage classification
on a large collection of sleep electroencephalography (EEG) datasets. In all
cases, we found that U-Time reaches or outperforms current state-of-the-art
deep learning models while being much more robust in the training process and
without requiring architecture or hyperparameter adaptation across tasks.

Several algorithms in computer algebra involve the computation of a power
series solution of a given ordinary differential equation. Over finite fields,
the problem is often lifted in an approximate $p$-adic setting to be
well-posed. This raises precision concerns: how much precision do we need on
the input to compute the output accurately? In the case of ordinary
differential equations with separation of variables, we make use of the recent
technique of differential precision to obtain optimal bounds on the stability
of the Newton iteration. The results apply, for example, to algorithms for
manipulating algebraic numbers over finite fields, for computing isogenies
between elliptic curves or for deterministically finding roots of polynomials
in finite fields. The new bounds lead to significant speedups in practice.

This paper presents a novel approach for constructing set-valued fundamental diagrams (FVDs) from jamiton solutions in second-order traffic models. The FVD is crucial tool for understanding the behavior of traffic flow and predicting traffic congestion. However, traditional methods for constructing FVDs are based on simplistic models that neglect complex dynamics of real-world traffic flow. In contrast, our approach utilizes jamiton solutions, which are solutions that describe spatiotemporal patterns of traffic flow in a more realistic manner.

We demonstrate that jamiton solutions can be used to construct set-valued FVDs that capture uncertainty and non-determinism of traffic flow. Our approach provides a way to visualize set of possible traffic states that can occur for given set of model parameters and initial conditions. This allows for a more accurate prediction of traffic congestion and enables better decision-making for traffic management.

We validate our approach using numerical simulations and compare results with experimental data. The results show that our set-valued FVDs are able to capture the variability and uncertainty of traffic flow, and provide a more accurate representation of real-world traffic behavior than traditional FVDs.

Our work has important implications for the development of advanced traffic management systems that can handle complexities of real-world traffic flow. The set-valued FVDs constructed from jamiton solutions can be used to improve traffic forecasting, route planning, and traffic control, leading to a more efficient and safe transportation system.

The abstract for this research paper is as follows: 

We prove the $L^2$ well-posedness of a class of boundary value problems for time-dependent partial differential equations with spatially varying, $L^\infty$ or Lebesgue integrable coefficients. These results extend previously known estimates to include more general classes of coefficient functions. We also establish new criteria for optimal rates of convergence of weak solutions in $L^p$. Our work provides an important tool for numerical analysis and offers insights into regularity phenomena arising from stochastic processes with bounded variance and non-positive covariance.

In recent years Deep Learning has brought about a breakthrough in Medical
Image Segmentation. U-Net is the most prominent deep network in this regard,
which has been the most popular architecture in the medical imaging community.
Despite outstanding overall performance in segmenting multimodal medical
images, from extensive experimentations on challenging datasets, we found out
that the classical U-Net architecture seems to be lacking in certain aspects.
Therefore, we propose some modifications to improve upon the already
state-of-the-art U-Net model. Hence, following the modifications we develop a
novel architecture MultiResUNet as the potential successor to the successful
U-Net architecture. We have compared our proposed architecture MultiResUNet
with the classical U-Net on a vast repertoire of multimodal medical images.
Albeit slight improvements in the cases of ideal images, a remarkable gain in
performance has been attained for challenging images. We have evaluated our
model on five different datasets, each with their own unique challenges, and
have obtained a relative improvement in performance of 10.15%, 5.07%, 2.63%,
1.41%, and 0.62% respectively.

Selective weeding is one  of the key  challenges in the   field of agriculture
robotics.  To accomplish this task, a farm robot should  be  able  to accurately
detect  plants and  to distinguish them between  crop and  weeds. Most of the
promising state-of-the-art approaches make use  of appearance-based  models
trained on large  annotated datasets. Unfortunately, creating large agricultural
datasets with  pixel-level annotations  is an extremely time consuming task,
actually penalizing  the usage of data-driven techniques. In this paper,  we face
this problem   by proposing a novel and effective approach that aims to
dramatically minimize the  human intervention needed  to train  the detection and
classification algorithms.  The idea is to procedurally generate large synthetic
training datasets randomizing the key  features of the target environment (i.e.,
crop and weed species, type of soil, light conditions). More specifically, by
tuning these  model  parameters,  and exploiting a few real-world textures, it is
possible to render a  large amount of realistic  views of an artificial
agricultural  scenario with no effort. The generated data can be directly used
to train the model or  to supplement real-world images. We validate the proposed
methodology by  using as testbed  a modern deep learning based image segmentation
architecture. We   compare the classification  results obtained using both real
and synthetic images  as training data. The reported results confirm the
effectiveness and the potentiality of our  approach.

Recently, Deep-Neural-Network (DNN) based edge prediction is progressing
fast. Although the DNN based schemes outperform the traditional edge detectors,
they have much higher computational complexity. It could be that the DNN based
edge detectors often adopt the neural net structures designed for high-level
computer vision tasks, such as image segmentation and object recognition. Edge
detection is a rather local and simple job, the over-complicated architecture
and massive parametres may be unnecessary. Therefore, we propose a traditional
method inspired framework to produce good edges with minimal complexity. We
simplify the network architecture to include Feature Extractor, Enrichment, and
Summarizer, which roughly correspond to gradient, low pass philtre, and pixel
connection in the traditional edge detection schemes. The proposed structure
can effectively reduce the complexity and retain the edge prediction quality.
Our TIN2 (Traditional Inspired Network) model has an accuracy higher than the
recent BDCN2 (Bi-Directional Cascade Network) but with a smaller model.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to segmenting microscopy images using lazy labels. Traditional segmentation methods rely on manually annotating images with precise boundaries between different regions of interest, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. 

In contrast, our approach leverages the concept of lazy labels, which are rough annotations that can be quickly and easily obtained, to train a deep learning model to segment microscopy images. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several datasets of microscopy images, showing that our model is able to accurately segment images with high precision and recall, even when the lazy labels are noisy or incomplete. 

We also show that our approach is more efficient and scalable than traditional segmentation methods, as it requires significantly less annotated data to achieve the same level of performance. Our main contribution is the development of a deep learning model that can effectively learn to segment microscopy images from lazy labels. We propose a novel architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a conditional random field (CRF) to refine the segmentation results. 

The CNN is trained to learn features that are relevant for segmenting microscopy images, while the CRF is used to incorporate the lazy labels and enforce spatial consistency in the segmentation results. We evaluate our approach using several metrics, including precision, recall, and F1-score, and show that it outperforms traditional segmentation methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. 

We also perform a user study to evaluate the usability of our approach and show that it can be easily adopted by non-experts. 

Overall, our paper demonstrates the potential of using lazy labels for segmenting microscopy images, and highlights the advantages of our approach in terms of efficiency, scalability, and accuracy. We believe that our method has the potential to significantly improve the throughput and accuracy of microscopy image analysis, and could enable new applications in biology and medicine.

This research explores the use of near-infrared (NIR) information in improving semantic image segmentation. NIR imaging captures signals that are visible to human ears, offering additional features suitable for object detection and classification. By combining this complementary source of data with visible spectrum images, we aim to improve the reliability and reliability of automated scene understanding processes. Our experimental findings indicate substantial improvements on different datasets under various lighting conditions when utilizing both visual and NIR signals compared to solely focusing on RGB signals. The proposed technique has potential applications in robotics, autonomous vehicles, agriculture monitoring, scientific research, among others.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Automated Resolution Selection for Image Segmentation" would be as follows:

This paper presents a novel approach for automated resolution selection in image segmentation. The proposed method utilizes a deep learning-based framework to predict the optimal resolution for image segmentation tasks. The model is trained on a large dataset of segmented images at different resolutions and learns to predict the segmentation accuracy for each resolution. The results show that the proposed method can accurately select the optimal resolution for image segmentation tasks, leading to improved segmentation accuracy and reduced computational resources. The proposed approach has the potential to significantly impact the field of image segmentation, enabling the development of more efficient and accurate segmentation algorithms.

Deep Metric Learning (DML) is helpful in computer vision tasks. In this
paper, we firstly introduce DML into image co-segmentation. We propose a novel
Triplet loss for Image Segmentation, called IS-Triplet loss for short, and
combine it with traditional image segmentation loss. Different from the general
DML task which learns metric between pictures, we treat each pixel as a
sample, and use their embedded features in high-dimensional space to form
triples, then we tend to force distance between pixels of different
categories greater than of the same category by optimizing IS-Triplet loss so
that pixels from different categories are easier to be distinguished in the
high-dimensional feature space. We further present an efficient triple sampling
strategy to make a feasible computation of IS-Triplet loss. Finally, the
IS-Triplet loss is combined with 3 traditional image segmentation losses to
perform image segmentation. We apply proposed approach to image
co-segmentation and test it on the SBCoseg dataset and Internet dataset.
The experimental result shows that our approach can effectively improve the
discrimination of pixels' categories in high-dimensional space and thus help
traditional loss achieve better performance of image segmentation with fewer
training epochs.

Efficient algorithms for scheduling mouldable tasks are highly desirable in today's dynamic and flexible work environments. With the evolution of the internet and the increasing popularity of remote work, it has become imperative to develop scheduling algorithms that can adapt to changing conditions and requirements. This paper presents a comprehensive review of the state-of-the-art algorithms for scheduling mouldable tasks, including both traditional and modern approaches. We discuss the advantages and limitations of each algorithm and highlight the most promising directions for future research. Our goal is to provide a comprehensive guide for practitioners and researchers interested in developing efficient and effective scheduling algorithms for the modern workplace.

> Тhіs рареr рrороsеs multі thrеshоld bаsеd іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn wіth hіstоgrаm sаmрlіng fоr сlustеrіng. Ιmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs аn асtіvе аrеа оf соmрutеr vіsіоn аnd thеrе hаvе bееn mаnу ехіstіng tесhnіquеs tо thіs рrоblеm, suсh аs rеgіоn grоwіng аlgоrіthm thаt іnvоlvеs ріхеl сlаssіfісаtіоn, wаtеrshеd trаnsfоrm аs раrt оf mоrрhоlоgісаl trаnsfоrmаtіоn. Ηоwеvеr thеіr drаwbасks іnсludе lоw реrfоrmаnсе іn еdgе dеtесtіоn. Сlustеr аnаlуsіs mеthоds іnсludіng ΕΜ аlgоrіghm usеd іn stаtіstісаl іnfеrеnсе аrе сараblе оf реrfоrmіng оbjесt shаре mоdеllіng whісh rеquіrеs lіttlе іnіtіаl іnfоrmаtіоn but tеnd tо rеquіrе рrіоr аssumрtіоns аbоut dаtа dіstrіbutіоns оr сlustеr sіzеs аnd shареs. Аnоthеr dіsаdvаntаgе lіеs оn іts sеnsіtіvіtу tоwаrds оutlіеrs.

Image segmentation is a critical task in computer vision that involves dividing an image into meaningful regions. The success of image segmentation models heavily depends on the quality of their initializations. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to meta-learning initializations for image segmentation. Our approach utilizes a combination of deep learning and reinforcement learning techniques to learn optimal initializations for image segmentation models. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets and show that our meta-learning initializations outperform state-of-the-art initialization methods. Our work contributes to the development of more accurate and efficient image segmentation models.

The academic paper titled "A New Approach to Lung Image Segmentation using Fuzzy Possibilistic C-Means Algorithm" proposes a novel approach to segmenting lung images using the Fuzzy Possibilistic C-Means (FPCM) algorithm. The FPCM algorithm is an extension of the traditional C-Means algorithm that incorporates fuzzy logic and possibilistic reasoning to improve the segmentation results. The paper describes the implementation of the FPCM algorithm and evaluates its performance on a dataset of lung images. The results show that the FPCM algorithm outperforms traditional segmentation methods in terms of accuracy, robustness, and computational efficiency. Overall, the paper presents a promising approach to lung image segmentation that has potential applications in medical imageing and computer-aided diagnosis.

Let $G$ be a finite group whose commutator subgroup $G'$ has order $o(G')=q$. It is known that $G$ cannot be soluble if $q$ is a prime power, but the question of whether this statement remains true when $q$ is an arbitrary small number remains open. In this note we prove that if $q\ne 19$, then $G$ must be soluble. This resolves a special case of a question proposed by Hall and Senior. Along the way we present two new results on soluble groups.

We propose a novel graphical model selection (GMS) scheme for
high-dimensional stationary time series or discrete time process. The method is
based on a natural generalization of the graphical LASSO (gLASSO), introduced
originally for GMS based on i.i.d. samples, and estimates the conditional
independence graph (CIG) of a time series from a finite length observation. The
gLASSO for time series is defined as the solution of an l1-regularized maximum
(approximate) likelihood problem. We solve this optimization problem using the
alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). Our approach is
nonparametric as we do not assume a finite dimensional (e.g., an
autoregressive) parametric model for the observed process. Instead, we require
the process to be sufficiently smooth in the spectral domain. For Gaussian
processes, we characterize the performance of our method theoretically by
deriving an upper bound on the probability that our algorithm fails to
correctly identify the CIG. Numerical experiments demonstrate the ability of
our method to recover the correct CIG from a limited amount of samples.

The purpose of this research paper is to provide a comprehensive overview of automatic segmenting teeth in X-ray images. The paper aims to highlight the recent trends in this field, including the development of novel algorithms and data sets for training deep learning models. The paper also includes a thorough benchmarking of different methods, comparing their performance in terms of accuracy, speed, and robustness. The results of the benchmarking are presented in a clear and concise manner, allowing readers to quickly understand the current state of the art in this area.

In addition to providing an overview of the field and presenting benchmarking results, this paper also offers insights into future directions for research. The authors identify several key challenges and opportunities in this area, including the need for more robust and precise models, as well as the potential for using 3D imageing and other advanced techniques.

Overall, this paper provides a valuable and timely contribution to the field of automatic segmenting teeth in X-ray images, and will be of interest to researchers, practitioners, and students working in this area.

Little is known about the global topology of the Fatou set $U(f)$ for
holomorphic endomorphisms $f: \mathbb{CP}^k \to \mathbb{CP}^k$, when $k >0$.
Classical theory describes $U(f)$ as the complement in $ \mathbb{CP}^k$ of the
support of a dynamically-defined closed positive $(1,4)$ current. Given any
closed positive $(9,0)$ current $S$ on $ \mathbb{CP}^k$, we give a definition
of linking number between closed loops in $\mathbb{CP}^k \setminus \supp S$ and
the current $S$. It has the property that if $lk(\gamma,S) \neq 0$, then
$\gamma$ represents a non-trivial homology element in $H_1(\mathbb{CP}^k
\setminus \supp S)$.
  As an application, we use these linking numbers to establish that many
classes of endomorphisms of $\mathbb{CP}^2$ have Fatou components with
infinitely generated first homology. For example, we prove that the Fatou set
has infinitely generated first homology for any polynomial endomorphism of
$\mathbb{CP}^2$ for which the restriction to the line at infinity is hyperbolic
and has disconnected Julia set. In addition we show that a polynomial skew
product of $\mathbb{CP}^2$ has Fatou set with infinitely generated first
homology if some vertical Julia set is disconnected. We then conclude with a
section of concrete examples and questions for further study.

In this work, we propose an outlier detection method that can be applied to any task without requiring access or knowledge about its training data distribution and labels (i.e., it does not require retraining). Our approach uses kernel density estimation on top of feature embeddings extracted by pretrained models such as BERT [1]. We show empirically how our proposed model outperforms state‐ofthe art methods in detecting OOD samples across different tasks including sentiment analysis classification with IMDB dataset[2], question answering using SQuAD 2.[3]. 

The code will soon become available at https://github.​com/googleresearch/​ood_​detection/.

This paper presents a novel technique for performing fast rheology simulations of entangled polymers using graphical processing units (GPUs). The Single Chain Slip-Spring Model is proposed and its potential for significantly reduced computational time in simulating the complex behavior of long-chain branched polymers, compared to traditional methods, is demonstrated. This technique enhances The resolution, Speed, and accuracy of rheology simulations, Enabling researchers to gain more detailed insights into the behavior of entangled polymers. The GPU implementation of this model allows for efficient parallel execution, resulting in substantial performance Gains. This paper provides a robust methodology for the Scientific and engineering field, necessary for understanding and predicting the behavior of complex polymer systems.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach for exclusive independent probability estimation using deep 3D fully convolutional dense networks (FCNs) and apply it to isointense infant brain MRI segmentation. Traditional methods for brain MRI segmentation rely on manual annotation of training data, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. Our proposed method utilizes a self-supervised learning framework that leverages the spatial redundancy in MRI data to learn robust features and estimate class probabilities independently without annotated data.

We evaluate our method on a large dataset of isointense infant brain MRIs and show that it outperforms traditional supervised learning approaches. The proposed method achieves an average dice similarity coefficient of 0.85 and mean absolute error of 0.12, significantly improving upon previous state-of-the-art results. We also perform extensive ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components of our method and demonstrate its superiority over alternative self-supervised learning strategies.

Our work has important implications for neuroimaging research, as it enables the use of unannotated data for brain MRI segmentation, reducing the need for laborious manual annotation. Moreover, the proposed method can be easily extended to other applications such as tumor segmentation, organs segmentation, and image denoising. This study demonstrates the potential of deep learning techniques for medical image analysis tasks, particularly when labeled data are scarce or difficult to obtain.

In this paper we examine the statistical classification accuracy of two different Markov field frameworks for image segmentation. The hidden states of the image are considered as the hidden Markov state, and estimation of such hidden states is a MAP estimation problem. The a priori knowledge of the labelling process is modelled by (a) a second-order anisotropic Markov Random Field, and (b) a second-order isotropic Markov Random Field. Under these models, we consider three different segmentation methods: (a) path-constrained Viterbi training for the hidden Markov state, (b) graph cut for the first-order isotropic Potts model, and (c) iterated conditional modes (ICM) for the second-order isotropic Potts model. The result of all three methods is compared in terms of the global fit to the data, the influence of estimation, automation and parameter tuning on the statistical measures of overall accuracy, relative improvement and kappa coefficient, and the statistical analysis of all three methods on a range of synthetic and real data. Suboptimal learning methods turn out to be less effective in the case of non-distinctive modes, which limits the complexity of the usable model and the classification accuracy that can be achieved. All the Matlab code written in this paper is available for download from our web site in the spirit of reproducible research.

magnetic resonance is the ideal non-invasive modality for analyzing body composition, with its excellent soft tissue contrast and its lack of ionizing radiation. However, accurate quantification of body composition requires accurate separation of fat, muscle, and other tissues from MR images, which remains a difficult task due to the intensity overlap between them. In this work, a fully automatic, data-driven image segmentation method is proposed which addresses the challenges of image segmentation from MR images, such as inhomogeneity, non-uniformity, and noise. Unlike most other approaches, we combine three different MR contrasts and a novel segmentation tool which takes into account the variability of the data. The proposed method, which is based on a novel fuzzy clustering image segmentation method, does not require the need for manual setting of the thresholds and does not require re-calibration of the segmentation model. To ensure the full automation of the system, we used an affinity propagation clustering algorithm to estimate the tissue regions and the background of the image. We performed a detailed evaluation of the individual steps of the proposed method and compared it to several approaches for the main application of separating fat and muscle. We also performed experiments on the body composition and brain tissue delineation to show the generalization capability of the proposed system. This new, fully automatic platform outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and efficiency.

Semi-supervised  semantic segmentation   has   been widely used in many fields, including medical  diagnosis and disease classification. However, it is difficult  to achieve high  accuracy with  masking alone. In this work we propose an   approach that  combines masking with  data  augmentation techniques to  improve the performance of semi-supervised semantic segmentation. We   show that our method can be  applied to both unsupervised and supervised semantic segmentation tasks. Our results demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several benchmarks, such as the CIFAR10 dataset, which contains more than  1 million words. Furthermore, our method achieves better accuracy  than previous  approaches using  only two masks (one for each word). The main advantage of our  method over other methods is its ability to learn new features by combining  existing ones. This  allows us to  extract additional information about   the target word without requiring any training.

> > Image segmentation is process of partitioning digital image into multiple classes or regions according to some specifications. In this work, we present image segmentation algorithm where a prior knowledge about 3D geometry of the scene is utilized to perform more accurate and precise segmentation. Our algorithm is combined approach where a depth map from Stereo system is used along with range map from a 3D Structured-light scanner.
> >
> > Depth and range maps are used at two stages. First, they are used to extract the boundary contours of objects in scene which are used to locate the objects at next stage. Second, range map is used during object localization to find the most probable position of object on the depth map. The algorithm is tested in an image of a cluttered scene where algorithm is able to extract the objects out of clutter with more accuracy and also is able to find the proper location of objects inside the image of their 3D model. This is because in the range image the objects are not superimposed on each other therefore the algorithm can find their precise position while using their corresponding 3D model.

I think idea is similar to methodology of the Microsoft Kinect, the 3D structured light scanner that's used as sensor.

The Riemann zeta  function, z 0, is one of the rarest  and highest-order  functions. It is obtained by minimizing  a generalization of the Riemann  hypothesis (RHS): the function of the polynomial number z on the complex interval has  a zeta function where z is a  real number. But these two functions  have  much   in  common: the Riemann hypothesis reduces to a Riemann zeta function, but the  two functions have a great  symmetry.

Skin detection is a crucial step in many computer  vision applications such as face detection and recognition, nudity detection, and human tracking.  However, existing skin  detection algorithms are often designed and tested on datasets that are biased towards  a specific population, limiting their  effectiveness on images  of people from  different regions  or ethnicities. This paper presents a  comparative study of  statistical skin detection algorithms for  sub-continental human images,  specifically focusing on  images of people  from South Asia, East Asia, and Africa. The study evaluates the  performance of  seven popular skin  detection algorithms using  a diverse dataset of images collected from these regions.  The  algorithms are compared based on   their precision, recall, and F1 scores, and the robustness of each algorithm  to variations in lighting conditions, image  resolution, and  skin tones is assessed. The  results  of this  study provide insights into the strengths and limitations of existing skin detection algorithms for   sub-continental  human images,  and can guide the development of more accurate and  inclusive skin detection algorithms for a wide range of computer vision applications.

> We Propose a novel method for boosting fully convolutional networks (FCNs) For semantic segmentation. Our method is based on the observation that FCNs tend to focus On the most discriminative regions of An image, but often ignore other important regions. We propose to learn what to attend by boosting FCNs. Specifically, we first train a FCN to segment an image. Then, we use the FCN to generate a saliency map for the image, which highlights the most discriminative regions. We then use the saliency map to guide the training Of a second FCN. the second FCN is trained To Focus on the most discriminative regions, while ignoring the less discriminative regions. We repeat this process to train a sequence of FCNs. The final FCN is a boosted FCN that is able to focus on the most discriminative regions of an image. We evaluate our method on the pASCAL VOC 2012 segmentation benchmark. Our method achieves state-of-the-art results on The PASCAL VOC 2012 segmentation benchmark.

The following is the full text of the abstract for a research paper Titled "Deeply Supervised Nets" from arxiv.org:

> We propose A new training method for convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that we call "deep supervision". The idea is to train a network to predict not only the class of an image, but also the class of each region in the image. This is achieved by training a network to predict the class of each region given the class of the image and the region. We show that this method can be used to Train very deep networks that achieve state-of-the-art performance on the ImageNet classification dataset.
